Marge keeping Harry 'under her eye at all times' to boom out suggestions for his improvement
Marge voicing the opinion that Harry is 'mentally subnormal'
Harry's coping mechanism of mentally reciting his Handbook of Do-It-Yourself Broomcare, showing the psychological toll of sustained exposure
The gradual escalation across three days before the final evening (wineglass on day three, inflation on day seven)
Marge comparing Harry unfavorably to Dudley repeatedly over multiple meals
Aunt Petunia wincing at Ripper's drool - the subtle tension in the Marge-Petunia relationship
The detail that Marge delights in buying Dudley expensive presents while glaring at Harry as though daring him to ask why he got nothing
comparative.key_observations: Marge Dursley is one of the more faithful minor adaptations in the series. Her core personality - self-righteous, casually cruel, warmly affectionate toward the Dursleys, convinced of her blood-purity worldview - is well-preserved, with key dialogue reproduced almost verbatim. The main adaptation loss is the compression of her visit from a week to a single evening, which removes the sustained, methodical quality of her cruelty and the psychological toll it takes on Harry. The film Marge is recognizable and functional but slightly more of a single-scene grotesque than the deliberate, week-long tormentor of the books. No agency is transferred to another character, no key relationship is fundamentally altered, and her narrative function as the catalyst for Harry's departure is fully intact. The score of 80 reflects a faithful but compressed portrayal where the compression, while not changing who Marge is, does remove the texture that made her more than a one-note villain.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Pomfrey is established across the books as brisk, strict, and professionally protective. Her dominant traits are: fierce competence and pride in her craft ('I can mend bones in a second'), maternal protectiveness of patients, a tendency to bristle when her authority over the hospital wing is overridden (especially by Dumbledore), and a dry, no-nonsense manner. She clucks disapprovingly at institutional decisions that endanger students (dementors, dragons, the Triwizard Tournament). She is not cold - she shows warmth and even emotion (a tear for the Petrified students, bursting into tears at Dumbledore's death, fierce approval when McGonagall is attacked). She uses short, declarative sentences and commands. She is not easily moved but is not unfeeling.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows Pomfrey in the Chamber of Secrets film. Her lines are short and direct: 'He should have been brought straight to me! I can mend bones in a heartbeat - but growing them back -', 'I'll be able to, certainly, but it will be painful. You're in for a rough night, Potter. Regrowing bones is nasty business.', 'Out! All of you! This boy's got thirty-three bones to regrow!', 'There's no point in talking to a petrified person. She won't hear a word you're saying.' and then relenting with 'Very well then. But be quick about it.' She also gasps at Colin's Petrification. The brisk competence, the protective authority, the dry pragmatism, and the underlying sympathy (the 'glint of sympathy' when Ron appeals for Hermione) are all present.
personality_voice.difference: The film captures the core personality well. The added line 'Well, what did you expect - pumpkin juice?' is a small invention but consistent with her dry wit. The film omits the richer emotional texture seen in books - her fierce indignation at the dementor attack, her tears at Dumbledore's death, her outrage at McGonagall being Stunned, her hysterical protests in the hospital wing during PoA. These moments show a more emotionally complex Pomfrey. The film Pomfrey is slightly flatter - competent and strict but without the moments of genuine outrage or grief.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core voice and personality are preserved with high fidelity in the available film material. The compression is real but the traits shown are consistent. The missing emotional depth (grief, political outrage, fierce loyalty) is a minor loss that warrants a small deduction but not a major one, as the film corpus is limited to CoS and the traits shown are faithful.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Pomfrey's narrative function is consistent across all books: she is the gatekeeper and authority of the hospital wing, the healer who restores characters to action, a source of medical exposition, a minor but recurring check on other characters' authority (she pushes back against Dumbledore, Fudge, Snape), and occasionally a witness to key plot events (Colin's Petrification, the PoA hospital wing confrontation, Dumbledore's death aftermath). She is active - she bustles, she insists, she provides information, she enforces rules. In PoA she is particularly agentic: she provides the alibi for Harry and Hermione, she confronts Snape, she is ordered out by Dumbledore and slams the door. In HBP she conjures a chair for McGonagall. She is never a plot driver but she is a consistent functional presence.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film corpus (CoS), Pomfrey performs her core functions: she treats Harry's boneless arm, she enforces visiting rules, she relents with sympathy for Ron's appeal, she is present at Colin's Petrification and reacts with a gasp. She is mentioned by Dumbledore in the feast scene ('Madame Pomfrey, whose Mandrake Juice has been successfully administered'). Her role as healer and hospital wing authority is intact.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film omits the richer agentic moments from later books - the PoA confrontation where she provides Harry and Hermione's alibi and stands up to Snape and Fudge, her emotional confrontation with Dumbledore ('Is this a hospital wing or not?'), her role in the HBP aftermath (conjuring a chair, treating Bill, being sent to tend to Winky). These are significant moments of agency. In the film corpus available, her role is present but reduced to its most basic functions.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The film corpus is limited to CoS, so many of the agentic book moments simply don't appear in the evaluated film material. Within CoS, her role is faithfully rendered. The absence of the PoA and later agentic moments is a real loss but partly a function of corpus scope. A moderate deduction is warranted because the film consistently reduces her to a more passive, functional presence even in scenes where the books give her more pushback and agency.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for CoS; reasonably inferred for later films based on corpus absence

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Pomfrey's motivations are simple and consistent: professional duty to heal and protect her patients, institutional loyalty to Hogwarts, and a genuine care for student welfare that sometimes conflicts with the authority of those above her (Dumbledore, Fudge). She has no internal conflict in a dramatic sense - she is not torn or ambiguous. Her tension is always external: her professional authority vs. the headmaster's or Ministry's override. She is motivated by competence and care, not ambition or fear.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film shows the same motivations: she is protective of Harry (insisting he be brought to her, enforcing rest), she is professionally proud ('I can mend bones in a heartbeat'), and she relents when sympathy is warranted. The motivation to protect patients and enforce her professional domain is intact.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not show the tension between her professional authority and institutional override (the PoA scenes where Dumbledore dismisses her, Fudge overrides her, Snape barges in). This tension is the closest thing Pomfrey has to internal conflict and it is absent from the film corpus. However, within CoS, her motivations are correctly rendered.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but correct. The external tension that gives her motivations dramatic texture is largely absent from the film corpus, but this is compression rather than distortion. Minor deduction.

character_arc.book_baseline: Pomfrey has no character arc in the traditional sense. She is a flat character by design - she is the same competent, strict, caring nurse from book 1 to book 7. Her 'arc' is intentionally flat. The only evolution is that later books give her more emotional moments (grief at Dumbledore's death, outrage at McGonagall's attack) that deepen her without changing her.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film presents her as the same character throughout. No arc is invented or removed. She is flat in the films as she is flat in the books.
character_arc.difference: No meaningful difference. The flat arc is preserved.
character_arc.penalty_logic: No penalty. A flat arc in the books that is flat in the films scores full marks per the scoring rules.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Pomfrey's key relationships are: (1) with Dumbledore - a dynamic of professional deference mixed with genuine pushback when he overrides her authority in her domain; (2) with Harry - a recurring patient/nurse relationship with warmth beneath the strictness; (3) with McGonagall - collegial, mutual respect, shared concern for students. The Dumbledore relationship is the most dramatically rich: she defers to him but protests, she is sent away and slams the door, she is asked to do tasks outside her role (tend to Winky). The Harry relationship is warm but boundaried.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film shows the Harry relationship (treating him, enforcing rest, relenting) and a brief moment with Dumbledore (she is present at Colin's Petrification scene). The Dumbledore dynamic of deference-with-pushback is not shown in the film corpus. The McGonagall relationship is absent from the film corpus.
key_relationships.difference: The most dramatically interesting relationship - with Dumbledore - is not rendered in the film corpus. The Harry relationship is present but simplified. The collegial McGonagall relationship is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Dumbledore relationship is the richest and most character-defining of Pomfrey's relationships and it is largely absent from the film corpus. This is a real loss. The Harry relationship is present but without the full texture of the books (the PoA chocolate scenes, the insistence on rest, the warmth). Moderate deduction.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for Harry relationship; not enough evidence for Dumbledore and McGonagall relationships in film corpus

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Pomfrey's complexity comes from: (1) the tension between her strict professional authority and her genuine warmth; (2) her willingness to push back against more powerful figures (Dumbledore, Fudge, Snape) in defense of her patients; (3) her emotional responses to institutional failures (dementors at school, dragons in the Tournament, McGonagall being Stunned); (4) her dry humor ('Of course not - that was the headmaster, quite different'); (5) her role as an institutional conscience - she voices what the reader feels about dangerous decisions. The PoA hospital wing sequence is the richest material: she stuffs chocolate in Harry's mouth, she is hysterical when Dumbledore keeps arriving, she slams the door, she provides the alibi. This is Pomfrey at her most complex and agentic.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film captures the dry humor ('Well, what did you expect - pumpkin juice?'), the strict competence, and the underlying sympathy. The 'glint of sympathy' when Ron appeals for Hermione is a faithful rendering of her warmth beneath the rules. However, the pushback against authority, the emotional outbursts, the institutional conscience role, and the PoA complexity are all absent from the film corpus.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film Pomfrey is recognizable but simplified. The most nuanced material - her confrontations with Dumbledore and Fudge and Snape in PoA, her grief at Dumbledore's death, her outrage at McGonagall's attack - is absent. What remains is the functional surface of the character without the emotional and political depth.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is a real simplification. The film preserves the recognizable traits but removes the material that makes Pomfrey more than a functional healer. The PoA sequence alone contains more character complexity than all the film corpus combined. Noticeable simplification warrants a score in the 8-10 range.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on book corpus vs. film corpus comparison

lost_or_transferred_material: PoA hospital wing confrontation: Pomfrey stuffing chocolate in Harry's mouth, hysterically protesting Dumbledore's repeated entries, slamming her office door
Pomfrey providing the alibi for Harry and Hermione ('Of course not! I would have heard them!') - a key agentic moment
Pomfrey confronting Snape ('Professor Snape! Control yourself!')
Pomfrey's emotional response to dementors at school ('Setting dementors around a school... terrible things')
Pomfrey bursting into tears at news of Dumbledore's death and then pulling herself together to conjure a chair for McGonagall
Pomfrey's fierce approval of McGonagall and outrage at her being Stunned ('Cowardice, that's what it was... if I wasn't worried what would happen to you students without me, I'd resign in protest')
Pomfrey's role overseeing evacuation in DH
Pomfrey treating the injured after the Battle of Hogwarts
Pomfrey's dry humor about Dumbledore's authority ('Well, of course, that was the headmaster, quite different')
Pomfrey's warmth toward Hermione during the Polyjuice cat-hair recovery (books show her protecting Hermione's dignity with curtains)
score_caps_applied: Cap 2 (above 80 if key relationships change substantially) considered: the Dumbledore relationship - the most dramatically rich - is largely absent from the film corpus. Score held at 80.
comparative.key_observations: The film portrayal of Pomfrey in the available corpus (primarily CoS) is faithful at the surface level: her brisk competence, dry humor, strict protectiveness, and underlying warmth are all present and recognizable. The 'pumpkin juice' line is a small invention that is entirely consistent with her book voice. However, the films lose the most complex and agentic material - particularly the PoA hospital wing sequence, which is the richest single block of Pomfrey characterization in the entire series. In that sequence she pushes back against Dumbledore, Fudge, and Snape; provides a crucial alibi; and is shown as both fiercely protective and genuinely funny. The films also omit her emotional responses to institutional failures and her role as an institutional conscience. What remains is a competent, recognizable version of the character that preserves her core traits but loses the depth that makes her more than a functional healer. The score of 80 reflects faithful surface rendering with meaningful loss of complexity, agency, and relational texture.

personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Hooch is brisk, authoritative, and professionally sharp. Her voice is commanding and direct ('Well, what are you all waiting for? she barked'), but she also shows warmth and care in moments of student injury ('Come on, dear' as she leads Neville away with her arm around him). She is a genuine Quidditch enthusiast who speaks with technical passion about brooms (her extended monologue about the Firebolt's balance, the Nimbus series' tail-list, Silver Arrows). She enforces rules firmly but fairly, issuing penalties to both sides without favoritism. She can be emotionally expressive when provoked - 'YOU DO NOT ATTACK THE KEEPER UNLESS THE QUAFFLE IS WITHIN THE SCORING AREA!' and 'YOU CHEATING SCUM' level outrage at dirty play. She also uses physical intervention (Impediment Jinx on Harry and George) and is not merely a passive whistle-blower.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves her brisk, commanding classroom manner ('Well, what are you waiting for?', 'With feeling!'), her care for Neville ('Oh, oh, oh, oh, oh dear. It's a broken wrist... Poor boy. Come on now, up you get'), and her firm rule-enforcement ('If I see a single broom in the air, the one riding it will find themselves out of Hogwarts before they can say Quidditch'). Her hawk-yellow eyes and short gray hair are preserved. In the Quidditch match she says 'Now, I want a nice, clean game from all of you' and blows her whistle to declare Gryffindor's win. The core personality - authoritative, caring, fair - is present.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses the extended broom-enthusiast dimension entirely (no Firebolt monologue, no technical commentary on broom models). The emotional volatility during dirty Quidditch play - her shrieking outrage, her use of the Impediment Jinx on students post-match - is absent from the film corpus. Her voice in the film is competent and warm but somewhat flatter; the sharp professional passion and the capacity for genuine anger are not shown.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core traits are preserved and recognizable. The loss of her broom-enthusiast voice and her more volatile, interventionist side during dirty play represents a secondary trait reduction rather than a fundamental personality change. Minor penalty for simplification.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Hooch serves as flying instructor, Quidditch referee across multiple years, and occasional practice overseer. She actively intervenes in matches - issuing penalties, shouting down rule-breakers, physically using her wand to stop a fight (Impediment Jinx on Harry and George). She is the authority figure who controls the Quidditch space. She also serves a minor plot-enabling function: her departure to take Neville to the hospital wing is what creates the opportunity for Harry's first unsupervised flight and Malfoy's provocation.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves her role as flying instructor and referee. Her departure with Neville creates the same plot opportunity. She blows the whistle to end the first Quidditch match and declares 'Gryffindor wins!' Her presence as referee is acknowledged by Lee Jordan's commentary. Her agency as an active enforcer during dirty play is not shown in the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Her active refereeing agency across the dirtier matches (Books 2, 3, 5, 6) - issuing penalties, shouting at players, physically intervening - is absent from the film corpus, which only covers the first match. Her role as practice overseer (Books 2, 3) is also absent. Within the scenes that do appear, her core functions are intact.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The film corpus is limited to PS/SS material. Within that scope her role and agency are preserved. The absence of her more active refereeing agency from later films is a reduction but the corpus does not provide film evidence of those later matches being handled differently. Moderate penalty for the narrowed scope of her agency as shown.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for PS/SS; not enough evidence for later films

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Hooch's motivations are simple and consistent: professional duty (teach flying safely, referee fairly), genuine love of Quidditch and brooms, and care for student welfare. There is no significant internal conflict - she is a flat character motivationally. Her tension, when it exists, is external: enforcing rules against rule-breakers.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The same motivations are present: she enforces rules, cares for injured students, and runs the match fairly. No new motivations are introduced and none are removed.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No meaningful difference. Her motivations are simple in the books and remain simple in the film.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: No penalty. A flat motivational profile that is faithfully flat in the film should not be penalized.

character_arc.book_baseline: Hooch has no arc. She is a flat, functional character across all seven books. Her role, personality, and function do not change. This is intentional - she is an institutional fixture.
character_arc.film_portrayal: No arc is introduced or implied. She remains a flat institutional figure.
character_arc.difference: None. Flat in books, flat in film.
character_arc.penalty_logic: No penalty. Per the scoring rules, a character whose arc is intentionally flat in the books scores full points if the film also keeps them flat.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Hooch's most notable relational dynamic is with students during flying/Quidditch contexts: a firm but caring authority figure. She has a brief warm moment with Neville (arm around him, 'Come on, dear'). She has a professional relationship with Wood (deferring to his team huddle, overseeing his practices). Her relationship with Malfoy is one of firm correction ('she told Malfoy he'd been doing it wrong for years'). No deep personal relationships are established.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Neville dynamic (care after injury) is preserved. The correction of Malfoy during the flying lesson is not explicitly shown in the film corpus, though her general authority over students is. The Wood/practice relationship is absent from the film corpus.
key_relationships.difference: The Malfoy correction moment - a small but characterizing beat showing she applies rules without favoritism - is not evidenced in the film corpus. The multi-year practice overseer relationship with Wood is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: These are minor relational beats for a functional character. The core dynamic (authority figure, caring toward injured students) is preserved. Small penalty for the absent Malfoy correction and Wood relationship.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Hooch's complexity is modest but real: she combines professional authority with genuine warmth, technical broom expertise with emotional volatility during dirty play, and fair enforcement with physical intervention when needed. The Firebolt monologue reveals a character with genuine passion beyond her institutional role. Her use of the Impediment Jinx on Harry and George shows she is not merely a passive referee but an active enforcer willing to act against protagonists.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film captures the authority-warmth combination. It does not show the broom-enthusiast dimension, the emotional volatility during dirty play, or the physical intervention against students. The character is recognizable but somewhat reduced to her most surface-level traits.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Lost: the Firebolt monologue (technical passion, personality beyond duty), the shrieking outrage at dirty play across multiple matches, the Impediment Jinx intervention (agency against protagonists), the multi-year practice overseer role. These are not transferred to another character - they are simply absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: For a minor functional character, this is a noticeable simplification. The film retains the core but removes the texture that made her more than a whistle-blower. The lost material does change the audience's understanding of her as a character with genuine passion and willingness to act forcefully.

lost_or_transferred_material: Hooch's extended Firebolt monologue demonstrating technical broom expertise and genuine Quidditch passion (Book 3)
Hooch's shrieking outrage and penalty enforcement during the dirty Slytherin matches (Books 2, 3, 5, 6)
Hooch's use of the Impediment Jinx on Harry and George Weasley after the post-match fight with Malfoy (Book 5)
Hooch's multi-year role as practice overseer for Gryffindor (Books 2, 3)
Hooch correcting Malfoy's broom grip during the flying lesson ('she told Malfoy he'd been doing it wrong for years')
Hooch falling asleep in the stands and then telling Harry and Ron off for not waking her (Book 3)
comparative.key_observations: Hooch is a minor functional character whose core identity - brisk flying instructor, fair Quidditch referee, caring toward injured students - is faithfully preserved in the film. The film captures her commanding voice, her warmth with Neville, and her role as the authority figure who enables Harry's first unsupervised flight. What is lost is the texture that made her more than a whistle-blower: her genuine technical passion for brooms, her emotional volatility during dirty play across multiple matches, and her willingness to physically intervene against protagonists. These losses simplify rather than transform her, keeping the score in the high-faithful range but preventing a near-perfect score. Confidence is medium throughout because the film corpus is largely limited to PS/SS material, making it difficult to evaluate her later-film portrayal comprehensively.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Fluffy is a giant three-headed dog — dangerous, loud, and instinctively aggressive when awake, but with a single exploitable weakness: music puts him to sleep. His 'personality' is entirely behavioral: he is a threat that must be managed, not reasoned with. He is not malicious in a directed sense; he simply guards and attacks. His one distinguishing trait beyond raw menace is this musical vulnerability, which Hagrid treats with affectionate familiarity ('Fluffy's a piece o' cake if yeh know how to calm him down').
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves both core behavioral traits: Fluffy is asleep when the trio arrives (the harp is playing), wakes when the harp stops, and immediately becomes a violent threat. He growls, barks, thrashes, and dives at the children. The drool detail adds a physical texture consistent with the books.
personality_voice.difference: The film compresses the encounter — there is no moment of the trio actively playing music to subdue him, as Harry uses Hagrid's flute in the book. In the film, Snape has already put a spell on the harp, so Fluffy is pre-subdued. The trio only has to move his paw and jump. This removes the brief active engagement with Fluffy's weakness. The core traits (dangerous when awake, subdued by music) are still communicated, just not demonstrated by the protagonists.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The behavioral identity is preserved. The compression slightly reduces the texture of the music-weakness interaction but does not misrepresent Fluffy's nature. Minor penalty for the loss of the protagonists actively exploiting the weakness themselves.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Fluffy serves two structural functions: (1) a physical guardian whose presence signals the Stone's location and importance, and (2) a plot mechanism whose weakness (music) becomes the key vulnerability that Voldemort/Quirrell exploits. His existence drives multiple scenes — the trio's discovery of the trapdoor, Hagrid's accidental revelation of the music secret, and the final descent. He is also a recurring tension marker: the trio checks the door repeatedly to confirm the Stone is still safe.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Both functions are preserved. Fluffy guards the trapdoor, his presence confirms the Stone's location, and his music-weakness is the plot point Hagrid accidentally reveals to the disguised Voldemort. The film includes the scene where Harry asks Hagrid about the stranger and Hagrid reveals the music secret. Fluffy is encountered in the descent sequence.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The recurring tension-check function (trio pressing ears to the door across weeks) is absent from the film. This removes Fluffy as an ongoing narrative presence and reduces him to two discrete appearances. The active use of the music weakness by the protagonists is transferred to Snape/Quirrell (the harp is already playing when they arrive), so the trio never directly exercises agency over Fluffy.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer of the music-exploitation action from the protagonists to an off-screen Snape/Quirrell is a minor agency transfer — the protagonists no longer solve the Fluffy problem themselves. The recurring tension-check scenes are absent. These are real reductions but do not eliminate Fluffy's core narrative function.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Fluffy has no internal conflict or complex motivation — he is an animal guard. His 'motivation' is purely instinctual: protect the trapdoor, attack intruders, sleep when music plays. There is nothing to analyze beyond this behavioral programming.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: Identical — Fluffy behaves as a guard animal with no depicted interiority.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: None.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: No penalty. A flat, instinct-driven creature cannot be penalized for lacking internal conflict that was never present in the books.

character_arc.book_baseline: Fluffy has no arc in the traditional sense. He begins as a guard, remains a guard, and is last referenced as still alive and well during exams. There is a minor narrative resolution: after the Stone is destroyed, Fluffy's purpose is fulfilled. Hagrid's comment in CoS ('the giant three-headed dog he'd christened Fluffy') confirms he remains part of Hagrid's world. The arc is intentionally flat — he is a static creature.
character_arc.film_portrayal: Also flat. Fluffy appears, guards, is bypassed, and is not referenced again. The film does not contradict the book's flat arc.
character_arc.difference: The book includes a brief ongoing presence (the trio checking the door during exam weeks) that gives Fluffy a sustained narrative presence across the story. The film reduces him to two scenes. However, the arc — which is flat — is not altered.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Minor penalty for the loss of the sustained narrative presence, which slightly compresses even the flat arc. But since the arc is intentionally flat and the film does not invent or contradict it, the score remains high.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Fluffy's primary relationship is with Hagrid, who bought him, named him, lent him to Dumbledore, and speaks of him with genuine affection ('Fluffy's a piece o' cake'). Hagrid's relationship with Fluffy is an expression of his broader trait: loving dangerous creatures others fear. Fluffy is also relationally significant to the trio — he is a recurring threat they must navigate and monitor.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Hagrid-Fluffy relationship is preserved in the key scene: Hagrid speaks of Fluffy with familiarity and pride, reveals the music secret with the same casual affection, and is horrified when he realizes what he has done. The trio's relationship with Fluffy as a threat to navigate is also preserved in the descent scene.
key_relationships.difference: The ongoing monitoring relationship (trio checking the door repeatedly) is absent. The depth of Hagrid's affection for Fluffy — shown across multiple book scenes where Hagrid defends and discusses him — is compressed to a single scene. The CoS callback ('the giant three-headed dog he'd christened Fluffy') which reinforces Hagrid's lasting bond is not present in the film corpus.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamic of Hagrid's affectionate ownership is preserved in the one scene that matters most. The compression removes texture but not the essential dynamic. Moderate penalty for the loss of the ongoing trio-Fluffy tension relationship.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Fluffy's complexity is limited by his nature as an animal, but the books give him more texture than a simple obstacle: (1) Hagrid's naming and affection humanizes him; (2) the music weakness is a specific, memorable trait that makes him more than a generic monster; (3) his ongoing presence as a tension marker across weeks of the story gives him narrative weight; (4) Hagrid's guilt over revealing the secret ('I'll never drink again!') ties Fluffy to a meaningful emotional beat about Hagrid's character.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The music weakness is communicated (via Hagrid's dialogue and the pre-playing harp). Hagrid's affection is present. The guilt scene ('It's all my ruddy fault!') is present in the film corpus. The physical encounter preserves the menace.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The loss of the recurring tension-check scenes removes Fluffy as an ongoing narrative presence. The protagonists never actively use the music weakness themselves — it is pre-solved by Snape/Quirrell. The specific detail of Harry using Hagrid's Christmas flute (a gift that becomes plot-relevant) is absent, removing a small but meaningful piece of connective tissue. The CoS retrospective reference that reinforces Fluffy's place in Hagrid's world is absent from the film.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film preserves the most important nuances (music weakness, Hagrid's affection, the guilt beat) but loses the sustained narrative texture and the protagonists' active engagement with Fluffy's weakness. The flute detail — a small but elegant piece of book craft — is lost. These are real but not severe losses for a creature character with inherently limited complexity.

lost_or_transferred_material: The trio actively playing music (Harry using Hagrid's Christmas flute) to subdue Fluffy — transferred to Snape/Quirrell via the pre-playing enchanted harp
Recurring scenes of the trio pressing their ears to the third-floor door to confirm Fluffy is still guarding the Stone (ongoing tension marker across weeks)
The specific detail of Harry pocketing Hagrid's Christmas flute as the tool to get past Fluffy
Chamber of Secrets retrospective reference to Fluffy reinforcing his place in Hagrid's world and Hagrid's lasting affection for dangerous creatures
comparative.key_observations: Fluffy is a creature character with inherently limited interiority, so the faithfulness evaluation is necessarily focused on behavioral traits, narrative function, and relational texture rather than psychology. The film preserves his two core traits (dangerous when awake, subdued by music), his primary relationship with Hagrid, and his structural role as guardian of the trapdoor. The most meaningful adaptation change is the transfer of the music-exploitation action from the protagonists to an off-screen Snape/Quirrell — the trio never actively solves the Fluffy problem in the film. The loss of the recurring tension-check scenes also reduces Fluffy from a sustained narrative presence to a two-scene obstacle. These are real but moderate losses for a character whose book role is already functional rather than psychological. The score of 79 reflects a faithful core portrayal with meaningful compression and one notable agency transfer.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Myrtle is defined by a cluster of tightly interlocking traits: chronic self-pity expressed in loud, theatrical sobbing; hypersensitivity to any perceived slight (real or imagined); a morbid fixation on her own death and the indignities she suffered in life; sudden mood reversals from misery to gleeful schadenfreude when others suffer (e.g., laughing at Hermione's cat-transformation, crowing about Hermione's tail); a sly, voyeuristic streak (spying on Cedric in the bath, admitting she watches prefects); and a plaintive, possessive attachment to Harry. Her speech is characterised by dramatic outbursts, rhetorical self-flagellation ('Fat Myrtle! Ugly Myrtle!'), sudden howls of rage, and a tendency to dive into toilets when upset. She oscillates rapidly between victimhood and a kind of petty cruelty.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core traits well. In CoS she introduces herself with the exact self-pitying litany ('fat, ugly, miserable, moping, moaning Myrtle'), dives into the toilet, reacts with theatrical outrage to Ron's 'fifty points through her nose' joke, and delivers the 'Let's all throw books at Myrtle' speech almost verbatim. In GoF she is flirtatious and sly with Harry in the bath, admits to spying on Cedric, giggles at Harry's discomfort, and delivers the 'Slow, aren't you?' taunt. The HBP bathroom scene (Myrtle comforting Draco, screaming 'MURDER!') is present in the film and preserves her dramatic, attention-seeking register.
personality_voice.difference: The films omit the Yule Ball scene where Myrtle's extreme sensitivity to being called 'fat' and 'pimply' by Peeves is on full display, and the GoF bathroom scene where she delivers the extended monologue about nobody missing her when she died, her haunting of Olive Hornby, and her being forced back to the toilet by the Ministry. These scenes add texture to her self-pity as something with genuine historical roots rather than pure performance. The HBP scene where she confides Draco's emotional state to Harry ('he's sensitive, people bully him too') is present in the book but absent from the film corpus provided; however the bathroom duel scene with her screaming is retained. The film Myrtle is slightly more comic and flirtatious and slightly less genuinely pitiable, but the core voice pattern is intact.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main traits — self-pity, dramatic outbursts, schadenfreude, voyeurism, possessive attachment to Harry — are all directly evidenced in the film corpus. The loss of the Yule Ball scene and the extended GoF monologue about her death removes some of the pathos that grounds her self-pity in real grievance, making her slightly more one-note comic. Minor penalty warranted but the character remains highly recognisable in voice and personality.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Myrtle serves several distinct narrative functions across the books: (1) she provides the bathroom as a safe brewing location for Polyjuice Potion; (2) she is the key witness who found Tom Riddle's diary and whose account of her own death identifies the Chamber entrance; (3) she actively helps Harry solve the second task by advising him to put the egg in the water and by providing information about the merpeople; (4) she is the victim whose death 50 years ago is the central mystery of CoS; (5) in HBP she is the confidante who reveals Draco's emotional breakdown to Harry, directly triggering the bathroom duel. In each case she initiates or enables a plot-critical action.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Functions (1), (2), (4), and (5) are preserved in the films. The bathroom is used for Polyjuice brewing; she identifies the diary and her death story leads Harry to the Chamber entrance; she helps Harry with the egg in GoF; the HBP bathroom scene retains her presence during the duel and her screaming alerts Snape. Function (3) is partially preserved: in the film she tells Harry to put the egg in the water and confirms the merpeople connection, though the extended back-and-forth where she actively guides Harry's reasoning ('Slow, aren't you?', 'it took Diggory much longer') is compressed.
narrative_role_agency.difference: In the books, Myrtle's role in the GoF second-task preparation is more active: she engages in extended dialogue, teases Harry, provides information about the lake's inhabitants, and reacts emotionally to the word 'breathe.' The film compresses this to a shorter exchange. Her HBP role as Draco's confidante — where she explicitly tells Harry that Draco is 'sensitive, people bully him too' and that she 'promised not to tell' — is present in the book but the film corpus provided does not include this dialogue, though the bathroom scene itself is present. The core agency (providing the diary clue, helping with the egg, being present at the duel) is retained.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: No major agency transfer occurs. The compression of the GoF scene reduces her active role slightly but does not reassign it to another character. The HBP confidante function is partially present. Minor penalty for compression of her active guidance role in GoF.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Myrtle's motivations are consistent and layered: she craves attention and sympathy (being dead means being ignored); she is driven by a need to be acknowledged as having suffered genuinely ('My life was nothing but misery'); she takes pleasure in others' misfortune as a form of compensation for her own; she is possessively attached to Harry because he visited her and showed her attention; she harbours a genuine grievance against the living world (Olive Hornby, the Ministry) that explains why she stays in the toilet rather than moving on. Her internal conflict is between wanting connection and pushing people away through her behaviour.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the attention-seeking and self-pity motivations clearly. Her attachment to Harry ('if you die down there, you're welcome to share my toilet') and her pleasure in others' suffering (laughing at Hermione) are present. The deeper motivation — that her self-pity is rooted in a specific history of bullying and institutional abandonment — is present in the CoS film through her self-introduction but the extended GoF monologue that makes this explicit ('Nobody missed me even when I was alive... I was sitting there waiting for them') is absent from the film.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film preserves the surface motivations but loses the specific historical grounding of her grievance. The GoF monologue where she details being ignored after death, haunting Olive Hornby, and being forced back to the toilet by the Ministry is not in the film corpus. This removes the sense that her behaviour is a rational (if extreme) response to genuine injustice, making her motivations slightly shallower.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but not wrong. The core drives are present; the historical specificity that makes them sympathetic is reduced. Moderate penalty.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for surface motivations; reasonably inferred for the deeper layer being absent

character_arc.book_baseline: Myrtle has a flat arc across the series — she does not change. She begins as a self-pitying, attention-seeking ghost and ends the same way. What the books do show is a consistent pattern of small, temporary emotional shifts: she can be cheered by attention (Harry asking how she died), delighted by others' misfortune, briefly wounded, briefly flirtatious. The arc is intentionally static; her function is to be a recurring comic-pathetic presence whose unchanging nature is itself the point.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films preserve this flat arc. She is the same character in CoS, GoF, and HBP: same self-pity, same attachment to Harry, same schadenfreude. No false arc is invented.
character_arc.difference: No meaningful difference. The flat arc is faithfully reproduced.
character_arc.penalty_logic: No penalty. A flat arc in the books that is flat in the films scores full points under the arc rule.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Myrtle's most important relationship is with Harry: she is possessively attached to him, he is the only person who treats her with any consistent (if reluctant) consideration, and this dynamic produces both comedy and genuine pathos. Her relationship with Hermione is one of mutual wariness — Hermione finds her exhausting, Myrtle is suspicious of Hermione's false brightness. Her relationship with Draco in HBP is significant: she becomes his confidante, drawn to him because he is also suffering and isolated, which reveals her capacity for genuine empathy beneath the self-pity. Her relationship with Peeves (antagonist who exploits her sensitivity) is present in the books.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry relationship is well-preserved: the flirtatious attachment, the 'share my toilet' offer, the possessive reproach ('you haven't been to see me for ages') are all present. The Hermione wariness is present in CoS. The Draco confidante relationship is present in HBP (the bathroom scene exists) but the specific dialogue where Myrtle explains her empathy for Draco ('he's sensitive, people bully him too') is not in the film corpus provided. The Peeves relationship is entirely absent from the films (Peeves was cut from all films), removing the dynamic where Myrtle is publicly humiliated and her vulnerability is weaponised.
key_relationships.difference: The Peeves relationship is lost entirely, removing a key dynamic that shows Myrtle as genuinely victimised rather than merely performatively so. The Draco confidante relationship is partially present but the emotional reasoning Myrtle gives for it is absent. The Harry relationship is well-preserved.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The loss of the Peeves dynamic is meaningful — it contextualises her self-pity as a response to ongoing real cruelty, not just historical grievance. The partial loss of the Draco confidante texture is a secondary penalty. The Harry relationship, the most important one, is intact.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for Harry relationship; directly evidenced for Peeves absence; reasonably inferred for Draco confidante reduction

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Myrtle's complexity lies in the tension between her as comic figure and her as genuinely tragic one. The books repeatedly show that her self-pity is not entirely unjustified: she was bullied, died alone, was ignored for hours, was forced back to the toilet by the Ministry when she tried to haunt her tormentor. She has a sly, voyeuristic intelligence (spying on Cedric, knowing about Polyjuice Potion). She is capable of genuine empathy (her connection to Draco). She takes active pleasure in others' suffering in a way that is both funny and slightly disturbing. The Peeves scenes show her as a victim of ongoing cruelty, not just historical grievance. The GoF monologue is the single richest piece of characterisation, grounding all her behaviour in a specific, sympathetic history.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the comic-pathetic duality reasonably well. The voyeurism (spying on Cedric) is present. The schadenfreude is present. The possessive attachment is present. What is lost: the GoF monologue that makes her tragedy explicit and sympathetic; the Peeves dynamic that shows her as an ongoing victim; the full Draco confidante scene that shows her capacity for empathy. The film Myrtle is more consistently comic and less genuinely pitiable.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The GoF monologue ('Nobody missed me even when I was alive... I was sitting there waiting for them... I made sure she didn't forget it until her dying day') is the most important lost material. It transforms Myrtle from a comic nuisance into a character with a real, specific grievance. Its absence means the film audience never fully understands why she is the way she is. The Peeves scenes are entirely absent. The Draco empathy dialogue is absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: These absences collectively flatten Myrtle from a character with genuine tragic depth into primarily a comic-pathetic figure. The core traits remain but the material that makes her genuinely complex — the historical specificity of her suffering, her capacity for empathy, her status as an ongoing victim — is significantly reduced. Noticeable simplification warranted.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; directly evidenced for what is absent based on corpus comparison

lost_or_transferred_material: GoF bathroom monologue: Myrtle's extended account of being ignored after her death, waiting for someone to find her body, haunting Olive Hornby, and being forced back to the toilet by the Ministry — the richest piece of characterisation establishing the genuine historical basis for her self-pity
Yule Ball scene: Myrtle's extreme reaction to being called 'fat' and 'pimply' by Peeves, showing her hypersensitivity in a social context and the ongoing cruelty she faces
All Peeves interactions: the dynamic where Peeves actively exploits and humiliates Myrtle, contextualising her self-pity as a response to real ongoing victimisation

HBP confidante dialogue: Myrtle's explicit explanation of why she empathises with Draco ('he's sensitive, people bully him too, and he feels lonely') — the scene exists in the film but this specific reasoning is absent from the film corpus
GoF scene detail: Myrtle's emotional reaction to Harry accidentally saying 'breathe' in front of her, and her extended guidance of Harry's reasoning about the merpeople (compressed in the film)
score_caps_applied: Cap 1 considered (above 85 requires no major complexity/agency loss): total does not reach 85, cap not binding
Cap 2 considered (above 80 requires key relationships intact): Peeves relationship entirely absent and Draco confidante partially lost; total kept at or below 80
comparative.key_observations: The films preserve Myrtle's core personality — self-pity, schadenfreude, voyeurism, possessive attachment to Harry, dramatic outbursts — with high fidelity, and her narrative functions (Polyjuice bathroom, diary discovery, egg clue, HBP bathroom presence) are largely intact. The primary losses are the GoF monologue that grounds her self-pity in specific historical injustice, the complete absence of Peeves (who contextualises her as an ongoing victim rather than merely a historical one), and the reduction of her Draco confidante role. These losses shift her from a character with genuine tragic depth to a primarily comic-pathetic figure — recognisable and largely faithful, but missing the material that made her genuinely complex and sympathetic in the books.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Bellatrix is defined by fanatical devotion to Voldemort expressed as near-erotic worship, explosive volatility, sadistic pleasure in cruelty, contempt for anyone she deems inferior (Mudbloods, blood traitors, half-bloods, even fellow Death Eaters she distrusts), and a theatrical, mocking quality in her speech. She oscillates between shrieking fury and cold menace. Her voice is characterized by rhetorical questions, sudden screams, mock-baby tones used to taunt, and passionate declarations of loyalty. She is also capable of sustained, pointed argument (the Spinner's End interrogation of Snape) and strategic thinking (recognizing the danger of the Sword of Gryffindor, stopping Lucius from summoning Voldemort prematurely). Her cruelty is not mindless — she enjoys it and is proud of it.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core traits: fanatical devotion to Voldemort ('I'd like to kill the boy'), explosive violence (stunning Snatchers instantly over the sword), theatrical mockery ('Come on, granny, give us your best'; 'Someone has to do the washing'), and the shrieking volatility. The mock-baby voice and taunting register are present. The Spinner's End scene preserves her distrust of Snape and her role as Bonder. The Malfoy Manor sequence preserves her sadism and her strategic alarm over the sword.
personality_voice.difference: The films compress or omit several speech patterns that build texture in the books: the extended Spinner's End interrogation is heavily cut, removing her sustained rhetorical challenge to Snape and her passionate self-justification ('He'd have me! I, who spent many years in Azkaban for him!'). Her mock-baby voice taunting Harry in the Ministry ('Come out, come out, little Harry!') is absent from the film. The film Bellatrix is slightly more physically theatrical and slightly less verbally complex. The Deathly Hallows Part 2 final duel line ('Be thankful we only took one... It's not like you don't have a spare') is a film invention that fits her cruelty but replaces the book's 'What will happen to your children when I've killed you? When Mummy's gone the same way as Freddie?' — a meaningful shift in register from taunting to cold cruelty.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The dominant traits — fanaticism, sadism, volatility, contempt, theatrical cruelty — are preserved across multiple scenes. The compression of Spinner's End removes some of her verbal complexity and self-righteous passion, but the core personality pattern is intact and consistently evidenced. Minor penalty for loss of the mock-baby taunting register and the compressed self-justification speech.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Bellatrix serves multiple narrative functions: primary antagonist to Harry and the Order in OotP (she kills Sirius, she is the target of Harry's pursuit and first Cruciatus attempt), instrument of Voldemort's will, torturer of the Longbottoms (establishing her as the series' emblem of Death Eater cruelty), key agent in the Malfoy Manor sequence (she controls the interrogation, identifies the sword's significance, tortures Hermione), and she dies at Molly Weasley's hands as a narrative payoff. She also functions as Bonder in the Unbreakable Vow scene. Her agency is consistently high: she initiates, decides, commands, and acts independently within Death Eater operations.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve her role as Sirius's killer, her function as Bonder, her command of the Malfoy Manor sequence, and her death at Molly's hands. She retains agency in the Malfoy Manor interrogation — she stuns Snatchers, commands the room, identifies the sword, and tortures Hermione. In the Battle of Hogwarts she is shown fighting independently and is the last Death Eater standing alongside Voldemort.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Ministry of Magic sequence in OotP is significantly compressed in the film. Bellatrix's extended pursuit of Harry through the Ministry, her taunting of him, her torture of Neville, and the sustained duel are all reduced. The film cuts her mock-baby taunting, her lesson to Harry about Unforgivable Curses ('You need to mean them, Potter!'), and her triumphant scream after killing Sirius is present but the chase is truncated. Her role in the Spinner's End scene is compressed — she loses the extended interrogation of Snape where she functions as a genuine challenger and ideological voice. In DH Part 1, the book's scene where she commands Greyback and manages the Malfoy Manor situation with strategic alarm is preserved, though some of her lines are redistributed or compressed.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Her core agency is preserved in the most important scenes. The compression of the Ministry sequence reduces her active role in that sequence but does not eliminate it. The Spinner's End compression is more significant — she loses her function as sustained ideological challenger to Snape. No major action is transferred to another character. Minor penalty for compressed agency in OotP Ministry and Spinner's End.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Bellatrix's primary motivation is absolute devotion to Voldemort — not merely obedience but something closer to worship and love. This is evidenced by her pride in her Azkaban imprisonment ('I, who spent many years in Azkaban for him!'), her near-erotic proximity to him at the Malfoy Manor table, her tears of delight at his attention, her terror when she thinks she has failed him ('MASTER, I TRIED, I TRIED — DO NOT PUNISH ME'), and her frenzied grief at his apparent death in the forest. Secondary motivations include blood-purity fanaticism (her rage at Harry's half-blood status, her disowning of Andromeda) and sadistic pleasure in cruelty as an end in itself. There is a meaningful internal tension: she is Voldemort's most devoted servant but is also capable of fear of his displeasure, which creates a vulnerability beneath the fanaticism.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the devotion to Voldemort (volunteering to kill Harry, her proximity to him in the forest, 'My lord, let me help you'). Her blood-purity fanaticism is present ('You dare speak his name? You filthy half-blood!'). Her fear of Voldemort's displeasure is shown in DH Part 1 when she stops Lucius from summoning him prematurely. The sadistic pleasure is consistently shown.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films lose the full depth of her devotion as a quasi-romantic obsession. The book's Malfoy Manor table scene — where her face floods with color and her eyes well with tears of delight at Voldemort's attention, and where she is publicly humiliated by the Tonks marriage joke — is absent from the film corpus provided. Her terror and self-abasement after the Ministry failure ('MASTER, I TRIED') is present in the book but not in the film corpus. The Spinner's End scene where she passionately defends her Azkaban imprisonment as proof of superior loyalty is heavily compressed in the film, losing the emotional texture of her motivation.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivation — fanatical devotion — is present and recognizable in the films. The secondary motivations (blood purity, sadism) are intact. What is lost is the full emotional register of that devotion: the quasi-erotic worship, the public humiliation and shame, the terror of Voldemort's displeasure expressed in self-abasement. These are simplifications rather than replacements, but they flatten the motivational texture meaningfully.

character_arc.book_baseline: Bellatrix has a largely flat arc by design — she is introduced as a fanatical Death Eater and dies as one. Her arc is not one of change but of escalating consequence: she escapes Azkaban, kills Sirius, tortures Hermione, and is finally killed by Molly Weasley. The arc's dramatic function is the payoff of her accumulated cruelty. There is a minor arc of hubris: she is Voldemort's most trusted lieutenant but her failures (Ministry, Gringotts) erode that position, and she dies in the moment of apparent triumph. The arc is intentionally flat in terms of character development.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films preserve this flat arc faithfully: she escapes Azkaban, kills Sirius, participates in the Malfoy Manor torture, and is killed by Molly. The escalating consequence structure is intact. The hubris-and-fall element is present in compressed form.
character_arc.difference: The film omits the Malfoy Manor table scene (DH Part 1 book) where Voldemort publicly humiliates her over the Tonks marriage, which is a meaningful beat in her arc — a moment where her status as 'most loyal' is publicly mocked. This is a minor loss since the arc is intentionally flat, but it removes a moment of vulnerability and humiliation that complicates her triumphalism.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is flat by design and the films preserve that flatness faithfully. The omission of the humiliation scene is a minor loss. Full compression penalty is not warranted since the arc's key beats (escape, Sirius's death, Hermione's torture, Molly's killing blow) are all present.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships define Bellatrix: (1) Voldemort — quasi-devotional worship, the emotional center of her character; (2) Sirius — familial hatred and rivalry, she kills him with apparent triumph; (3) Narcissa — complex sisterly dynamic where Bellatrix is dominant, contemptuous of Narcissa's maternal weakness, yet ultimately serves as Bonder and is bound to her sister's fate. Secondary: her relationship with Neville (she destroyed his parents and he witnesses her cruelty directly) and with Harry (she is his primary antagonist in OotP, the target of his first Cruciatus attempt).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Voldemort relationship is present but the quasi-erotic devotion is reduced to proximity and verbal deference. The Sirius relationship is preserved in its outcome (she kills him) but the familial dimension is not developed in the film corpus. The Narcissa relationship is preserved in the Spinner's End scene — the dynamic of Bellatrix as dominant, skeptical, and ultimately compliant is intact. The Neville relationship is present in OotP ('Neville Longbottom, is it? How's Mom and Dad?') but the extended torture scene is compressed. The Harry antagonist relationship is preserved.
key_relationships.difference: The Voldemort relationship loses its most emotionally intense moments — the tears of delight, the public humiliation, the self-abasement. The Sirius-Bellatrix familial dimension (they are cousins, she is on the Black family tree, Kreacher's devotion to her photograph) is entirely absent from the films. The Neville relationship loses the extended torture scene that makes her cruelty toward him personally meaningful rather than incidental.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Narcissa and Harry relationships are adequately preserved. The Voldemort relationship is recognizable but emotionally flattened. The Sirius familial relationship is essentially absent from the films. The Neville relationship is compressed. These are meaningful losses that change the emotional texture of her key dynamics.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Bellatrix's complexity comes from several sources: (1) her sustained ideological challenge to Snape at Spinner's End, where she functions as a genuine intellectual adversary rather than just a screaming fanatic; (2) her strategic intelligence (recognizing the sword's significance, stopping Lucius from summoning Voldemort prematurely, managing the Malfoy Manor situation); (3) her vulnerability — the terror of Voldemort's displeasure, the public humiliation at the Malfoy Manor table, the moment of fear when Harry tells her the prophecy is smashed; (4) the quasi-erotic dimension of her devotion, which makes her fanaticism psychologically specific rather than generic; (5) her relationship with Kreacher and the Black family legacy; (6) the Neville dimension — she destroyed his parents and takes pleasure in reminding him of it.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve her strategic intelligence (Malfoy Manor sword scene), her sadism, her volatility, and her fanaticism. The Spinner's End scene preserves her distrust of Snape and her role as Bonder, though the extended interrogation is cut. The Neville confrontation in OotP is present but compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Lost: the full Spinner's End interrogation (her sustained ideological challenge, her passionate self-justification, her discomfiture when Snape outmaneuvers her); the Malfoy Manor table scene (public humiliation, tears of delight, the quasi-erotic devotion made visible); her terror and self-abasement after the Ministry failure; the Kreacher/Black family dimension; the extended Neville torture scene; her mock-baby taunting of Harry in the Ministry. These losses collectively flatten her from a psychologically specific fanatic with vulnerability and strategic intelligence into a more generically theatrical villain.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The losses are significant enough to warrant a meaningful penalty. The Spinner's End compression removes her most intellectually complex scene. The absence of the Malfoy Manor table scene removes the most emotionally specific expression of her devotion. The Kreacher/Black family material is entirely absent. However, the films do preserve enough of her strategic intelligence and sadistic pleasure to prevent a severe penalty. The character remains recognizable and substantially present, but the nuances that make her psychologically specific are noticeably reduced.

lost_or_transferred_material: Extended Spinner's End interrogation of Snape — Bellatrix's sustained ideological challenge, her passionate self-justification of Azkaban imprisonment as proof of superior loyalty, and her discomfiture when Snape outmaneuvers her
Malfoy Manor table scene (DH Part 1 book) — Bellatrix's tears of delight at Voldemort's attention, her public humiliation over the Tonks marriage, the quasi-erotic proximity to Voldemort
Ministry of Magic mock-baby taunting of Harry ('Come out, come out, little Harry!') and her lesson on Unforgivable Curses ('You need to mean them, Potter!')
Bellatrix's terror and self-abasement after the prophecy is smashed ('MASTER, I TRIED, I TRIED — DO NOT PUNISH ME')
Extended Neville torture scene in the Ministry — her sadistic pleasure in reminding Neville of his parents' fate and her deliberate prolonging of his suffering
Kreacher's devotion to Bellatrix's photograph and the Black family legacy dimension
Bellatrix's triumphant scream after killing Sirius is present in the film but the familial/Black family dimension of their relationship is entirely absent
Voldemort's public humiliation of Bellatrix over the Tonks marriage (a moment of vulnerability and shame)
score_caps_applied: Cap 2 (above 80 if key relationships change substantially) considered but not applied — relationships are altered but not substantially changed in their core function; the Narcissa and Harry relationships are preserved, and the Voldemort relationship is recognizable if emotionally flattened

Cap 1 (above 85 if major complexity is removed) applied implicitly — the score of 78 reflects the removal of the Spinner's End depth, the quasi-erotic devotion texture, and the Kreacher/Black family material, which together constitute a major portion of her psychological complexity
comparative.key_observations: The films deliver a recognizable and largely faithful Bellatrix Lestrange — her fanaticism, sadism, volatility, strategic intelligence, and theatrical cruelty are all present and consistently evidenced. The core personality pattern is intact. However, the adaptation loses the psychological specificity that makes her more than a theatrical villain: the quasi-erotic dimension of her Voldemort devotion, her sustained intellectual challenge to Snape at Spinner's End, her vulnerability and self-abasement when she fears Voldemort's displeasure, and the Black family legacy dimension are all significantly compressed or absent. The result is a Bellatrix who is faithful in broad strokes but flattened in the nuances that make her fanaticism psychologically specific and her cruelty personally motivated rather than generically theatrical. The score of 78 reflects a substantially faithful portrayal that nonetheless loses enough complexity and relational texture to prevent a higher rating.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Dobby is defined by a cluster of highly distinctive, observable traits: intense devotion to Harry expressed through hyperbolic reverence ('Harry Potter is valiant and bold!'); compulsive self-punishment when he violates his enslavement (head-banging, ear-ironing, oven-door threats); third-person self-reference as a consistent speech pattern; emotional volatility swinging between tearful adoration and fierce protectiveness; dark humor about his own suffering ('Dobby is used to death threats, sir. Dobby gets them five times a day at home'); and, post-freedom, an exuberant, slightly chaotic joy expressed through eccentric clothing and sock obsession. His voice is high-pitched, earnest, grammatically distinctive (third-person, present-tense constructions), and oscillates between abject submission and surprising defiance. A key contradiction: he repeatedly harms Harry while claiming to protect him, and is simultaneously self-flagellating and capable of blasting Lucius Malfoy across a corridor.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core vocal and behavioral signature almost intact: third-person speech, self-punishment (head-banging on floor/wall, ironed hands), tearful adoration of Harry, the death-threat joke, the pillowcase enslavement explanation, the sock obsession, and the fierce defiance at the moment of freedom ('Dobby has no master! Dobby is a free elf!'). In Deathly Hallows Part 1, the film adds a notable line - 'Dobby meant only to maim or seriously injure, not kill' - which is a film invention but is entirely consistent with his book voice and dark humor. The Goblet of Fire and Order of the Phoenix material (kitchen scenes, Room of Requirement, DA warning, Malfoy surveillance) is absent from the film corpus, meaning the post-freedom Dobby - eccentric, wage-negotiating, sock-collecting, Winky-caring - is largely cut.
personality_voice.difference: The core personality markers are faithfully reproduced in the scenes that exist. The main loss is not distortion but omission: the films never show the post-freedom Dobby who negotiates his own wages down, decorates the DA room with Harry-faced baubles, brings gillyweed, warns about Umbridge, or tails Malfoy. This removes the fuller texture of his voice - the comic pride, the growing confidence, the slightly absurd independence - without replacing it. What remains is accurate but weighted toward the enslaved/newly-freed version.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: No significant distortion of personality or voice in scenes that appear. The omission of the GoF/OotP/HBP material removes secondary texture (the free-elf comic pride, the sock-gifting warmth, the Winky-caretaking tenderness) but the dominant traits are intact. Minor penalty for the loss of the fuller post-freedom voice register.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Dobby serves multiple distinct narrative functions across the books: (1) CoS: active antagonist-protector who independently intercepts letters, seals the barrier, enchants the Bludger, and drops the pudding - all self-initiated acts; (2) GoF: plot-critical agent who steals gillyweed from Snape and delivers it to Harry, directly enabling him to complete the second task; (3) OotP: discovers and reveals the Room of Requirement to Harry, warns the DA about Umbridge's raid; (4) HBP: independently surveils Draco Malfoy and reports findings; (5) DH: rescues the prisoners from Malfoy Manor, drops the chandelier, disarms Narcissa, and dies doing so. In every book, Dobby initiates, decides, and changes events.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: CoS film preserves his agency well: he intercepts letters, seals the barrier, enchants the Bludger, drops the pudding, and blasts Lucius - all self-initiated. DH Part 1 preserves his rescue agency: he apparates into the cellar, knocks out Wormtail, drops the chandelier, disarms Narcissa, and dies. However, the GoF gillyweed plot is transferred to Neville in the film (Neville reads about gillyweed in a book and Harry overhears/uses this), removing Dobby's most significant plot-driving act in that book. The OotP Room of Requirement revelation, the DA warning, and the HBP Malfoy surveillance are entirely absent.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The gillyweed transfer to Neville is the most significant agency loss - it removes Dobby's single most consequential plot contribution in GoF. The Room of Requirement revelation (a major narrative gift to Harry in OotP) is also absent. The Malfoy surveillance subplot is cut entirely. These are not compression of scenes Dobby appears in - they are wholesale removal of his narrative function in three separate films.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The gillyweed transfer is a clear Transfer Rule violation: an action that defines Dobby's agency in GoF is given to another character. The Room of Requirement and Malfoy surveillance removals further reduce his narrative function. Cap 3 (above 75 if a core act of agency is transferred) is triggered. The CoS and DH agency is well-preserved, preventing a lower score.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Dobby's motivations are layered and consistent: (1) genuine love and protectiveness toward Harry, rooted in Harry's symbolic importance to all oppressed magical beings; (2) the internal conflict between his enslaved compulsion to obey/self-punish and his growing desire for freedom and dignity; (3) post-freedom, a specific motivation to prove that a free elf can be happy and useful - he negotiates wages down, works hard, cares for Winky - showing that freedom for him means chosen service, not idleness. The internal conflict between loyalty-as-compulsion and loyalty-as-choice is the richest motivational thread.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core motivation (protecting Harry, love for Harry) and the enslavement conflict (self-punishment, the pillowcase explanation, the freedom moment). The hospital wing scene in CoS preserves his explanation of why he acts against Harry's apparent interests. The DH rescue preserves his declaration of free-elf identity as motivation. What is absent is the post-freedom motivational complexity: the wage negotiation, the Winky caretaking, the chosen-service philosophy.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The motivations present in filmed scenes are accurate. The post-freedom motivational layer - that Dobby's freedom is not escape from service but transformation of its nature - is largely invisible in the films because those scenes are cut. This is a simplification rather than a distortion.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but not wrong. The core is intact. The loss of the post-freedom motivational complexity (chosen service vs. compelled service) is a genuine simplification but does not make the filmed motivation incoherent or incorrect.

character_arc.book_baseline: Dobby's arc runs from enslaved, self-punishing house-elf who acts against Harry's interests while claiming to protect him (CoS) → freed elf navigating the disorientation of liberty (GoF kitchen scenes) → confident, joyful free elf who has found his identity through chosen service, friendship, and eccentric self-expression (OotP/HBP) → heroic death as a fully realized free being who chooses to risk everything for Harry and his friends (DH). The arc is one of the most complete in the series for a supporting character: from bondage to chosen freedom to death in freedom.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films show the beginning (enslaved, self-punishing, CoS) and the end (heroic death as free elf, DH). The middle - the GoF kitchen scenes showing his adjustment to freedom, the OotP scenes showing his confident new identity, the HBP surveillance showing his competence and loyalty as a free agent - is almost entirely absent. The arc jumps from 'newly freed' (end of CoS) to 'heroic free elf' (DH) without the intervening development.
character_arc.difference: The arc's starting and ending points are preserved. The middle development - the most humanizing and character-building portion - is cut. The audience sees Dobby freed and then sees him die heroically, but the journey between those points (the disorientation, the growth, the chosen identity) is largely invisible.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed to the point where important steps are missing. The film audience cannot fully understand why Dobby's death lands as it does without the intervening arc. This is a genuine arc incompleteness, not merely compression of scenes that preserve the arc's logic.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Harry-Dobby is the central relationship: defined by Harry's instinctive decency toward Dobby (offering to sit, freeing him, buying socks, treating him as a person), Dobby's adoration escalating into genuine friendship and chosen loyalty, and a power dynamic that inverts over time (Dobby begins as supplicant, ends as equal-in-sacrifice). Secondary: Dobby-Winky (tender, worried caretaking of a fellow elf who cannot adapt to freedom) and Dobby-Malfoys (the defining trauma of his enslavement, which the freedom moment resolves).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Harry-Dobby is preserved in its emotional core across CoS and DH: Harry's decency, Dobby's adoration, the sock-freeing moment, the death scene. The Dobby-Winky relationship is entirely absent (Winky does not appear in the films). The Dobby-Malfoy dynamic is preserved in CoS and DH.
key_relationships.difference: The Harry-Dobby relationship is faithfully rendered in the scenes that exist. The Dobby-Winky relationship - which in the books shows Dobby's capacity for compassion, his role as caretaker, and the contrast between his adaptation to freedom and Winky's inability to adapt - is completely absent. This removes a dimension of Dobby's relational complexity.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The primary relationship (Harry-Dobby) is faithful. The Winky relationship loss removes secondary relational texture but does not fundamentally alter how the Harry-Dobby dynamic reads. Some penalty for the loss of the Winky dynamic, which reveals Dobby's compassion and his role as a model of free-elf identity.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Dobby's complexity in the books comes from several sources: (1) the contradiction between his self-harm and his agency - he is simultaneously the most self-destructive and most effective character in CoS; (2) the post-freedom scenes showing that his 'freedom' is not simple liberation but a complicated renegotiation of identity (he negotiates his wages down, he still wants to serve, he cares for Winky); (3) his role as a comic figure who is also genuinely heroic and genuinely suffering; (4) his function as a lens on house-elf enslavement - his arc is the most developed argument in the series for why the system is wrong; (5) small moments of wit and dark humor that complicate the 'loyal servant' archetype.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the self-harm/agency contradiction in CoS and the heroic death in DH. The dark humor is preserved ('Dobby meant only to maim or seriously injure'). What is lost: the post-freedom complexity (wage negotiation, Winky caretaking, the chosen-service philosophy), the Room of Requirement scenes (showing his knowledge and usefulness as a free elf), the Malfoy surveillance (showing competence and loyalty as chosen acts), the Christmas sock-gifting scenes (showing warmth and reciprocal friendship), and the DA warning scene (showing courage and conflict between house-elf prohibition and personal loyalty).
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The films preserve the most dramatic expressions of Dobby's complexity (CoS self-harm/agency, DH heroic death) but remove the quieter, accumulative material that builds his full character: the post-freedom adjustment, the Winky relationship, the small acts of friendship and competence. The result is a character who is recognizable and emotionally effective but flatter than the books.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Noticeable simplification. The character is not reduced to a single trait, but the removal of GoF/OotP/HBP material means the audience sees only the extremes of his arc (enslaved/heroic) without the middle texture. The Winky relationship, the wage negotiation, the Room of Requirement revelation, and the Malfoy surveillance all add complexity that is absent.

lost_or_transferred_material: Gillyweed procurement (GoF): transferred to Neville Longbottom in the film
Room of Requirement revelation to Harry (OotP): absent from films entirely
DA warning about Umbridge's raid (OotP): absent from films
Draco Malfoy surveillance and reporting (HBP): absent from films
Post-freedom kitchen scenes: wage negotiation, Winky caretaking, sock-gifting, free-elf identity development (GoF): absent from films
