Christmas morning sock exchange with Harry and Ron (GoF): absent from films
Dobby decorating the DA room with Harry-faced baubles (OotP): absent from films
Dobby's relationship with Winky entirely: absent (Winky does not appear in films)
Dobby's role in helping Harry understand house-elf enslavement through his own post-freedom experience: absent from films
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 applied (score capped below 80): a core act of agency - the gillyweed procurement in GoF - is transferred to Neville Longbottom in the film
comparative.key_observations: Dobby's film portrayal is faithful in the scenes that exist - his voice, self-punishment, adoration of Harry, the freedom moment, and heroic death are all accurately rendered. The primary penalty is not distortion but systematic omission: the entire middle of his arc (GoF kitchen scenes, OotP Room of Requirement and DA warning, HBP Malfoy surveillance) is absent, and his most significant plot contribution in GoF (the gillyweed) is transferred to Neville. The result is a character who is emotionally recognizable and whose key moments land correctly, but who is substantially simpler than his book counterpart - the audience sees the enslaved Dobby and the heroic free Dobby without the intervening development that makes the latter meaningful. The Winky relationship, which is the richest expression of his post-freedom complexity, is entirely absent from the films.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Slughorn is defined by a cluster of tightly interlocked traits: vanity, social calculation, comfort-seeking, cowardice dressed as pragmatism, genuine warmth toward talented students, and a collector's mentality toward people. His voice is expansive, self-congratulatory, and name-dropping ('Gwenog Jones, captain of the Holyhead Harpies — free tickets whenever I want them!'). He is capable of genuine emotion — his grief over Lily is real — but he habitually retreats behind bluster and self-interest. He is not cruel; his flaw is moral cowardice and self-preservation, not malice. He speaks in long, digressive sentences, frequently pivoting from sentiment to self-interest mid-thought. He is also capable of sharp, unguarded moments: his terror when Harry asks about Horcruxes ('WRONG!' bellowed at full volume), his half-proud, half-irritable admission about the Intruder Charm, his unselfconscious collector's language about the Black brothers ('I'd have liked the set'). His prejudice about Muggle-borns is present but unconscious — he genuinely believes he is not prejudiced while demonstrating it.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core personality well: the walrus mustache, the belly, the name-dropping photographs, the crystallized pineapple, the social maneuvering, the warmth toward Harry, the cowardice about Voldemort. His voice retains the expansive, self-amused quality. The film adds a small invented anecdote about a fish named Francis (a surrogate for Lily), which is tonally consistent with the character. His terror at the Horcrux question is preserved. His collector's vanity is shown through the shelf of photographs and his comments about students. His cowardice is shown in his initial refusal to return to Hogwarts and his reluctance to give the memory.
personality_voice.difference: Several secondary traits are compressed or lost. The film removes his sharp, unselfconscious social cruelty — the way he simply ignores Ron at every encounter (present in the film but less systematically developed), the cold dismissal of Belby when his uncle connection proves thin, the calculated way he works the Slug Club train compartment. His unconscious Muggle-born prejudice ('Funny how that sometimes happens') is present in the film but Harry's cold pushback ('Not really') is absent, softening the exchange. His half-proud, half-irritable self-awareness ('Two. Didn't hear my Intruder Charm go off, I was taking a bath') is compressed in the film into simpler self-deprecation. His voice in the film is slightly more genial and less barbed than in the books.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main personality traits — vanity, social calculation, cowardice, genuine warmth, collector mentality — are preserved and recognizable. The loss is in secondary texture: the sharper edges of his social cruelty, the unconscious prejudice being called out, the more complex self-awareness. This warrants a minor deduction but not a heavy one, as the dominant pattern is intact.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Slughorn serves three structural functions: (1) gatekeeper of the Horcrux memory, the most plot-critical piece of information in HBP; (2) Potions teacher whose class drives the Half-Blood Prince subplot; (3) social operator whose Slug Club illuminates the wizarding world's class dynamics and Harry's celebrity status. His agency is substantial: he actively resists Dumbledore, actively avoids Harry after the first Horcrux attempt, stops issuing party invitations as a defensive measure, and ultimately makes the pivotal decision to surrender the memory. He is also the one who introduces Felix Felicis as a prize, which drives a key plot thread.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves all three structural functions. Slughorn remains the gatekeeper of the memory, the Potions teacher, and the social operator. His active resistance and avoidance are shown. His decision to surrender the memory is the emotional climax of his arc. Felix Felicis is introduced by him. The Slug Club dinners are shown. His agency in the memory scene is intact — he makes the choice himself after Harry's appeal.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film compresses his active avoidance after the first failed attempt. In the books, Slughorn stops issuing party invitations entirely as a calculated defensive move, Harry lags behind after multiple Potions lessons and is repeatedly evaded, and there is a sustained cat-and-mouse dynamic over weeks. In the film this compression means his agency as an active resister is less visible. The train compartment Slug Club scene — where he actively works the room, interrogates students about their connections, and demonstrates his social machinery in detail — is replaced by a simpler dinner party. The bezoar classroom scene is present but the sustained pattern of Slughorn raving about Harry's abilities over multiple lessons is compressed to a single class.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions and the key act of agency (surrendering the memory) are preserved. The compression of his active avoidance and the reduction of the Slug Club's social machinery reduce the visibility of his agency as a resister and operator, but do not eliminate it. A moderate deduction is warranted.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Slughorn's motivations are layered and consistent: (1) self-preservation above all — he hid for a year, he fears Voldemort, he fears being implicated; (2) genuine affection for talented students, especially Lily, which creates real guilt about the Horcrux conversation; (3) vanity and the desire to be associated with greatness, which makes Harry irresistible to him; (4) moral cowardice — he knows what he did was wrong ('I am not proud... I think I may have done great damage that day') but cannot bring himself to act without being pushed. The internal conflict is between his self-interest and his genuine shame and affection. He is not evil; he is weak.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves all four motivational layers. His self-preservation is shown in his hiding, his fear of Voldemort's name, his resistance to returning. His affection for Lily is made more explicit through the Francis fish anecdote. His vanity is shown through the shelf and his treatment of Harry. His shame is shown in his trembling hands and 'Don't think too badly of me once you've seen it.' The internal conflict between self-interest and guilt is the engine of the Hagrid's hut scene and is well-rendered.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film simplifies the motivation slightly by making the Lily connection more sentimentalized (the fish anecdote) and less complex. In the books, Slughorn's guilt is more specifically about having given Riddle information that enabled mass murder; the film preserves this but the weight of it is slightly reduced by the more emotional framing. His motivation to avoid Harry after the first attempt — calculated self-protection, not just embarrassment — is less clearly shown.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but correct. The core conflict between self-interest and guilt/affection is intact. The simplification does not change the character's fundamental motivation structure.

character_arc.book_baseline: Slughorn's arc in HBP runs from hiding/self-preservation → reluctant return → active avoidance of Harry → gradual softening through social contact → crisis of conscience at Hagrid's hut → surrender of the memory as an act of moral courage. The arc is not a transformation into a hero; it is a small, hard-won act of moral courage from a fundamentally cowardly man. The endpoint — 'I am not proud... I think I may have done great damage that day' — is the culmination. In DH, he returns to fight at Hogwarts, dueling Voldemort alongside McGonagall and Kingsley, which represents the completion of his arc from cowardice to action.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the arc through HBP faithfully: hiding → return → avoidance → surrender of memory. The Hagrid's hut scene is the emotional climax and is well-executed. The DH arc (returning to fight) is present in the film corpus (he is shown arriving with reinforcements and dueling Voldemort alongside McGonagall and Kingsley).
character_arc.difference: The middle section of the arc — the sustained cat-and-mouse over weeks, the multiple failed attempts, the growing desperation — is compressed. This means the surrender of the memory feels slightly less hard-won in the film, because the audience has seen fewer failed attempts. The arc's shape is correct but some of its texture is lost through compression.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but faithful. The key turning points (return, avoidance, surrender, fighting) are all present. The compression reduces the sense of how difficult the surrender was, but does not change the arc's direction or meaning.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships: (1) Slughorn/Harry — a complex dynamic of mutual use: Slughorn wants to collect Harry, Harry wants the memory; genuine warmth develops but the power dynamic is always present. (2) Slughorn/Lily — the emotional core of his guilt; she is his favorite student and her death is the wound he cannot face. (3) Slughorn/Dumbledore — a relationship of mutual respect and mild antagonism; Dumbledore knows exactly how to handle Slughorn and uses Harry as a tool to do so. (4) Slughorn/Voldemort (Riddle) — the relationship that defines his guilt; he was charmed by Riddle and gave him dangerous information.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: All four relationships are present. The Slughorn/Harry dynamic is well-rendered, including the mutual use and the genuine warmth. The Slughorn/Lily relationship is made more explicit and emotional through the fish anecdote. The Slughorn/Dumbledore dynamic is present in the opening scene. The Slughorn/Riddle relationship is shown in the Pensieve memory.
key_relationships.difference: The Slughorn/Harry relationship loses some of its social-calculation dimension — in the books, Slughorn's pursuit of Harry through the Slug Club is more systematically shown as a collector's project, with Harry as the 'jewel of his collection.' The film shows this but less elaborately. The Slughorn/Dumbledore relationship is compressed to the opening scene and loses the ongoing dynamic of Dumbledore managing Slughorn from a distance. Ron's systematic exclusion from Slughorn's attention — which defines the Slughorn/Ron non-relationship and illuminates Slughorn's class snobbery — is present in the film but less developed.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are mostly faithful with some compression. The emotional dynamics are preserved. The loss is in the elaboration of the collector/trophy dynamic with Harry and the ongoing Dumbledore management dynamic.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Slughorn's complexity rests on several elements: (1) his unconscious class prejudice (ignoring Ron, the Muggle-born 'funny how that sometimes happens' exchange); (2) his collector's mentality shown in granular detail across multiple Slug Club scenes; (3) his active, calculated avoidance of Harry over weeks — showing he is not merely passive but strategically self-protective; (4) his genuine pedagogical enthusiasm (the bezoar scene, his delight at Harry's potions work) coexisting with his vanity; (5) the moral weight of his guilt — he knows he may have enabled Voldemort's worst acts; (6) his small acts of self-interest even in emotional moments (pocketing the acromantula venom during Aragog's eulogy).
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves (4), (5), and (6) well. The acromantula venom extraction is shown. His pedagogical enthusiasm is shown. His guilt is shown. The fish anecdote adds emotional texture not in the books but consistent with the character. However, (1) is reduced — the Muggle-born prejudice exchange is softened and Harry's cold response is absent. (2) is compressed — the Slug Club machinery is shown in one dinner party rather than across multiple scenes. (3) is significantly compressed — the weeks of active avoidance, the stopped party invitations, the repeated evasions after Potions class, are reduced to a brief reference.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most significant loss is the sustained cat-and-mouse dynamic that shows Slughorn as an active, calculating self-protector rather than merely a passive coward. In the books, his avoidance of Harry after the first Horcrux attempt is itself an act of agency — he stops the parties, he leaves the dungeon fast, he doesn't answer his door. This shows a more complex, strategically self-aware character. The film's compression makes him seem more passively reluctant than actively evasive. The Muggle-born prejudice nuance is also reduced.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Noticeable simplification of the active-avoidance dimension and the social-machinery dimension. The character remains substantially recognizable and the most important nuances (guilt, warmth, cowardice, collector mentality) are preserved. But the loss of the sustained evasion pattern and the softening of the class prejudice texture represent a meaningful reduction in complexity.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's cold response to Slughorn's 'funny how that sometimes happens' comment about Muggle-borns ('Not really') — removed, softening Slughorn's unconscious prejudice

The sustained multi-week cat-and-mouse dynamic after the first failed Horcrux attempt — Slughorn stopping party invitations, evading Harry after Potions, not answering his door — compressed to near-absence

The train compartment Slug Club scene with its detailed social machinery (interrogating Zabini, Belby, McLaggen, Neville about their connections; the cold dismissal of Belby when his uncle connection proves thin) — replaced by a simpler dinner party
Slughorn's half-proud, half-irritable self-awareness about his own Intruder Charm ('Two. Didn't hear it go off, I was taking a bath') — compressed
The collector's language about the Black brothers ('I'd have liked the set') — present in film but less developed

Dumbledore's explicit post-visit analysis of Slughorn's psychology to Harry ('He likes his comfort... he prefers the backseat') — absent from film, reducing the audience's analytical framework for the character
Slughorn's comment about Amelia Bones's death shaking him — absent, reducing the specificity of his fear
The bezoar classroom scene's full dynamic — Slughorn's sustained raving about Harry over multiple lessons, Hermione's growing frustration — compressed to a single class
score_caps_applied: No hard caps triggered. Total of 78 falls within acceptable range given the level of compression and nuance loss identified.
comparative.key_observations: The film adaptation of Slughorn is substantially faithful. The core personality — vain, warmly calculating, cowardly, genuinely fond of talented students, haunted by guilt over Lily and the Horcrux conversation — is preserved and well-performed. The key narrative function (gatekeeper of the Horcrux memory) and the emotional arc (cowardice to a small act of moral courage) are intact. The Hagrid's hut memory scene is the emotional centerpiece of both versions and is faithfully adapted, with the fish/Lily anecdote serving as an effective film-original surrogate for the book's more extended emotional manipulation. The main losses are in texture and complexity: the sustained active-avoidance dynamic that shows Slughorn as a strategic self-protector rather than merely a passive coward; the granular social machinery of the Slug Club across multiple scenes; and the softening of his unconscious Muggle-born prejudice. These losses reduce the character's complexity without fundamentally altering who he is. The score of 78 reflects a faithful but compressed adaptation that preserves the essential character while losing some of the book's richer behavioral texture.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Narcissa is defined by cold aristocratic composure that cracks under maternal desperation. She is not a fanatic like Bellatrix - she is a pragmatist whose loyalty to Voldemort is entirely conditional on her family's safety. Her voice is controlled and icy in public (Madam Malkin's: 'If you attack my son again, I shall ensure that it is the last thing you ever do'; 'now I know the kind of scum that shops here'), but collapses into raw hysteria when Draco's life is threatened (she sobs, crumples to the floor, seizes Snape's robes, presses her lips to his hand). She is capable of threatening her own sister with a wand. She is not passive - she initiates, she acts, she decides. Her dominant trait is fierce, consuming maternal love that overrides ideology.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the cold composure and the maternal desperation in the Spinner's End scene. Her line 'He's just a boy!' and her plea to Snape are faithful in tone. The forest scene - whispering to Harry, lying to Voldemort - is present and correctly motivated. At the courtyard she calls Draco to her side with quiet authority: 'Come, Draco. Her tone is quiet, but absolute.' This matches the book's pattern of controlled command.
personality_voice.difference: The film compresses the Spinner's End breakdown significantly. The book shows Narcissa crumpling to the floor, sobbing, seizing Snape's robes, pressing her lips to his hand - a full physical and emotional collapse. The film cuts this to a more restrained plea. The Madam Malkin's confrontation with Harry is absent from the film corpus, removing her cold threatening register in a non-domestic context. The line 'There is nothing I wouldn't do anymore!' - her most explicit statement of maternal extremism - is cut. The film adds a small moment of pragmatic wit not in the books ('Well, who can one trust these days?') which is tonally consistent but not book-derived.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - cold composure over desperate maternal love, pragmatism over ideology - is preserved in both key scenes. The compression of the breakdown loses some texture but not the essential trait. The Madam Malkin's absence removes one register (cold public threat) but the forest and Malfoy Manor scenes compensate. Minor penalty for loss of the physical collapse and the 'nothing I wouldn't do' line, which are the most extreme expressions of her defining trait.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Narcissa has three distinct narrative functions: (1) she initiates the Unbreakable Vow plot by going to Snape against orders - this is entirely her decision and her action; (2) she is the agent who lies to Voldemort in the forest, the single most consequential individual act in the entire series; (3) she is the person who delays summoning Voldemort at Malfoy Manor by questioning the identification of Harry, buying time. In all three she is the initiating agent, not a reactor.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The Unbreakable Vow scene is present and Narcissa initiates it. The forest lie is present and she performs it. The Malfoy Manor identification delay is present in the book corpus but absent from the film corpus provided - the film corpus for Malfoy Manor only shows the Deathly Hallows Part 1 drawing room scene (the Voldemort meeting), not the Snatchers scene. The courtyard scene shows her calling Draco to her side and handing him Harry's wand - a small but active gesture.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Malfoy Manor Snatchers scene (book scenes 87-120) is not represented in the film corpus. In the books, Narcissa actively delays the identification, calls for Draco, questions the wand, and asserts 'This is my house, Bella, you don't give orders in my -' - all acts of agency. The film corpus does not include this scene, so I cannot confirm whether this agency is preserved or reduced in the film. Based on the film corpus provided, the two most critical agency moments (Spinner's End initiation, forest lie) are present. The Bellatrix dynamic at Malfoy Manor where Narcissa asserts house authority is not evidenced in the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The two highest-agency moments are preserved. The Malfoy Manor scene is absent from the film corpus, creating a gap. The film corpus does show the Voldemort meeting at Malfoy Manor (DH1 scene 3) where Narcissa's only action is a subtle nod to Lucius to hand over his wand - this is present in the books too (book scene 121 references her apprehension). Minor penalty for the unconfirmed Malfoy Manor Snatchers agency, but the two defining agency moments are intact.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for Spinner's End and forest; reasonably inferred gap for Malfoy Manor Snatchers scene

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Narcissa's motivation is singular and explicit: protect Draco at any cost. This overrides her loyalty to Voldemort, her obedience to Death Eater rules, her relationship with her sister, and her own safety. The internal conflict is between her role as a Death Eater's wife (obedience to the Dark Lord) and her role as a mother (protect her son). She resolves this conflict consistently in favor of motherhood. She is not conflicted about which she chooses - she is conflicted about whether she can succeed.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves this motivation clearly. 'He's just a boy!' is the direct expression of it. The Unbreakable Vow scene shows her desperation to secure Draco's protection. The forest scene shows her lying to Voldemort for Draco. The motivation is never ambiguous in the film.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film loses the explicit articulation of the conflict between Death Eater loyalty and maternal love. In the books, Narcissa explicitly acknowledges she is breaking the Dark Lord's orders ('I know I ought not to be here, I have been told to say nothing to anyone'), and the tension between those two loyalties is dramatized at length. The film compresses this but retains the essential shape. The line 'This is vengeance for Lucius's mistake, I know it!' - which shows her understanding of the political dimension of Draco's assignment - is cut, removing a layer of her analysis.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but correct. The film does not change what she wants or why; it reduces the articulation of the conflict. The core motivation (protect Draco) is fully intact. Minor penalty for loss of the political analysis layer and the explicit acknowledgment of rule-breaking.

character_arc.book_baseline: Narcissa's arc runs from compliant Death Eater wife (GoF, background figure) through desperate mother taking unilateral action against Voldemort's orders (HBP, Spinner's End) to the decisive moment of full defection from Voldemort's cause (DH, forest lie). The arc is a gradual subordination of ideology to maternal love, culminating in an act of active betrayal of Voldemort. The final image in the books is her and Lucius running through the battle 'not even attempting to fight, screaming for their son' - complete abandonment of the Death Eater role. The arc is complete and has a clear trajectory.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film covers the same arc: Spinner's End (desperate mother acting against orders), forest lie (full defection), courtyard (calling Draco to her side, handing him Harry's wand). The trajectory is preserved. The final image of her with Draco in the courtyard is consistent with the arc's resolution.
character_arc.difference: The early arc (GoF background, establishing her as a status-conscious pure-blood aristocrat) is absent from the film corpus, but this is compression rather than alteration - the arc's meaningful stages are present. The Madam Malkin's scene (HBP), which shows her in the intermediate stage (still threatening, still aristocratic, but already protective of Draco in a public confrontation), is absent from the film corpus. This removes a step in the arc between Spinner's End and the Malfoy Manor/forest scenes. The arc is compressed but the key turning points are present.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but the essential trajectory is intact. The Madam Malkin's scene would have shown an intermediate stage, but its absence does not break the arc's logic. The starting point is less established (no GoF presence in the film corpus), but the arc from HBP onward is faithfully rendered. Score reflects compression without fundamental alteration.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for HBP onward; reasonably inferred for earlier arc compression

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships: (1) Narcissa-Draco: consuming protective maternal bond, the engine of all her actions; (2) Narcissa-Bellatrix: complex sisterly dynamic - Bellatrix is fanatical and dismissive of Narcissa's maternal concerns, Narcissa is willing to threaten Bellatrix with a wand, assert herself against her, but ultimately they operate together; (3) Narcissa-Snape: she approaches him as a supplicant, collapses at his feet, presses her lips to his hand - a relationship of desperate dependence in that moment, though she is not generally subordinate to him. The Narcissa-Lucius relationship is present but less dramatized in the corpus - she is shown defending him ('Don't you dare blame my husband!') and subtly directing him (the nod at the Voldemort meeting).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Narcissa-Draco: preserved - her actions are entirely motivated by him, she calls him to her, hands him the wand. Narcissa-Bellatrix: the Spinner's End dynamic is compressed but present; the Malfoy Manor confrontation ('This is my house, Bella') is absent from the film corpus. Narcissa-Snape: the supplicant dynamic is present but the physical collapse (kneeling, kissing his hand) is cut, reducing the intensity of her dependence. Narcissa-Lucius: the subtle nod at the Voldemort meeting is present in the film and is a small but faithful detail.
key_relationships.difference: The Narcissa-Bellatrix relationship loses the most. In the books, Narcissa draws her wand on Bellatrix, physically wrenches away from her, and later asserts house authority against her. The film compresses this to a more passive dynamic. The physical intensity of the Narcissa-Snape supplicant moment is reduced. The 'Don't you dare blame my husband!' defense of Lucius is absent from the film corpus.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Narcissa-Draco relationship is intact. The Narcissa-Bellatrix dynamic is simplified - the book shows Narcissa actively resisting and threatening her sister, the film shows less of this assertiveness. The Narcissa-Snape physical collapse is cut, reducing the emotional register of that relationship. These are meaningful reductions in relational texture, though the core dynamics are recognizable.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for Narcissa-Draco and Narcissa-Snape; partially evidenced for Narcissa-Bellatrix (Malfoy Manor scene absent from film corpus)

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Narcissa's complexity comes from several sources: (1) she is not a true believer - her Death Eater affiliation is entirely instrumental, which makes her more morally interesting than Bellatrix; (2) she is capable of threatening her own sister with a wand; (3) she physically collapses in desperation while maintaining aristocratic composure in other contexts - the contrast is striking; (4) her Madam Malkin's scene shows her as cold, threatening, and classist in a non-domestic context; (5) her line 'There is nothing I wouldn't do anymore!' is the clearest statement of her moral extremism in service of love; (6) she asserts territorial authority at Malfoy Manor against Bellatrix; (7) the forest lie is not just brave - it is a calculated, cold-blooded deception performed under extreme pressure, showing her intelligence and nerve.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the forest lie's intelligence and nerve. The Spinner's End desperation is present. The non-true-believer quality is present (she lies to Voldemort without hesitation). The courtyard scene adds a small moment of quiet authority.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Lost: the physical collapse at Spinner's End (kneeling, kissing Snape's hand, crumpling to the floor) - the most extreme expression of her desperation; the Madam Malkin's scene entirely - her cold threatening register outside the domestic sphere; 'There is nothing I wouldn't do anymore!'; the wand-drawing on Bellatrix; the 'Don't you dare blame my husband!' defense of Lucius; the Malfoy Manor house-authority assertion against Bellatrix. These losses collectively reduce her from a character with striking contradictions (cold aristocrat / desperate mother / pragmatic survivor) to primarily the desperate mother and the forest liar.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film retains the two most narratively prominent moments but loses most of the texture that makes those moments feel earned and complex. The contrast between her public coldness and private collapse is largely absent. Her capacity for aggression toward her own sister is absent. Her classism and threatening register are absent. The character remains recognizable but is substantially simplified. Cap 1 applies: major complexity is removed even though remaining material is superficially faithful.

lost_or_transferred_material: Physical collapse at Spinner's End: Narcissa crumpling to the floor, seizing Snape's robes, pressing her lips to his hand
'There is nothing I wouldn't do anymore!' - explicit statement of maternal extremism overriding all other loyalties
Narcissa drawing her wand on Bellatrix and wrenching free from her grip during the approach to Spinner's End
Madam Malkin's confrontation: Narcissa's cold threatening register toward Harry ('Dumbledore won't always be there to protect you') and classist dismissal of Hermione
'Don't you dare blame my husband!' - defense of Lucius against Bellatrix at the Voldemort meeting
'This is my house, Bella, you don't give orders in my -' - territorial authority assertion at Malfoy Manor (absent from film corpus)
Narcissa's political analysis of Draco's assignment as Voldemort's revenge on Lucius ('This is vengeance for Lucius's mistake, I know it!')
Narcissa and Lucius running through the final battle 'not even attempting to fight, screaming for their son' - the arc's final image of complete abandonment of the Death Eater role
Malfoy Manor Snatchers scene agency: Narcissa delaying identification, questioning the wand, asserting house authority (not confirmed in film corpus)
score_caps_applied: Cap 1 (85 ceiling): The films remove a major part of Narcissa's complexity - specifically the contrast between her cold aristocratic register and her physical collapse, her aggression toward Bellatrix, and her Madam Malkin's threatening persona. The remaining material is superficially faithful but the character is substantially simplified. Score kept below 85.
comparative.key_observations: The film captures Narcissa's two most narratively critical moments faithfully - the Unbreakable Vow initiation and the forest lie - and preserves her core motivation (protect Draco at any cost) without distortion. Her non-true-believer quality and pragmatic intelligence are intact. However, the film loses most of the texture that makes her complex: the physical extremity of her Spinner's End collapse, her capacity for aggression toward Bellatrix, her cold threatening register in public contexts (Madam Malkin's), and her territorial authority at Malfoy Manor. The result is a character who is recognizable and correctly motivated but reduced primarily to the desperate mother and the forest liar, stripped of the contradictions and registers that made her one of the more morally interesting Death Eater-adjacent figures in the books.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan is bombastic, theatrical, and absurdly chivalric. He speaks in archaic, overwrought language ('Come follow me, dear friends, and we shall find our goal, or else shall perish bravely in the charge!'), challenges everyone to duels, invents ridiculously complex passwords, and is physically incompetent - his sword is too long, he can't mount his pony, he falls facedown. He is simultaneously brave and ridiculous, earnest and oblivious to how he is perceived. His emotional tone swings rapidly: rage to elation the moment a 'quest' is offered. He is not malicious, just unhinged with enthusiasm.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core voice well. He uses the same archaic register ('What villains are these that trespass upon my private lands!', 'Back, you scurvy braggarts!', 'Farewell, comrades! If ever you have need of noble heart and steely sinew, call upon Sir Cadogan!'). He is visually depicted brandishing his sword wildly. His proud, oblivious delivery when admitting he let Sirius in ('Certainly, good lady! He had the password. Had the whole week's, in fact. On a little piece of paper.') perfectly captures his earnest incompetence.
personality_voice.difference: The film omits the physical comedy of his incompetence - the sword sinking into the grass, failing to mount the pony, flopping facedown. It also omits the rapid mood swing from rage to quest-joy, and the ongoing harassment of students with changing passwords (mentioned by Neville but not shown as a behavioral pattern). His voice and register are preserved but the full behavioral texture is compressed.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - theatrical, archaic, brave-but-ridiculous, oblivious - is clearly preserved in both dialogue and staging. The loss of physical comedy and the mood-swing moment is a minor reduction in texture, not a change in character. Minor penalty for the compressed behavioral pattern.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan serves two narrative functions: (1) comic guide - he leads Harry, Ron, and Hermione to the North Tower in PoA, demonstrating his absurd bravery and incompetence; (2) plot catalyst - as temporary guardian of Gryffindor Tower, his proud admission that he accepted Neville's written passwords directly enables Sirius Black's entry, a major plot event. He also appears briefly in GoF and OotP as a recurring minor presence.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film retains function (2) - his proud admission about the passwords is present and intact, and it is the direct cause of the security crisis. Function (1) - the quest to the North Tower - is entirely absent from the film corpus. His role as recurring minor presence (GoF reference, OotP brandishing) is also absent from the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The North Tower quest scene, which is his most active moment of agency in the books (he initiates, leads, guides, and performs), is cut. His film role is reduced to the password incident and a brief introductory scene. He retains agency in the password scene - he speaks, he explains, he is the cause - but the active guiding role is lost.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The password scene preserves meaningful agency - he is the direct cause of a major plot event and his admission drives the scene. However, the North Tower quest, his most extended moment of active agency and personality display, is absent. This is a real reduction in narrative function, though not a transfer to another character.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan has no complex internal conflict - he is a portrait knight driven entirely by chivalric enthusiasm: the desire for quests, combat, and glory. His motivation for taking the guardian role is explicitly courage - he was the only portrait brave enough to volunteer after the Fat Lady was attacked. His 'motivation' for letting Sirius in is not malice but oblivious rule-following: he had the passwords, so he let him in. There is no internal conflict; his character is intentionally flat in this dimension.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film does not address his motivation for taking the guardian role explicitly - Dean Thomas's line ('After what happened to the Fat Lady, none of the other pictures would take the job') implies it but does not state his courage as the reason, unlike Percy's explicit 'Sir Cadogan was the only one brave enough to volunteer' in the books. The password admission retains his oblivious rule-following motivation.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The explicit framing of his courage as motivation for volunteering is softened - the film implies it through context rather than stating it. This is a minor compression.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Since Sir Cadogan has no meaningful internal conflict and his motivations are simple, the compression is minor. The core motivation (chivalric enthusiasm, oblivious rule-following) is preserved. Small penalty for the loss of the explicit courage framing.

character_arc.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan has a flat arc - he does not change. He is introduced as absurd and brave, serves as temporary guardian, causes the security breach through oblivious pride, is fired, and returns to his landing. In GoF and OotP he is simply a recurring minor presence, unchanged. The arc is intentionally static; he is a comic recurring figure, not a developing character.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film presents the same flat trajectory: introduced as temporary guardian, causes the breach, is implicitly dismissed (the Fat Lady returns). No arc is invented or altered.
character_arc.difference: The film omits his dismissal scene and the GoF/OotP appearances, but since his arc is flat by design, these omissions do not alter a trajectory that does not exist.
character_arc.penalty_logic: A flat arc in the books that remains flat in the films scores well. The omission of his firing and later appearances is a compression, not an arc change. Minor penalty only for the loss of the GoF/OotP appearances which reinforce his static, recurring nature.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan's key relationships are functional rather than emotional: (1) with Harry, Ron, and Hermione - he guides them, they find him ridiculous (Ron: 'if we ever need someone mental'); (2) with Neville - Neville's written passwords are the direct cause of the breach, creating a specific dynamic of Neville's anxiety vs. Cadogan's oblivious pride; (3) with McGonagall - his proud admission to her is a key confrontational moment; (4) with the Gryffindor students generally - he harasses them with changing passwords, they resent him.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the McGonagall confrontation dynamic (his proud obliviousness vs. her horror). The Neville connection is present - Neville is shown keeping a list of passwords in the introductory scene, and every eye shifts to Neville after the admission. Ron's dismissive 'if we ever need someone mental' is preserved. The student resentment is shown through Seamus's 'He's barking mad!'
key_relationships.difference: The Harry/Ron/Hermione guiding relationship (North Tower quest) is absent. The Neville dynamic is present but less developed - in the books, Neville directly pleads with Sir Cadogan and is refused entry, creating a specific scene of interaction; in the film, Neville is implicated but the direct confrontation between them is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The most important relational dynamics (McGonagall confrontation, student resentment, Neville implication) are preserved in function. The loss of the North Tower guiding relationship and the direct Neville confrontation scene reduces relational texture but does not fundamentally alter how the character is understood through his relationships.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Sir Cadogan's complexity is intentionally limited - he is a comic minor character. His nuance lies in the specific combination of genuine courage (volunteering when others refused) with total incompetence and obliviousness. The physical comedy (sword too long, can't mount pony, falls facedown) is essential texture. The rapid mood swing from rage to quest-joy is a defining behavioral pattern. His ongoing harassment of students with changing passwords builds his presence as a recurring irritant. The GoF and OotP appearances reinforce his static, absurd nature across the series.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the courage/obliviousness combination and the archaic theatrical voice. It loses the physical comedy entirely, the rage-to-quest mood swing, the ongoing password harassment as a lived experience (mentioned but not shown), and all post-PoA appearances.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The physical comedy and mood-swing pattern are the most distinctive nuances of his book characterization and are absent from the film. These are not merely scenes - they are the behavioral texture that makes him more than just 'archaic portrait knight.' Their absence leaves a recognizable but somewhat flattened version.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Sir Cadogan is a minor character whose complexity ceiling is low by design. The film preserves his core identity and his most plot-relevant moment faithfully. However, the physical comedy and mood-swing pattern are genuinely defining, and their absence is a real simplification. The loss of GoF/OotP appearances removes the reinforcement of his recurring nature. Penalty is moderate given the character's inherently limited complexity.

lost_or_transferred_material: North Tower quest scene: Sir Cadogan actively guides Harry, Ron, and Hermione - his most extended moment of agency, physical comedy, and personality display
Physical comedy: sword sinking into grass, failing to mount the fat pony, falling facedown - essential behavioral texture
Rage-to-quest mood swing: the defining behavioral pattern of his personality (rage vanishing instantly when a 'quest' is offered)
Explicit framing of his courage as the reason he volunteered (Percy's line: 'Sir Cadogan was the only one brave enough to volunteer')
Direct confrontation scene between Sir Cadogan and Neville at the portrait hole (Neville pleading, Sir Cadogan refusing, calling him a 'loon')
Sir Cadogan being fired and his portrait returned to the seventh-floor landing
GoF reference (Bill Weasley asking about 'the mad knight')
OotP appearance: Sir Cadogan brandishing sword at Harry on the landing, attempting to follow him into a neighboring picture and being rebuffed by a wolfhound
comparative.key_observations: The film portrayal of Sir Cadogan is faithful in its most essential elements: his archaic theatrical voice, his oblivious pride, his role as temporary guardian, and his plot-critical admission about the passwords are all preserved. The core identity - brave, ridiculous, earnest, oblivious - comes through clearly. The main losses are the physical comedy and mood-swing behavioral pattern that give him his distinctive texture in the books, the active North Tower guiding scene, and all post-PoA appearances. These are real reductions for a character whose complexity is already intentionally limited, but they do not fundamentally misrepresent who he is. The film gives a compressed but recognizable and largely faithful version of a minor comic character, with the penalty reflecting genuine loss of behavioral nuance rather than any distortion of his core identity.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Vernon is defined by a cluster of tightly consistent traits: aggressive conformism, explosive temper delivered in short barked commands and roaring outbursts, deep fear of anything abnormal or magical, social anxiety about neighbours and reputation, bullying dominance over Harry combined with physical cowardice when genuinely threatened, and a blunt, self-satisfied pomposity. His speech is characterised by short declarative commands ('Get the mail', 'Don't ask questions'), sudden volume escalation, sputtering when frightened, and occasional dark humour at Harry's expense. He is not purely one-note: the books show him capable of calculation (the Hogsmeade form negotiation, the Sirius godfather gambit), momentary self-control when it serves him, and even a kind of grudging pragmatism. His fear is always present beneath the bluster.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: Richard Griffiths captures the core traits faithfully: the purple-faced roaring, the barked commands, the physical bulk used to intimidate, the explosive reaction to anything magical. The films preserve his key verbal patterns - short commands, sudden shouting, sputtering. His social anxiety about the Masons dinner, his paranoid letter-burning campaign, his cowardice before Hagrid and Dumbledore, and his bullying of Harry are all present. The Deathly Hallows departure scene adds a small moment of awkward near-civility that has some book basis.
personality_voice.difference: The films lose several layers of Vernon's voice. His internal rationalisation process - the extended self-persuasion sequences in Book 1 where he talks himself out of worry, his calculating use of Marge's visit as leverage, his elaborate paranoid logic about the house plot in DH - is necessarily absent as internal narration. More significantly, the films omit the sustained OotP confrontation where Vernon engages in a prolonged, almost rational debate with Harry about Dementors, Voldemort, and the Ministry, showing a Vernon capable of following complex magical logic when frightened enough. This reveals a more textured cowardice than the films show. The films also omit his humming 'Tiptoe Through the Tulips' while boarding up the house - a small but revealing detail of his self-soothing absurdity.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality is strongly preserved. The loss is primarily of secondary texture: the rationalising interior monologue, the moments of dark self-satisfied humour, and the more extended displays of calculating pragmatism. These are real losses but do not fundamentally alter the recognisable character. Minor penalty warranted.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Vernon serves multiple narrative functions: primary antagonist in Harry's domestic world, embodiment of Muggle hostility to magic, comic grotesque, and - crucially - an active agent who initiates escalating campaigns against Harry (letter suppression, imprisonment, flight to the hut, barring windows, locking Harry in). He also functions as a pressure valve that forces Harry to develop agency of his own. In OotP he is the site where the magical and Muggle worlds collide most directly, and his extended interrogation of Harry about Dementors and Voldemort is a genuine narrative scene with information exchange. In HBP, Dumbledore's visit uses Vernon as a foil to deliver moral judgement. In DH, his departure is a small but structurally complete scene.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his antagonist function and his role as the initiator of the letter-suppression campaign. His imprisonment of Harry in CoS is shown. The Hagrid confrontation, the Dumbledore visit, and the DH departure are all present. He retains agency in the key escalation scenes.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The OotP extended kitchen confrontation - where Vernon actively interrogates Harry, processes information about Dementors and Voldemort, reaches his own (wrong) conclusions, and is then overruled by Petunia's Howler - is almost entirely cut from the film. In the film, the Dementor attack aftermath is compressed to a few lines and Vernon's role is reduced to reactive outrage rather than the sustained, almost-rational engagement of the book. This removes a scene where Vernon has genuine narrative agency as an interlocutor. The GoF scene where Vernon is manipulated by Harry's mention of Sirius (the godfather gambit) is also absent from the film corpus, removing a moment where Harry outmanoeuvres Vernon through calculation. The PoA negotiation over the Hogsmeade form is compressed in the film to a brief exchange, losing the full back-and-forth where both characters display agency.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core antagonist and domestic-oppressor functions are preserved. The losses are real: the OotP kitchen scene is a significant reduction of Vernon's role as an active narrative participant rather than just a reactive obstacle. The godfather gambit removal is a minor loss. Overall role is recognisable but meaningfully reduced in the later films.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Vernon's motivations are consistent and clearly established: terror of abnormality and its social consequences, desire to protect his family's 'normal' status, genuine (if irrational) belief that suppressing Harry's magic is both possible and necessary, and a deep social anxiety about what neighbours and colleagues will think. There is a secondary motivation of genuine dislike and resentment of Harry as an intrusion. His internal conflict is limited but present: in Book 1 he genuinely wrestles with whether to tell Petunia about the Potter whispers; in DH he oscillates repeatedly between denial and acceptance of the danger, and his near-handshake with Harry at departure suggests a suppressed, unresolved ambivalence.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core motivations clearly: fear of magic, social anxiety (Mason dinner), desire to suppress Harry's abnormality. The DH departure scene preserves the ambivalence. The OotP film preserves his fear-driven expulsion attempt.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films lose the Book 1 extended internal monologue where Vernon's self-persuasion and rationalisation are shown in detail - his motivation is shown but not its texture. The DH oscillation (packing and unpacking the car, changing his mind repeatedly over four weeks) is compressed to a single scene, losing the portrait of a man genuinely paralysed between fear and denial. The house-plot theory ('a plot to get this house') is present in the film corpus but the extended back-and-forth that reveals Vernon's specific brand of paranoid logic is reduced.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but correct. The core fear-of-magic and social-anxiety drivers are intact. The loss is in the texture of his internal conflict, not its substance. Minor simplification only.

character_arc.book_baseline: Vernon has a largely flat arc across most of the series - he is consistently hostile, fearful, and bullying. However, there is a meaningful micro-arc within each book (escalation then defeat) and a very small but genuine macro-arc: by DH, prolonged exposure to real magical danger has worn down his denial to the point where he almost shakes Harry's hand and accepts, however grudgingly, that Harry is leaving into genuine peril. This is not a redemption arc but a minimal softening of the most rigid denial. The DH departure scene is the culmination.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films preserve the flat arc across most of the series. The DH departure scene is present and captures the awkward near-civility. The overall trajectory from aggressive denial to grudging, wordless acknowledgement is intact.
character_arc.difference: The film DH departure is slightly more compressed than the book equivalent, and the four-week oscillation that precedes it (which gives the final scene its weight as a resolution) is reduced to a single conversation. The arc's endpoint is present but its preparation is thinner. The book's version of Vernon nearly shaking Harry's hand and then stopping himself is rendered in the film as Dudley's handshake substituting for Vernon's failed gesture - Vernon himself only says 'Well, this is goodbye then, boy' and walks away, which is broadly faithful but loses the specific detail of Vernon's own aborted gesture.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but the endpoint is preserved. The flat nature of the arc across most books means there is less to lose. The DH scene is present. Minor compression penalty.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Vernon's key relationships are: (1) Harry - defined by bullying dominance, fear, and resentment, with Harry increasingly able to leverage Vernon's fears against him; (2) Petunia - a partnership of shared denial and social anxiety, with Petunia often the more emotionally complex partner and Vernon deferring to her on magical matters when frightened; (3) Dudley - uncritical adoration and indulgence, used as a mirror of Vernon's own values. The Harry-Vernon dynamic evolves from pure dominance to a more contested power balance as Harry grows older and learns to use Sirius and the threat of magic as leverage.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry-Vernon antagonism is well preserved. The Petunia partnership is present. The Dudley indulgence is shown. The power-balance shift in the Harry-Vernon relationship is partially shown (CoS imprisonment, OotP expulsion attempt, DH departure).
key_relationships.difference: The films lose the specific scenes where Harry actively outmanoeuvres Vernon - the Hogsmeade form negotiation and the Sirius godfather gambit are the clearest examples. These scenes define the later-book dynamic where Harry is no longer simply a victim but a strategic actor in the relationship. Without them, the film relationship skews slightly more toward simple antagonism and less toward the contested, evolving power dynamic of the books. The Petunia-Vernon relationship loses the OotP Howler scene's revelation that Petunia has independent knowledge of the magical world that Vernon does not share - a moment that briefly fractures their united front and shows Vernon genuinely confused and subordinate.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics are preserved but the evolution of the Harry-Vernon power balance is partially lost, and the Petunia-Vernon fracture moment is absent from the film corpus. These are meaningful but not fundamental losses.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Vernon's complexity lies not in moral ambiguity but in the richness of his specific brand of fearful conformism. Key nuances: his self-persuasion rituals (Book 1 morning), his capacity for calculation when frightened (Hogsmeade form, Sirius gambit), his moments of genuine absurdity (humming while boarding up the house, the house-plot theory), his cowardice beneath the bluster (immediate capitulation to Hagrid, Dumbledore, and the Order), and the DH portrait of a man genuinely destabilised by four weeks of oscillating between denial and terror. The books also show Vernon as someone capable of a kind of dark wit ('Magic carpets all got punctures?') that the films partially preserve.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the bluster-cowardice dynamic, the absurdity, and some of the dark wit. The core texture is recognisable.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Lost material includes: the extended Book 1 internal monologue and self-persuasion; the Hogsmeade form negotiation (calculation and leverage); the Sirius godfather gambit (Harry outmanoeuvring Vernon); the OotP kitchen interrogation (Vernon as active interlocutor processing magical information); the four-week DH oscillation (Vernon paralysed between denial and terror); the 'humming Tiptoe Through the Tulips' detail; Vernon sleeping in a sleeping bag at the front door. These are not individually catastrophic losses but collectively they remove the texture that makes Vernon more than a simple bully - specifically his capacity for calculation, his specific brand of self-soothing absurdity, and his role as an active (if comic) participant in the magical-Muggle collision.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The character remains recognisable and the core traits are present, but a meaningful layer of nuance - particularly the calculating, self-persuading, actively-engaged Vernon of the later books - is thinned. This is a noticeable simplification without being a fundamental flattening.

lost_or_transferred_material: Book 1 extended internal monologue: Vernon's self-persuasion process throughout the day of the wizarding celebrations
Vernon sleeping in a sleeping bag at the front door to intercept Harry
Vernon humming 'Tiptoe Through the Tulips' while boarding up the house
PoA: Harry's Hogsmeade form negotiation - Harry leveraging the Marge visit against Vernon to extract the signature
GoF: The Sirius godfather gambit - Harry manipulating Vernon into allowing the World Cup trip by invoking his convicted-murderer godfather
OotP: Extended kitchen interrogation scene where Vernon actively processes information about Dementors, Voldemort, and the Ministry over multiple owl deliveries
OotP: Vernon's expulsion attempt ('You're not staying here if some loony's after you') and Petunia's Howler overruling him - the fracture in the Dursley united front
DH: Four-week oscillation of packing and unpacking, Vernon changing his mind repeatedly before departure
DH: Vernon's own aborted handshake gesture toward Harry (in the film this beat is displaced onto Dudley)
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function or act of agency is transferred to another character) considered but not applied: the Dudley handshake substitution in DH is a partial displacement but Vernon's farewell line is still present; the cap is not triggered at the level that would force a reduction below the dimension scores already assigned.

Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove a major part of complexity or narrative function) applied implicitly: the OotP kitchen scene removal and the loss of the calculating-Vernon material in PoA/GoF constitute meaningful complexity loss, keeping the total below 85.
comparative.key_observations: Vernon Dursley is substantially faithful in the films: his core personality, dominant traits, voice patterns, motivations, and antagonist function are all clearly preserved, and Richard Griffiths's performance captures the bluster-cowardice dynamic convincingly. The main losses are in texture and evolution rather than identity: the calculating, self-persuading Vernon who actively outmanoeuvres and is outmanoeuvred by Harry in PoA and GoF is largely absent, the extended OotP kitchen confrontation that gives Vernon genuine narrative agency as an interlocutor is cut, and the four-week DH oscillation that gives the departure scene its full weight is compressed. The result is a Vernon who is recognisably the same character but somewhat flatter - more purely a reactive bully and less the specific, richly absurd, fearfully calculating figure of the books. The score reflects strong core faithfulness with meaningful but not fundamental simplification.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Umbridge's defining personality pattern in the books is the sustained, weaponized contrast between her saccharine exterior and her genuine cruelty. She speaks in a high-pitched, girlish, breathy voice; uses diminutives ('dear', 'teensy', 'hem hem'); smiles constantly even while inflicting pain; and deploys bureaucratic language as a form of violence. Her emotional tone is one of controlled, self-righteous pleasure in power. She is not impulsive — she is methodical and patient, watching for weakness, escalating punishment incrementally, and maintaining plausible deniability through institutional language. Key observable patterns: the 'hem hem' throat-clearing as a dominance gesture; the fake-sweet voice that becomes 'dangerously sweet' when she is most threatening; the wide toadlike smile that widens as victims suffer; the use of Ministry authority as personal armor; and sudden, cold fury when her authority is directly challenged (e.g., the Cruciatus scene, shaking Marietta). She also shows genuine ideological conviction — she is not merely following orders but believes in blood purity and Ministry supremacy.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core personality markers with considerable fidelity. Imelda Staunton's performance captures the girlish voice, the 'hem hem', the saccharine sweetness masking cruelty, the toadlike physical description, the pink aesthetic, and the bureaucratic self-righteousness. Key scenes — the first DADA lesson, the blood quill detention, the Trelawney sacking, the forest confrontation — all preserve her essential voice and behavioral pattern. The film Umbridge smiles while inflicting pain, uses 'dear' and sweet tones while threatening, and escalates methodically.
personality_voice.difference: The film compresses her classroom control rituals (the chanting of 'Good afternoon, Professor Umbridge', the hand-raising enforcement, the extended Hermione confrontation) which in the books build a detailed picture of her as a micro-manager of submission. The film also omits her ideological speeches about blood purity and Ministry authority that reveal the depth of her conviction. The Deathly Hallows courtroom scene preserves her cruelty but strips the extended bureaucratic performance that makes her so chilling in the books. The Cruciatus scene is present in the film but truncated — in the books she talks herself into it, revealing her self-justifying psychology ('I am sure the Minister will understand'); the film moves faster. Her admission that she sent the Dementors after Harry is retained, which is crucial.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality — the specific voice, the smile-while-hurting pattern, the 'hem hem', the bureaucratic cruelty — is preserved. The loss is in texture and depth of the ideological dimension and the micro-control rituals. This is compression rather than transformation. Minor penalty for the loss of the extended classroom dominance patterns and the self-justifying internal monologue visible in the Cruciatus buildup.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Umbridge serves multiple structural functions in the books: (1) primary antagonist for the bulk of OotP, replacing Voldemort as the immediate threat; (2) instrument of Ministry oppression, making the institutional villain concrete and personal; (3) driver of the DA's formation and escalation; (4) the agent who fires Trelawney, bans Harry from Quidditch, creates the Inquisitorial Squad, becomes Headmistress, and attempts the Cruciatus Curse on a student. She initiates most of the major plot movements in OotP. In DH she runs the Muggle-Born Registration Commission, possesses the locket Horcrux, and is the direct obstacle Harry must overcome to retrieve it. Her agency is consistently high — she acts, decides, escalates, and forces others to react.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves her as the primary antagonist of OotP and retains her key actions: the blood quill detentions, the Educational Decrees, the Trelawney sacking, the discovery of the DA, and the forest confrontation. In DH Part 1, she appears in the Ministry courtroom running the Muggle-Born Registration Commission and wearing the locket. Her role as the obstacle to retrieving the Horcrux is intact.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several specific acts of agency are reduced or omitted. The film does not show her intercepting Harry's mail or attacking Hedwig (book: Hermione deduces Umbridge tipped off Filch to read Harry's letters). The Inquisitorial Squad's formation and her use of it as a personal enforcement arm is present but compressed. Her attempt to use Veritaserum on Harry (and Snape's deliberate sabotage of this) is retained in the film but the extended interrogation scene in her office — where she tries to drug Harry's tea — is omitted, removing a significant act of premeditated agency. The film also omits her role in the Azkaban breakout aftermath and her escalating campaign against Hagrid and Trelawney (shown only in summary). In DH, her extended courtroom procedure and the full bureaucratic machinery she commands is compressed to a single scene.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Her core agency is preserved — she remains the initiating force in OotP and the obstacle in DH. The omitted material (drugged tea, mail interception, extended Veritaserum interrogation) represents real losses of specific acts of agency that demonstrate her methodical, premeditated cruelty. These are not merely decorative scenes. Minor-to-moderate penalty.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Umbridge's motivations are layered. Surface motivation: enforce Ministry policy, suppress Dumbledore's influence, maintain institutional order. Deeper motivation: personal ambition and the pleasure of power over others. She is not merely a bureaucrat following orders — she drafted anti-werewolf legislation on her own initiative, sent Dementors after Harry without Ministry authorization ('He never knew I ordered Dementors to go after Potter last summer'), and uses the Cruciatus Curse on a student for personal satisfaction. Her ideology (blood purity, distrust of 'half-breeds') is genuine, not performed. There is no meaningful internal conflict — she is ideologically consistent and self-righteous, which is itself a characterization choice. Her motivation in DH (running the Muggle-Born Registration Commission, wearing the locket as a 'family heirloom') shows her opportunism and vanity alongside her ideology.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves her surface motivations (Ministry enforcement, suppression of Dumbledore) and her personal pleasure in cruelty. The Dementor admission is retained. Her ideological dimension is present but less developed — the film does not show her anti-werewolf legislation or her specific prejudices against half-breeds beyond the classroom insult. In DH, her use of the locket as a pure-blood credential is shown (the 'S stands for Selwyn' line is in the screenplay), preserving her vanity and opportunism.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film underplays the depth of her ideological conviction. In the books, her prejudice against 'half-breeds' is a recurring, explicit theme — she insults Lupin in her first lesson, drafted anti-werewolf legislation, and her treatment of Hagrid and the centaurs is framed as ideological as much as tactical. The film shows the behavior but not the consistent ideological framework behind it. Her self-justifying psychology (the internal monologue before the Cruciatus attempt) is absent. These are simplifications rather than contradictions.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Core motivations are present and correct. The simplification is real but does not fundamentally misrepresent her — the film Umbridge acts from the same drives. Moderate penalty for the loss of ideological depth and the self-justifying psychology.

character_arc.book_baseline: Umbridge's arc in OotP is one of escalating power followed by humiliating defeat. She arrives as a teacher, becomes High Inquisitor, becomes Headmistress, attempts the Cruciatus Curse, is carried off by centaurs, and leaves Hogwarts traumatized and silent. In DH she has recovered and risen further — now running the Muggle-Born Registration Commission, a more powerful and more overtly evil position. Her arc across the series is one of institutional evil that survives and adapts. There is no redemption arc and no meaningful internal change — she is flat by design, and that flatness is the point.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows the same arc in OotP: escalation to Headmistress, discovery of the DA, forest confrontation, centaur removal, traumatized departure. The newspaper headline in the film epilogue ('Umbridge suspended for pending investigation') compresses her post-OotP fate. In DH Part 1, she appears running the Commission, showing her survival and adaptation. The arc is structurally intact.
character_arc.difference: The film omits her traumatized silence in the hospital wing and the detail of Peeves chasing her from the school — small but characteristically satisfying beats that complete her humiliation arc. The 'suspended for pending investigation' headline is a significant compression of her book fate (in the books she is simply removed from Hogwarts by Dumbledore's order). The DH arc is present but compressed to a single scene.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is structurally faithful. The compression of her departure and the omission of the hospital wing scene reduce the completeness of her humiliation arc but do not alter its direction or meaning. Minor penalty.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Umbridge's key relationships are: (1) Harry — a sustained battle of wills, with Umbridge as the institutional oppressor and Harry as the resistant victim; the blood quill detentions are the core of this. (2) McGonagall — a power struggle between institutional authority and professional integrity, with McGonagall as the most effective resister among the staff. (3) Fudge — Umbridge as his instrument and enforcer, with a dynamic of mutual use. (4) Dumbledore — Umbridge as the Ministry's weapon against him, with Dumbledore consistently outmaneuvering her. (5) Snape — a tense, mutually suspicious relationship; Snape sabotages her Veritaserum request. (6) The students collectively — Umbridge as oppressor, the DA as organized resistance.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry-Umbridge relationship is well-preserved: the blood quill, the battle of wills, the detention scenes. The McGonagall-Umbridge dynamic is present and effective (the inspection scene, the Trelawney sacking confrontation). The Fudge-Umbridge relationship is shown. The Dumbledore-Umbridge dynamic is present. The Snape-Umbridge scene (Veritaserum refusal) is retained in the film.
key_relationships.difference: The film significantly compresses the McGonagall-Umbridge relationship — the extended careers advice scene where McGonagall openly defies Umbridge ('I will assist you to become an Auror if it is the last thing I do') is absent, removing one of the most dramatically satisfying expressions of staff resistance. The Snape-Umbridge dynamic is present but the full complexity of their mutual contempt and Snape's deliberate sabotage is compressed. The relationship with the broader staff (Flitwick, Sprout, Trelawney) — where Umbridge's isolation becomes clear — is shown but not developed. The DH relationship with Yaxley (her co-prosecutor) is present but minimal.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Core relationships are present and functionally correct. The loss of the extended McGonagall confrontation and the compression of the Snape dynamic represent real reductions in relational texture. The emotional dynamics are preserved in outline but not in full depth. Moderate penalty.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Umbridge's complexity in the books comes from several sources: (1) The sustained contrast between her aesthetic (pink, kittens, lace, girlish voice) and her actions (blood quill, Cruciatus, Dementors) — this is not just visual irony but a consistent characterization of how institutional evil disguises itself. (2) Her genuine ideological conviction, not mere careerism — she drafted anti-werewolf legislation, she believes in blood purity. (3) Her self-awareness about her own cruelty — she talks herself into the Cruciatus, she knows she is breaking the law and justifies it. (4) Her vulnerability — she is genuinely terrified by the centaurs, she is humiliated and traumatized, she sits silent in the hospital wing. (5) The detail of her office (kitten plates, lace, doilies) as a character statement. (6) Her use of Filch, the Inquisitorial Squad, and institutional mechanisms as extensions of her will. (7) The DH material showing her as Head of the Muggle-Born Registration Commission — the full horror of her ideology given institutional power.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the aesthetic contrast (pink cardigan, kitten plates, the office), the blood quill, the 'hem hem', the Trelawney sacking, the centaur scene, and the DH courtroom. The Patronus-as-cat detail in DH (showing she is 'happy' running the Commission) is in the screenplay and is a sophisticated preservation of her characterization.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses: the extended micro-control rituals in the classroom that build her as a totalitarian personality; the anti-werewolf legislation and explicit blood-purity ideology; the drugged tea scene (premeditated, methodical cruelty); the mail interception (showing her surveillance state); the hospital wing silence (her vulnerability and trauma); the full Peeves departure sequence; the extended McGonagall careers confrontation; the detail of her using Willy Widdershins as an informant (showing her institutional corruption); and the full bureaucratic machinery of the Muggle-Born Registration Commission in DH. The film Umbridge is recognizable and effective but is missing the ideological depth and the full range of her methodical cruelty.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film preserves the surface of Umbridge's complexity but loses significant depth — particularly her ideological conviction, her premeditated surveillance and manipulation, and her vulnerability. These are not merely decorative; they are what makes her more than a cartoon villain. The loss of the drugged tea scene and the mail interception removes evidence of her as a methodical, patient predator rather than simply a reactive bully. Noticeable simplification warrants a score in the 8-10 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Umbridge's drafting of anti-werewolf legislation (establishes ideological conviction beyond careerism)
The drugged tea scene in her office (premeditated attempt to extract information from Harry)
Mail interception via Filch / Hedwig attack (methodical surveillance apparatus)
Extended classroom dominance rituals (chanting, hand-raising enforcement, Hermione confrontation across multiple lessons)
Willy Widdershins as paid informant (institutional corruption)
Hospital wing silence and trauma after centaur attack (vulnerability and humiliation arc completion)
Peeves chasing her from Hogwarts (departure humiliation)
Extended McGonagall careers advice confrontation ('I will assist you to become an Auror if it is the last thing I do')
Umbridge's self-justifying internal psychology before the Cruciatus attempt
Full bureaucratic machinery of the Muggle-Born Registration Commission in DH (compressed to single courtroom scene)
Explicit blood-purity ideology as recurring verbal theme (half-breed insults in classroom, treatment of Hagrid framed ideologically)
Snape's deliberate sabotage of Veritaserum request shown but not developed as a relationship dynamic
score_caps_applied: Cap 1 considered (above 85 if major complexity removed): total 77 is below this threshold, cap not binding but consistent with it
Cap 3 considered (above 75 if core agency transferred): no major agency transfer to another character; cap not triggered
comparative.key_observations: The film adaptation of Umbridge is one of the more faithful villain translations in the series. The core personality — the girlish cruelty, the bureaucratic sadism, the 'hem hem', the pink aesthetic, the blood quill — is preserved with high fidelity, and Imelda Staunton's performance captures the essential characterization. The structural arc (escalation to Headmistress, humiliation by centaurs, return in DH as Commission head) is intact. The primary losses are in depth rather than direction: the film Umbridge lacks the ideological conviction (blood-purity beliefs, anti-werewolf legislation), the methodical surveillance apparatus (mail interception, drugged tea), and the full texture of her micro-control rituals that make her so distinctively chilling in the books. The DH Patronus-as-cat detail is a sophisticated preservation of her characterization. The score of 77 reflects a portrayal that is recognizable, effective, and structurally faithful, but meaningfully simplified in the dimensions that made her one of Rowling's most complex antagonists.

personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Flint is established as physically imposing ('trollish cunning'), deliberately dirty in play (blocking Harry on purpose, smashing into Angelina), verbally cunning when it suits him (producing Snape's note with a smug flourish, commenting on the new brooms with casual nastiness), and capable of low scheming (the Dementor disguise prank in PoA). He is not a talker — his lines are short, functional, and often contemptuous. He laughs at Ron's slug-belching, doubles over at others' misfortune, and his emotional register is narrow: aggression, mockery, and cold satisfaction.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the physical description ('trollish Slytherin Captain') and the broad behavioral pattern: he produces the Snape note with the same casual confidence, delivers the line about Draco's father's 'generous gift,' and is shown as aggressive on the pitch. His dialogue is minimal but tonally consistent — short, dismissive, slightly smug. The film does not add warmth, heroism, or complexity that contradicts the book version.
personality_voice.difference: The film compresses his presence significantly. Several book moments that build his voice — the post-match howling about Harry 'nearly swallowing' the Snitch, the Dementor disguise scheme in PoA, his reaction to Fred's bat to the nose — are absent or reduced. The film Flint speaks even less than the book Flint, which risks flattening him, but what dialogue exists is tonally faithful. The 'Give me that!' and 'Take that side!' lines in the film are consistent with his commanding, blunt register.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality — aggressive, cunning in a low way, contemptuous, physically dominant, short-spoken — is preserved in what the film shows. The compression removes texture (the post-match gloating, the PoA scheming) but does not introduce contradictory traits.
