Minor penalty for loss of secondary texture rather than core personality change.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what compression removes.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Flint's narrative function is as the primary Slytherin antagonist on the Quidditch pitch across the first three books. He actively fouls (blocks Harry deliberately in PS, smashes into Angelina in PoA), schemes off-pitch (produces the Snape note to steal practice time, organizes the Dementor disguise in PoA), and serves as the institutional face of Slytherin's win-at-all-costs ethos. He is the one who acts — he initiates confrontations, exploits loopholes, and directs his team.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves his role as Slytherin Quidditch Captain and shows him in the pitch confrontation scene (producing the note, referencing Draco's father's gift). On the pitch in CoS, he is shown as aggressive. However, the PoA Dementor disguise scheme — where Flint actively organizes sabotage off the pitch — is absent from the film corpus entirely. The film also gives Malfoy more prominence in the courtyard scene (Malfoy delivers the key insult and the broomstick reveal speech), with Flint reduced to a supporting line ('A generous gift from Draco's father').
narrative_role_agency.difference: In the book courtyard scene, Flint leads the confrontation more actively — he delivers the line about the new brooms with 'trollish cunning,' he physically interposes himself to stop Fred and George from attacking Malfoy. In the film, Wood holds back Fred and George, and Malfoy drives the scene. Flint's line is reduced to one sentence. The PoA sabotage scheme is absent from the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Some agency is transferred to Malfoy and Wood in the courtyard scene. The PoA scheme is a meaningful act of off-pitch agency that is absent. However, Flint retains his core Quidditch role and the note scene. Moderate penalty for partial agency transfer and missing scheme.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for the courtyard transfer; reasonably inferred for PoA absence based on corpus.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Flint's motivations are simple but consistent: win at Quidditch by any means, assert Slytherin dominance, exploit institutional advantages (Snape's favoritism), and humiliate Gryffindor. There is no internal conflict — he is not torn, not ambivalent. His motivation is purely competitive and tribal.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film does not contradict these motivations. His actions (producing the note, aggressive play, directing teammates) are consistent with the same win-at-all-costs drive. No new motivation is introduced.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No meaningful change. The motivations are simple in the books and remain simple in the film. The absence of the PoA scheme removes one expression of those motivations but does not alter what drives him.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simple in the books and are preserved as simple in the film. No penalty for simplification that was already present in the source. Minor penalty only because the film provides so little material that motivations must be largely inferred rather than demonstrated.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from behavioral patterns; limited direct evidence in film corpus.

character_arc.book_baseline: Flint has a flat arc across the books. He is the same character in PS, CoS, and PoA: aggressive, scheming, losing to Gryffindor. His arc is intentionally flat — he exists as a recurring antagonist without growth or change. He loses the Quidditch Cup in PoA and disappears from the series. The flat arc is canonical and intentional.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film also presents a flat arc. He appears in PS and CoS as the same character. No false arc is invented. No growth or change is implied.
character_arc.difference: No meaningful difference. The arc is flat in both. The film does not attempt to give him a redemption arc or a fall arc that contradicts the books.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Per the scoring rules, a character scores full points if their arc is flat in the books and flat in the films. Minor deduction only because the PoA material (his final appearance and loss) is absent from the film corpus, making the arc assessment incomplete rather than contradicted.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for flatness; not enough evidence to confirm the PoA endpoint from the film corpus.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Flint's key relationships are: (1) adversarial rivalry with Oliver Wood — mutual contempt, physical intimidation, competitive aggression; (2) subordinate alignment with Malfoy/Snape — he benefits from Snape's favoritism and operates in Malfoy's orbit without being subordinate to Draco personally; (3) antagonism toward Harry as the Gryffindor Seeker.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Wood rivalry is preserved in the note confrontation and the handshake scenes (both books and film show them gripping each other's hands with hostility). The Snape/Malfoy alignment is preserved via the note scene. The antagonism toward Harry is present on the pitch.
key_relationships.difference: In the film courtyard scene, Flint's relationship with Malfoy shifts slightly — Malfoy drives the scene and Flint becomes more of a supporting presence, whereas in the books Flint leads and Malfoy is the one who delivers the slur after Flint has set the stage. This subtly repositions Flint as subordinate to Malfoy rather than operating in parallel. The Wood rivalry loses some texture (no PoA handshake with broken-finger grip from that book).
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics are preserved but the Malfoy relationship dynamic is subtly altered by the film's choice to center Malfoy in the courtyard scene. Moderate penalty for this repositioning and for the loss of PoA rivalry texture.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for the dynamic shift.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Flint is not a complex character, but he has specific texture: the post-match gloating ('He didn't catch it, he nearly swallowed it'), the opportunistic exploitation of Harry's broom crisis to score five goals unnoticed, the PoA Dementor disguise scheme showing premeditated off-pitch scheming, the physical comedy of doubling over at Ron's slug-belching, and the detail that he has 'some troll blood in him' establishing him as physically grotesque rather than merely large. These details build a specific kind of low cunning and unsportsmanlike opportunism.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the physical description and the note scheme. It loses the post-match gloating, the opportunistic scoring during Harry's broom crisis, the PoA scheme, and the slug-belching reaction. The film Flint is recognizable but stripped of the specific moments that make him memorably unpleasant rather than generically villainous.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The lost material — particularly the opportunistic scoring during the broom crisis and the PoA scheme — removes the most distinctive expressions of his character: that he is not just aggressive but specifically opportunistic and premeditated in his unsportsmanship. Without these, he reads as a generic bully rather than a specifically crafted minor antagonist.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The losses are real but Flint is a minor character whose complexity was always limited. The film preserves enough to keep him recognizable. The penalty reflects the loss of the specific texture that distinguished him from a generic antagonist, without overclaiming that this fundamentally changes who he is.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is absent; directly evidenced for what is preserved.

lost_or_transferred_material: Post-match gloating after PS Quidditch match ('He didn't catch it, he nearly swallowed it') — absent from film
Opportunistic scoring of five goals while Harry's broom is cursed and the crowd is distracted — absent from film
PoA Dementor disguise scheme (Flint organizing Malfoy, Crabbe, Goyle to sabotage Harry before the match) — absent from film corpus
Flint physically interposing himself to stop Fred and George from attacking Malfoy in the courtyard — transferred to Oliver Wood in the film
Flint leading the courtyard confrontation with 'trollish cunning' delivery of the broom reveal — partially transferred to Malfoy in the film
Flint doubled over laughing at Ron's slug-belching — absent from film
Fred Weasley batting his club at Flint's nose (PoA) and Flint bleeding freely — absent from film corpus
Flint's penalty shot saved by Wood in PoA match — absent from film corpus
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 considered (transfer of agency): partial transfer of courtyard scene agency to Malfoy and Wood noted; cap not fully triggered as transfer is partial and Flint retains his core Quidditch role
Cap 6 considered (limited screen time): Flint is a minor character; limited screen time does not automatically reduce score, but removed material does affect texture and nuance scores
comparative.key_observations: The film portrayal of Marcus Flint is broadly faithful in personality, tone, and core function — he is recognizably the same aggressive, scheming, physically imposing Slytherin captain. The main losses are specific textual moments that gave him distinctive unsportsmanlike texture (opportunistic scoring during the broom crisis, post-match gloating, the PoA sabotage scheme) and a partial shift in the courtyard scene where Malfoy takes more of the lead. These losses reduce his memorability as a specifically crafted minor antagonist without fundamentally changing who he is. Confidence is medium throughout because Flint is a minor character with limited film material, making several dimensions dependent on inference from behavioral patterns rather than direct evidence.

personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Mary Cattermole is defined by visible, sustained terror. She trembles from head to foot before entering the courtroom, shudders passing the dementors, and cries repeatedly throughout the hearing. Her speech is fragmented and broken ('T-took? I didn't t-take it... I b-bought it'), reflecting panic rather than composure. She is not passive in the sense of being blank - she is actively distressed, emotionally overwhelmed, and her voice is that of someone barely holding together. She also shows a flash of confusion and distrust toward Harry when she recognizes him as the one who submitted her name. After being freed, she remains frightened and disoriented, not immediately grateful or composed.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of Mary's terror - she is described as 'frail', her face 'wan', her eyes 'glistening with fear'. Her dialogue retains the key assertion about her wand ('I got it in Diagon Alley, at Ollivander's, when I was eleven. It chose me.') and her desperate appeal to Ron ('Tell them what I am!'). The moment of recognizing Harry is also preserved. However, the film compresses her emotional arc significantly: the sustained sobbing, the fragmented speech, and the prolonged distress of the book are replaced by a more contained, quieter portrayal.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses the broken, stuttering speech pattern that signals Mary's extreme psychological state in the books. The book's 'T-took? I didn't t-take it... I b-bought it' becomes the film's fluent 'But I didn't take it. I got it in Diagon Alley.' The repeated sobbing, the shaking, and the post-rescue confusion and fear are compressed or absent. The film also omits her distrust of Harry upon being freed ('But Reg said you were the one who submitted my name'), which in the books adds a layer of complexity to her reaction.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The dominant trait - terror - is preserved, and the key dialogue beats are intact. However, the specific texture of her voice (fragmented, sobbing, barely coherent) is smoothed out in the film. The loss of her post-rescue distrust of Harry removes a small but characteristically human note of confusion. This is a minor-to-moderate simplification of voice and emotional texture, not a fundamental change.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Mary Cattermole's narrative role is to be the victim whose rescue drives the Ministry infiltration sequence to its crisis point. She is the object of the hearing, the catalyst for Harry's intervention, and the person whose escape Harry organizes. She has limited agency by design - she is chained, accused, and powerless - but she does speak in her own defense, she reacts with distrust to Harry, and she participates in the chaotic escape. Her presence in the Atrium and her confusion over the two Reggies contributes to the tension of the escape.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves her role as the victim-catalyst. She is present at the hearing, she is freed, and she recognizes Harry. However, two specific agency moments are transferred: in the books, Harry frees her chains (casting Relashio himself after a failed Diffindo); in the film, Hermione casts Relashio. In the books, Harry gives her the direct instruction to flee ('Go home, grab your children, and get out'); in the film this beat is compressed or absent. The Atrium confusion with the two Reggies is also absent from the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The chain-breaking is transferred from Harry to Hermione. The explicit instruction to flee is absent. The Atrium scene with the two Reggies - where Mary's confusion and the comedic/tense doubling of her husband contributes to the escape chaos - is not present in the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer of the Relashio moment from Harry to Hermione is a minor agency reassignment within the scene. More significantly, the Atrium sequence where Mary interacts with both Reggies is absent, removing her participation in the escape's climactic chaos. Her role as victim-catalyst is preserved, but some of the texture of her participation in the escape is lost. This is a moderate reduction, not a fundamental reassignment.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Mary's motivations are simple and clear: she wants to prove she is a witch, she is terrified for herself and her children, and she wants to find her husband. These are stated explicitly ('They're frightened, they think that I might not come home', 'I don't know where he is, he was supposed to meet me here'). There is no deep internal conflict - she is a victim, not a morally complex figure - but her fear for her children is a distinct and humanizing motivational layer.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core motivation - she asserts her identity as a witch and appeals to Ron/Reg. The fear for her children is not explicitly stated in the film corpus, though her distress is visible. The line about not knowing where Reg is ('he was supposed to meet me here') is absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The explicit statement of fear for her children is absent from the film corpus. The line about Reg being supposed to meet her is also absent. These are small but humanizing details that ground her terror in specific maternal and relational stakes rather than abstract fear.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivation (prove she is a witch, survive the hearing) is intact. The loss of the explicit maternal fear is a minor simplification - the film conveys distress but without the specific grounding in her children's fear. This is a slight flattening, not a fundamental change.

character_arc.book_baseline: Mary has a micro-arc within the Ministry sequence: she enters as a terrified, powerless accused; she is freed by Harry; she remains confused and frightened even after being freed; she is reunited with (the real) Reg in the Atrium amid chaos; and she is last seen being urged to let go and flee. The arc is not one of growth but of survival - from captive to (barely) free, still disoriented.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the essential shape: accused, freed, recognizes Harry, is urged to flee. The post-rescue confusion and the Atrium chaos with the two Reggies is absent, so the arc ends earlier and more cleanly than in the books.
character_arc.difference: The arc's endpoint in the film is the moment of recognizing Harry, which is more of a triumphant beat ('Reg, it's Harry Potter!') than the book's continued disorientation and confusion. The book's arc ends in ongoing chaos; the film's ends on a note of wonder.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but the essential movement (captive to freed) is preserved. The tonal difference at the endpoint - wonder vs. continued confusion - is a minor shift. The arc is short enough that compression does not fundamentally alter it.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Mary's key relationships in this sequence are: (1) with Ron-as-Reg, her husband, who she clings to for reassurance during the hearing; (2) with Harry-as-Runcorn, whom she initially distrusts upon being freed because he submitted her name; (3) with the real Reg, whose appearance in the Atrium creates the comedic and tense doubling. The dynamic with Ron-as-Reg is one of desperate dependence; the dynamic with Harry is one of confused distrust that shifts to reluctant compliance.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the dynamic with Ron-as-Reg: she calls out to him, he places his hands on her shoulders reassuringly, she appeals to him during the hearing. The moment of distrust toward Harry upon being freed is absent - instead she reacts with wonder ('You? It's you! Reg, it's Harry Potter!'). The real Reg's appearance and the doubling confusion is absent from the film corpus.
key_relationships.difference: The distrust dynamic with Harry is replaced by wonder and recognition. The real Reg's appearance and the resulting relational confusion is absent. The dependence on Ron-as-Reg is preserved but slightly softened (the book has her throwing herself into his arms; the film has a quieter dynamic).
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The loss of the distrust moment with Harry changes the emotional texture of that relationship from complicated to straightforwardly positive. The absence of the real Reg removes a key relational beat. These are moderate losses for a character whose relationships are the primary vehicle for her characterization in this sequence.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Mary's complexity is limited by her role, but the books give her specific humanizing details: the fragmented, sobbing speech; the explicit fear for her named children (Maisie, Ellie, Alfred); the distrust of Harry upon being freed; the continued disorientation after rescue; the chaotic Atrium scene with two Reggies. These details prevent her from being merely a generic victim and give her a specific emotional texture.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of her victimhood and her key assertion about her wand. It loses the fragmented speech, the explicit maternal fear, the distrust of Harry, the post-rescue disorientation, and the Atrium chaos. What remains is a recognizable but somewhat flattened version of the character.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The specific humanizing details that distinguish Mary from a generic victim are largely absent. The film's Mary is frightened and sympathetic but lacks the particular texture of the book's Mary - the broken voice, the named children, the distrust, the ongoing confusion.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: For a minor character, the loss of these details is meaningful because they are the primary vehicle for her characterization. Without them, she functions as a plot device (the person to be rescued) rather than a specific human being. This is a noticeable simplification, though not a fundamental distortion.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's casting of Relashio to free Mary's chains is transferred to Hermione in the film
Harry's explicit instruction to Mary to flee with her children ('Go home, grab your children, and get out of the country') is absent from the film
Mary's fragmented, sobbing speech pattern ('T-took? I didn't t-take it... I b-bought it') is smoothed into fluent dialogue in the film
Mary's explicit distrust of Harry upon being freed ('But Reg said you were the one who submitted my name for questioning') is absent; replaced by wonder
Mary's explicit fear for her named children ('They're frightened, they think that I might not come home') is absent from the film
The Atrium sequence with the real Reg Cattermole appearing and Mary's confusion between the two Reggies is absent from the film corpus
Mary's continued disorientation and fear after being freed ('I don't understand') is compressed in the film
Mary's line about Reg being supposed to meet her at the Ministry is absent
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Mary Cattermole's essential function and core characterization - a terrified Muggle-born witch facing a rigged tribunal, whose rescue drives the Ministry sequence. The key dialogue beats (the wand chose her, the appeal to Reg) are intact, and her terror is conveyed. However, the film loses the specific humanizing texture that distinguishes her from a generic victim: the fragmented sobbing speech, the explicit maternal fear for her named children, the distrust of Harry upon being freed, and the chaotic Atrium sequence with the real Reg. One agency moment (freeing her chains) is transferred from Harry to Hermione. The result is a recognizable but somewhat flattened portrayal - faithful in outline, simplified in texture. Confidence is Medium throughout because Mary is a minor character with limited corpus material, making fine-grained evaluation necessarily tentative.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Norbert in the books is established through behavior rather than speech: dangerous, instinctively aggressive (bites Ron, bites Hagrid, snaps at fingers), physically destructive (bangs tail on walls, rips teddy bear apart), yet simultaneously treated by Hagrid as an innocent baby. The dragon's 'personality' is conveyed through a consistent pattern of violence contrasted with Hagrid's delusion about its gentleness. It hiccups sparks, grows rapidly, eats dead rats by the crate, and has poisonous fangs. The comedy of the character comes from the gap between its genuine danger and Hagrid's maternal projection onto it.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core behavioral pattern: Norbert hatches, is immediately dangerous (hiccups a fireball into Hagrid's beard), snaps and is physically threatening. Hagrid's 'Isn't he beautiful? Look, he knows his mummy' dialogue is directly preserved. The physical design matches the book description (spiny, jet-black, orange eyes). The fireball hiccup replaces the biting/poisonous fang detail as the primary danger demonstration.
personality_voice.difference: The film compresses Norbert's dangerous personality into a single scene rather than the sustained week-long escalation in the books (growing three times in length, eating crates of rats, biting Ron with poisonous fangs, biting Hagrid on the leg, banging tail on walls). The poisonous bite - which has real plot consequences for Ron - is entirely absent. The ripping-apart-the-teddy-bear detail is absent. The film Norbert is dangerous but the sustained, escalating, genuinely threatening nature is reduced to a single comedic fireball moment.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality traits are present and recognizable. The fireball hiccup faithfully captures the 'dangerous but treated as cute' dynamic. However, the removal of the poisonous bite and the week of escalating danger means the film presents a slightly softer version - more comedic hiccup than genuinely threatening creature. This is a minor simplification rather than a fundamental change.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Norbert serves multiple narrative functions in the books: (1) a direct plot driver - the need to smuggle Norbert out causes Harry, Ron, and Hermione to be caught out of bed, which leads to their detention in the Forbidden Forest, which leads to Harry learning about Voldemort and the unicorn blood; (2) a source of real physical consequence - Ron's poisonous bite is a subplot with Malfoy using it as leverage; (3) a recurring reference point throughout later books (GoF, OotP, DH) that reinforces Hagrid's character. Norbert is also the reason Malfoy discovers the plan, creating the Malfoy-detention subplot.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core narrative chain: Norbert's existence leads to Malfoy seeing him through the window, which leads to the midnight tower mission, which leads to detention, which leads to the Forbidden Forest and the Voldemort/unicorn revelation. The causal chain is intact. However, the film simplifies the mechanism: Dumbledore sends Norbert to Romania directly (off-screen), removing the Charlie subplot entirely. The detention is triggered by being caught without the cloak rather than the more complex book sequence involving Ron's hospitalization and Malfoy's leverage.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Charlie subplot - Ron's idea, the owl exchange, the planning, Ron's hospitalization from the poisonous bite, Malfoy finding Charlie's letter - is entirely removed. In the film, Norbert's departure is handled by Dumbledore off-screen. This removes a significant portion of Norbert's plot-driving function and transfers the resolution agency from the trio (specifically Ron's idea) to Dumbledore.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer of the 'how to get rid of Norbert' solution from Ron to Dumbledore is a meaningful agency transfer. In the books, the trio actively solve the Norbert problem; in the film, it is resolved for them. The core narrative function (Norbert causes detention causes Forest scene) is preserved, but the intermediate plot machinery is removed. This warrants a moderate penalty.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: As an animal, Norbert has no complex internal motivations in the human sense. Its 'motivations' are purely instinctual: self-preservation, aggression, hunger. There is no internal conflict. The interest lies entirely in how Norbert functions as an object of Hagrid's projection and the trio's problem.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film presents the same instinctual creature with no internal life beyond animal behavior.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No meaningful difference. Norbert is not a character with motivations or internal conflict in either medium.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: This dimension is largely inapplicable to Norbert as a non-speaking animal character. Scoring reflects the absence of any distortion rather than rich preservation. Score is high because there is nothing to get wrong.

character_arc.book_baseline: Norbert's arc in the books is: egg won in a card game → hatching → rapid dangerous growth → smuggled out to Charlie in Romania → later revealed to be female (Norberta) → referenced as one of Hagrid's most painful memories during Azkaban. The arc spans from PS through DH, with the Norberta reveal in HBP adding a small retroactive detail. The Azkaban reference in PoA gives Norbert emotional weight as one of Hagrid's defining losses.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film arc is: egg → hatching → Dumbledore sends to Romania (off-screen). The Norberta reveal does not occur in the films. The Azkaban emotional callback does not occur. The arc is truncated to the PS storyline only.
character_arc.difference: The Norberta gender reveal (a small but characteristically Rowling retroactive detail) is absent. More significantly, the Azkaban callback - where Hagrid lists letting Norbert go alongside his father's death and his Hogwarts expulsion as his most painful memories - is absent from the films. This removes the emotional weight Norbert carries as a symbol of Hagrid's losses.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The core arc (hatching, danger, departure) is present but compressed. The loss of the Azkaban callback is notable - it elevates Norbert from a one-book plot device to something with lasting emotional resonance for Hagrid. However, this is primarily a loss for Hagrid's characterization rather than Norbert's arc per se. The Norberta reveal is a minor omission. The arc is compressed but the essential shape is present.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Norbert's primary relationship is with Hagrid: a one-sided parental projection in which Hagrid treats a genuinely dangerous creature as a beloved baby, calling himself 'Mommy,' singing lullabies, packing a teddy bear, and weeping at the separation. This relationship is central to Hagrid's characterization as someone who cannot see danger in creatures he loves. Secondary relationship is with the trio, who experience Norbert as a genuine threat and problem to be solved.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Hagrid-Norbert relationship is preserved in its essential dynamic: 'Isn't he beautiful? Look, he knows his mummy. Hehehe. Hallo, Norbert' is directly in the film. Hagrid's grief at Norbert's departure ('What if he don't like Romania? What if the other dragons are mean to him? He's only a baby') is present and faithful. The trio's relationship with Norbert as a problem is also preserved.
key_relationships.difference: The film omits the lullaby, the teddy bear, and the extended week of Hagrid's delusional parenting. The depth of Hagrid's attachment is somewhat compressed. The Azkaban callback that shows the relationship's lasting emotional weight for Hagrid is absent from the films.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamic is faithfully preserved - Hagrid's delusional parental love for a dangerous creature is clearly present in both the hatching scene and the departure dialogue. The compression removes texture but not the essential dynamic. Minor penalty for the loss of the teddy bear/lullaby details and the Azkaban callback.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Norbert's complexity in the books comes from: (1) the sustained escalation of danger over multiple weeks; (2) the poisonous bite with real plot consequences (Ron hospitalized, Malfoy leverage); (3) the Charlie subplot showing the trio's active problem-solving; (4) the Azkaban callback giving Norbert lasting emotional weight; (5) the Norberta reveal as a small retroactive complication; (6) Ron's repeated references to Norbert as evidence against trusting Hagrid's judgment about dangerous creatures.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the hatching, the immediate danger (fireball), Hagrid's parental delusion, and the departure grief. It loses the sustained escalation, the poisonous bite subplot, the Charlie planning sequence, the Azkaban callback, and the Norberta reveal.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most significant lost material is: (a) Ron's poisonous bite and Malfoy's leverage - this removes a real consequence of Norbert's danger and a subplot that shows Malfoy's cruelty; (b) the Charlie subplot - this removes the trio's active agency in solving the problem; (c) the Azkaban callback - this removes Norbert's lasting emotional significance to Hagrid; (d) Ron's later references to Norbert as a cautionary example of Hagrid's dangerous pets.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film presents a recognizable but simplified Norbert. The core comedic/dangerous dynamic is present, but the sustained consequences, the active problem-solving subplot, and the lasting emotional resonance are absent. This is a noticeable simplification - Norbert becomes a one-scene comedic beat rather than a multi-week plot driver with real consequences.

lost_or_transferred_material: Ron's poisonous dragon bite and hospitalization - entirely absent from the film
Malfoy finding Charlie's letter and using Ron's injury as leverage - absent
The Charlie subplot: Ron's idea to send Norbert to Romania, the owl exchange, the planning - absent; resolution transferred to Dumbledore acting off-screen
The sustained week of Norbert's escalating danger (growing three times in length, eating crates of dead rats, biting Hagrid on the leg)
Hagrid singing Norbert a lullaby and packing a teddy bear for the journey
The invisibility cloak tower sequence and the physical smuggling of Norbert - absent from the film
The Azkaban callback in PoA where Hagrid lists letting Norbert go as one of his most painful memories
The Norberta gender reveal in HBP
Ron's later references to Norbert as a cautionary example against Hagrid's dangerous creature friendships (PoA, GoF)
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75) considered: a core book action - the trio's plan to send Norbert to Charlie, specifically Ron's idea and the active smuggling sequence - is transferred to Dumbledore in the film. Cap applied, score kept at 77.
comparative.key_observations: Norbert is a non-speaking animal character whose 'faithfulness' is necessarily evaluated through behavioral patterns and narrative function rather than voice or internal life. The film preserves the essential comedic-dangerous dynamic and Hagrid's delusional parental attachment faithfully. The core narrative chain (Norbert causes detention causes Forest scene) is intact. However, the film significantly compresses Norbert's role by removing the poisonous bite subplot, transferring the resolution from the trio's active planning to Dumbledore acting off-screen, and omitting the later-book material that gives Norbert lasting emotional weight. The result is a recognizable but simplified version - faithful in its immediate presentation but stripped of the sustained consequences and emotional resonance that make Norbert more than a one-scene comedic beat in the books. Confidence is medium overall because Norbert is a minor non-speaking character and several dimensions (motivations, internal conflict) are largely inapplicable, limiting the depth of evaluation possible.

personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Xenophilius is eccentric, credulous, and pedantic - he speaks with authority on fringe beliefs ('Gernumbli gardensi', 'Gurdyroot infusion'), is dismissive of skeptics ('painfully limited. Narrow. Close-minded'), and oscillates between smug certainty and visible terror. His voice is a mix of professorial confidence when discussing his beliefs and querulous panic when cornered. He is 'maddeningly smug' when he thinks he has won an argument, and his speech patterns include rhetorical questions, pedantic corrections, and airy dismissals. Under pressure he becomes shaky, whispering, and physically trembling.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core eccentricity: the candy-floss hair, the nightshirt, the Gurdyroot infusion, the printing press, the Erumpent horn. His dialogue retains the key beats - the smug explanation of the Hallows, the drawing of the symbol, the Peverell connection. His panic and betrayal are also present. However, the film removes his pedantic verbal texture almost entirely: no 'Gernumblies', no extended dismissal of Hermione as 'narrow' and 'close-minded' (reduced to a brief exchange), no 'maddeningly smug' rhetorical victories, no extended haggling over the Resurrection Stone's existence ('Prove that it does not'). His voice is compressed into functional plot delivery.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses the sustained verbal sparring that defines his personality in the Hallows chapter - his smug rhetorical gambits, his condescension toward Hermione, his airy dismissals. The wedding scene is also compressed: his whispered political declaration to Harry ('we at The Quibbler fully supported Dumbledore') replaces the more naturalistic gnome-saliva enthusiasm of the books. The film Xenophilius is recognizable but flatter in voice.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main traits are preserved - eccentricity, belief, panic, betrayal. But the verbal texture that makes him distinctive (pedantry, smugness, rhetorical maneuvering) is substantially reduced. This is a minor-to-moderate simplification of voice, not a transformation. Score in the 18-22 range.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Xenophilius serves two structural functions: (1) exposition vehicle for the Deathly Hallows mythology, and (2) a morally complex obstacle - a man who betrays the protagonists out of desperation to save his daughter. Both functions require active agency: he explains, argues, deceives, and ultimately acts (drawing his wand, firing the Stunning Spell at the Erumpent horn). He also initiates the confrontation by sending an owl to the Ministry while pretending to fetch Luna.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Both functions are preserved in the film. He explains the Hallows, draws the symbol, connects the Peverells, and ultimately betrays the trio by saying Voldemort's name to summon the Death Eaters. He waves from the window ('Stop! I've got him') and is blasted off his feet. His agency as exposition-giver and as betrayer is intact.
narrative_role_agency.difference: One meaningful agency transfer occurs: in the books, Harry deduces Luna's absence himself by noticing the dusty room, the empty wardrobe, the cold bed - and confronts Xenophilius directly ('Where's Luna?'). In the film, this detective beat is removed; instead Hermione notices the Quibbler with Harry's face under the press roller, which triggers the revelation. Harry's active deduction is replaced by a passive discovery. Additionally, the book's extended argument where Xenophilius actively outmaneuvers Hermione rhetorically ('Prove that it does not') is compressed, reducing his agency as intellectual sparring partner.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions are preserved. The agency transfer of Harry's deduction to a passive prop discovery is a moderate loss - it removes a moment where Xenophilius's deception is actively unmasked by Harry rather than accidentally revealed. The rhetorical sparring reduction also slightly diminishes his role as active intellectual obstacle. Score in the 14-17 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Xenophilius's motivation is entirely clear and emotionally coherent: he loves Luna above everything, and the Death Eaters have taken her. He is caught between his public ideology (helping Harry is everyone's duty) and his private terror (he cannot lose Luna). This internal conflict is explicitly shown - he undergoes a 'painful internal struggle', his hands shake, he lies about Luna's whereabouts, and when exposed he whispers 'I cannot lose Luna' with the desperation of a broken man. His betrayal is not villainy but parental desperation.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves this motivation clearly. Xenophilius says directly: 'They took my Luna... But it's really you they want.' The conflict between his public stance and private terror is shown through his evasiveness and the Quibbler cover. His confession is emotionally coherent with the book version.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film compresses the internal struggle - the book's extended sequence of Xenophilius visibly wrestling with himself ('steeling himself', 'painful internal struggle', shaking hands, lying about Luna fishing) is reduced. The film moves more quickly to the revelation. However, the motivation itself is not changed or simplified - it is the same motivation, just less elaborately staged.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified in staging but not in substance. The core conflict (love for Luna vs. public ideology vs. fear) is intact. Per the scoring rules, 'poorly explained does not equal different.' Score in the 11-13 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Xenophilius has a contained arc within Deathly Hallows: he begins as an eccentric true believer and public champion of Harry, is revealed to be a desperate, compromised father who has been repeatedly humiliated by Death Eaters (they rejected his previous attempts to trade information), betrays the trio, is exposed, and ends imprisoned but alive - a figure of pathos rather than villainy. The arc moves from apparent ally to betrayer to broken man, with the revelation that he has been trying and failing to save Luna for weeks.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the broad arc: ally to betrayer to broken man. The key beats are present. However, the detail that Xenophilius had made multiple prior attempts to trade with the Death Eaters (the headdress, the Snorkack proof) - which in the books makes him more pathetic and the Death Eaters more contemptuous - is entirely absent from the film. This detail deepens the arc by showing how far he has already fallen before the trio arrives.
character_arc.difference: The film omits the Death Eaters' dialogue revealing Xenophilius's prior failed attempts ('Remember last week? When you wanted to swap your daughter for that stupid bleeding headdress?'). This removes a layer of the arc - the sense that his betrayal of Harry is not his first desperate act but the latest in a series of humiliations. The arc is present but missing this important step.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but recognizable. The missing prior-attempts detail is a meaningful omission that reduces the arc's emotional depth and the sense of how far Xenophilius has already degraded. Score in the 11-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Xenophilius's defining relationship is with Luna. In the books this is shown through his delight in her ('Luna, my love'), his belief in her gifts (gnome saliva), his Christmas surprise (the Erumpent horn bought as a Snorkack horn for her), and ultimately his total collapse when she is taken. The relationship reveals his capacity for genuine love beneath the eccentricity. His relationship with Harry is also functional: he is simultaneously terrified of and mesmerized by Harry, and his public ideology creates a direct tension with his private cowardice.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The father-daughter relationship is present but thin. Luna appears briefly at the wedding; Xenophilius's love for her is stated ('They took my Luna') but not shown through the texture of their interaction. The book's small moments - his excitement about her gnome bite, the Christmas horn, his delight in her - are mostly absent. The film wedding scene gives him one line about gnome saliva before Luna pulls him away. The relationship with Harry is preserved in its functional tension.
key_relationships.difference: The emotional texture of the Xenophilius-Luna relationship is substantially reduced. In the books, their shared eccentricity and his genuine delight in her are shown across multiple small interactions. In the film, the relationship is asserted (he loves her, she was taken) but not demonstrated through behavior. This weakens the emotional impact of his betrayal, which in the books is grounded in a relationship the reader has seen.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The relationship's dramatic function (his love for Luna drives his betrayal) is preserved, but the emotional dynamic - the warmth, the shared worldview, the small demonstrations of affection - is largely absent. This is a meaningful reduction in relational texture. Score in the 5-6 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Xenophilius is a genuinely complex minor character. He is simultaneously ridiculous and sympathetic, a true believer and a coward, a public idealist and a private hypocrite. His complexity comes from: (1) the sustained rhetorical sparring with Hermione that shows his intelligence within his eccentricity; (2) the extended deception sequence showing his internal struggle; (3) the Death Eaters' revelation of his prior failed attempts, which reframes him as a broken man rather than a simple traitor; (4) the small domestic details (the headdress, the Erumpent horn as a Christmas gift, the Gurdyroot infusion) that build texture; (5) Ron and Hermione's contrasting reactions to him ('cowardly old wart' vs. 'I'd feel so sorry for him').
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the broad contradiction (idealist/coward) and the domestic eccentricity (nightshirt, printing press, Gurdyroot). It loses the rhetorical sparring, the prior failed attempts, the Christmas horn detail, and the extended deception sequence. The characters' contrasting reactions to him are also absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film Xenophilius is recognizable but flattened. The material that makes him more than a plot device - his intellectual engagement, his pathetic prior attempts, the warmth of his relationship with Luna, the moral complexity of his position - is substantially reduced. What remains is functional but not rich.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is a noticeable simplification. Important nuances are missing: the prior failed attempts (which reframe his arc), the rhetorical sparring (which shows his intelligence), the Luna relationship texture (which grounds his motivation emotionally). The character is not reduced to a single trait, but the material that made him genuinely complex is largely gone. Score in the 8-10 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's active deduction of Luna's absence (noticing dusty room, empty wardrobe, cold bed) transferred to passive prop discovery (Hermione finds the Quibbler under the press roller)
Extended rhetorical sparring between Xenophilius and Hermione, including 'Prove that it does not' and 'painfully limited, narrow, close-minded'
Death Eaters' revelation of Xenophilius's prior failed attempts to trade (the headdress, the Snorkack proof) - removed entirely
Xenophilius's extended internal struggle before agreeing to help (shaking hands, steeling himself, painful visible conflict)
The Erumpent horn as a Christmas gift for Luna, bought in good faith as a Snorkack horn - removed
Small domestic moments establishing the Xenophilius-Luna relationship (gnome bite delight, 'Luna my love', shared belief system)
Ron and Hermione's contrasting post-escape reactions ('cowardly old wart' vs. 'I'd feel so sorry for him')
Xenophilius actively drawing his wand and firing the Stunning Spell at the Erumpent horn (in the film the teapot strikes the horn by accident from a ricocheting spell)
Viktor Krum's confrontation with Xenophilius over the Grindelwald symbol at the wedding - entirely absent from the film
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function or act of agency is transferred to another character): Harry's deduction of Luna's absence is transferred to a passive prop discovery. Cap considered but total score falls below 75 naturally.
Cap 2 (score above 80 if key relationships change substantially): The Xenophilius-Luna relationship is substantially reduced in emotional texture. Total score kept below 80.
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Xenophilius's core identity - eccentric true believer, desperate father, reluctant betrayer - and his two main narrative functions (Hallows exposition, moral obstacle) are intact. The portrayal is recognizable and broadly faithful. However, the film loses the material that made him genuinely complex: the sustained rhetorical sparring that showed his intelligence, the prior failed attempts that deepened his arc, the domestic warmth of his relationship with Luna, and Harry's active unmasking of his deception. The Krum confrontation at the wedding - which established the Deathly Hallows symbol as genuinely ambiguous and Xenophilius as unwittingly provocative - is entirely absent. The result is a character who functions correctly in the plot but lacks the texture, pathos, and intellectual engagement of his book counterpart.

personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Kingsley is defined by a consistent set of observable traits: a deep, slow, calming voice; physical composure and deliberateness; measured authority that commands respect without aggression; dry wit and quiet confidence; and a capacity for strategic deception (feeding false information to the Ministry about Sirius, modifying Marietta's memory under pressure). He is not verbose. His interventions are brief, purposeful, and often carry weight disproportionate to their length. He expresses concern and care (his comment about Harry not being made prefect, his insistence on staying to protect Harry over the Prime Minister) but always through understatement. He is calm in crisis, reassuring without being soft.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core vocal and physical identity: deep voice, physical presence, composure. His line 'You're more important' (DH1) and his calm challenge to Umbridge ('You want to calm yourself, Madam Umbridge') both reflect the book's register. His brief Patronus message at the wedding is tonally accurate. In the Battle of Hogwarts he is present and active. The OotP scene where he winks at Mr. Weasley and passes The Quibbler is cut, removing a moment of dry humor and covert intelligence.
personality_voice.difference: The film preserves the dominant surface traits - voice, composure, authority - but loses the texture of his dry wit and the quiet strategic intelligence shown in the Ministry cubicle scene (the wink, the magazine, the performance of bureaucratic normalcy). His brief but pointed opinion about Harry not being made prefect (book OotP) is absent, removing a moment that shows he thinks independently and cares about Harry's standing. These are secondary traits, not core ones.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main personality is preserved. The losses are real but secondary: wit, independent opinion, covert cleverness. The dominant traits - calm authority, deep measured voice, composure under pressure - are intact across both OotP and DH film appearances. Minor penalty for loss of secondary texture.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Kingsley serves multiple active functions across the books: covert intelligence operative (feeding false Sirius information to the Ministry), protector of the Muggle Prime Minister, active combatant in the DoM battle and Battle of Hogwarts, early warning system at the wedding (Patronus), battle coordinator at Hogwarts (Room of Requirement speech), and ultimately the first post-war Minister of Magic. He initiates, decides, and acts independently across all these roles. In OotP he modifies Marietta's memory under pressure - a covert act of agency that Dumbledore explicitly credits him for. He is also the one who challenges Fudge's order to arrest Dumbledore by being hexed rather than complying, which Dumbledore frames as 'remarkably quick on the uptake.'
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves his role as protector (DH1 arrival, 'You're more important'), active combatant (DoM, Hogwarts), and Patronus warning at the wedding. His Room of Requirement battle coordination speech is present in DH2 and is a direct book transfer. His post-war appointment as Minister is referenced in the epilogue context. However, his memory modification of Marietta is absent from OotP - the film does not show this act of covert agency at all. His covert intelligence role (feeding false Sirius information) is mentioned in dialogue by Mr. Weasley in the book but has no film equivalent.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The memory modification of Marietta - his most significant individual act of covert agency in OotP - is absent. His intelligence role feeding false information about Sirius is not shown or referenced in the films. These are not transferred to another character; they are simply removed. His battle coordination role in DH2 is preserved. His Patronus warning is preserved.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The loss of the Marietta memory modification removes a specific act of independent agency that Dumbledore explicitly credits. The loss of the Sirius intelligence operation removes a key function. These are not transferred but simply absent. His remaining agency (combatant, protector, coordinator, Patronus warning) is substantial and preserved. Moderate penalty.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Kingsley's motivations are consistent and clear: loyalty to Dumbledore and the Order, protection of the innocent (both wizarding and Muggle), and opposition to Voldemort and Ministry corruption. He is willing to operate covertly within the Ministry while working against it. His choice to leave the Prime Minister to protect Harry ('You're more important') shows a clear hierarchy of loyalties. There is no significant internal conflict in the books - he is a stable, committed figure. His motivations are simple but not shallow: they are grounded in principle (his Potterwatch speech about 'every human life is worth the same').
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves his core motivations: loyalty to Harry and the Order, willingness to act against Ministry authority, protection of the innocent. His 'You're more important' line is retained. His Potterwatch speech is absent from the films entirely, which removes the most explicit articulation of his principled motivation.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The Potterwatch segment - where Kingsley explicitly articulates his moral framework ('one short step from Wizards first to Purebloods first to Death Eaters... every human life is worth the same') - is absent from the films. This is the clearest statement of his internal values in the books. Its absence means his motivations are shown through action but not articulated. They remain inferrable but less explicit.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but still correct - they are shown through action (protecting Harry, fighting, coordinating) even if not articulated. The Potterwatch absence is a loss of explicit motivation but not a change or contradiction. Mild penalty for underexplanation.

character_arc.book_baseline: Kingsley has a flat arc in the books - he is consistently competent, loyal, and principled from his first appearance to his appointment as Minister of Magic. The arc is not one of internal change but of escalating responsibility: Order member → covert Ministry operative → active combatant → battle coordinator → Minister of Magic. The endpoint (Minister) is the culmination of his trajectory and represents the wizarding world's recognition of his qualities.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the trajectory: Order member → protector → combatant → (implied) post-war authority. His appointment as Minister is not explicitly shown in the films but is consistent with his portrayal. The escalating responsibility is visible across OotP and DH. The flat arc is preserved.
character_arc.difference: The Minister of Magic appointment is not explicitly shown in the films (it is referenced in the book epilogue context but not dramatized in the films). The covert Ministry operative phase (feeding false Sirius information) is absent, which removes one step of the escalating responsibility arc.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is flat and intentionally so - no internal transformation is expected. The escalating responsibility is mostly preserved. The missing covert operative phase is a compression, not a distortion. The arc remains recognizable. Minor penalty for missing step.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Kingsley's key relationships in the books are: (1) Dumbledore - deep loyalty and trust, Dumbledore explicitly credits his quick thinking; (2) the Order collectively - collegial, professional, mutual respect; (3) Harry - protective concern, independent advocacy (prefect comment), willingness to prioritize Harry over official duties; (4) Mr. Weasley - professional cover relationship at the Ministry, covert cooperation; (5) the Muggle Prime Minister - professional protector relationship that reveals his ability to operate across worlds.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the Order collegiality and the protective relationship with Harry ('You're more important'). The Dumbledore relationship is implied through his presence and loyalty but not developed. The Mr. Weasley Ministry relationship is absent. The Prime Minister relationship is absent from the films entirely (the HBP chapter with the Prime Minister is not adapted).
key_relationships.difference: The Prime Minister relationship - which in the books reveals Kingsley's unique ability to operate in both worlds and his professional competence recognized by Muggles - is entirely absent. The Mr. Weasley covert cooperation at the Ministry is absent. The Dumbledore relationship is present only implicitly. The Harry relationship is preserved in its protective dimension but loses the independent advocacy dimension (prefect comment).
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Prime Minister relationship is a meaningful loss - it is the only relationship that shows Kingsley operating outside the wizarding world and being recognized for his competence by Muggles. Its absence removes a dimension of his characterization. The Mr. Weasley relationship loss is less significant. The Harry and Order relationships are preserved in their essential form.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Kingsley's complexity in the books comes from several sources: (1) his ability to operate as a double agent within the Ministry while remaining loyal to the Order - a form of moral courage that requires sustained deception; (2) his covert act of modifying Marietta's memory under pressure, credited by Dumbledore; (3) his Potterwatch role as a voice of moral clarity and resistance under occupation; (4) his independent opinions (the prefect comment shows he thinks critically about Dumbledore's decisions); (5) his recognition by Muggles (the Prime Minister relationship); (6) his dry humor (the wink and Quibbler scene). He is not a simple hero - he is a strategist who operates in moral grey zones when necessary.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves his composure, authority, and loyalty. His one notable film-specific moment of wit ('You may not like him, Minister, but you can't deny Dumbledore has got style') is actually a good addition that fits his book voice. However, the double-agent complexity, the memory modification, the Potterwatch moral articulation, the Prime Minister relationship, and the independent critical opinion about Dumbledore are all absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses the material that makes Kingsley more than a loyal, competent Order member: his willingness to operate deceptively within corrupt institutions, his capacity for covert action under pressure, his explicit moral framework, and his recognition across both worlds. What remains is a faithful but simplified version - the surface is right but the depth is reduced.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The simplification is real and affects the audience's understanding of who Kingsley is. He becomes a reliable, impressive supporting figure rather than a strategically complex one. The losses are not transferred to other characters - they are simply absent. Noticeable simplification of important nuances.

lost_or_transferred_material: Kingsley's covert intelligence operation feeding false information about Sirius to the Ministry (OotP book, mentioned by Mr. Weasley)
Kingsley modifying Marietta Edgecombe's memory during the Dumbledore arrest scene - credited by Dumbledore as 'remarkably quick on the uptake' (OotP book)
Kingsley's Potterwatch role and his explicit moral articulation: 'one short step from Wizards first to Purebloods first to Death Eaters... every human life is worth the same' (DH book)
Kingsley's independent critical opinion about Dumbledore not making Harry a prefect (OotP book)
The Muggle Prime Minister relationship - Kingsley's undercover assignment, the PM's trust in him, and his ability to operate credibly in the Muggle world (HBP book)
The Ministry cubicle scene: Kingsley's dry wit, the wink, passing The Quibbler to Mr. Weasley for Sirius (OotP book)
Kingsley's appointment as temporary Minister of Magic explicitly stated (DH book epilogue context - not dramatized in films)
Kingsley visiting the Dursleys with Mr. Weasley to explain the threat and the Dursleys' specific trust in him (DH book)
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function is transferred or removed): Kingsley's covert agency (Marietta memory modification, Ministry double-agent role) and his Potterwatch moral articulation are removed without transfer. Score kept at 76, which is at the boundary - no further cap needed as the total naturally falls within range.
comparative.key_observations: The films preserve Kingsley's most visible traits - his deep calming voice, physical composure, authority, loyalty to Harry and the Order, and his role as combatant and coordinator - with reasonable fidelity. The surface characterization is accurate. However, the films lose the material that gives him depth: his double-agent operation within the Ministry, his covert act of modifying Marietta's memory (his most significant individual act of agency in OotP), his Potterwatch role as a voice of principled resistance, his relationship with the Muggle Prime Minister, and his dry strategic wit. What remains is a faithful but flattened version - recognizable and tonally correct, but missing the complexity that makes him more than a reliable, impressive background figure. The one film-specific addition ('You may not like him, Minister, but you can't deny Dumbledore has got style') is well-calibrated to his book voice and partially compensates for lost wit.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Sprout is brisk, cheerful, practical, and unsentimental. She is competent and confident in her domain, speaks directly and without fuss, and has a dry matter-of-factness about unpleasant tasks (e.g., describing Mandrake pus as 'extremely valuable' without squeamishness, slapping the Venomous Tentacula without ceremony). She is warm but not soft — she awards points efficiently, corrects students briskly ('Quite enough chat over here!'), and shows quiet solidarity with colleagues against Umbridge (exchanging significant glances with McGonagall). In the Battle of Hogwarts she is grimly determined and even darkly humorous ('Tentacula, Devil's Snare. And Snargaluff pods... yes, I'd like to see the Death Eaters fighting those'). Her emotional register is understated: she expresses care through action rather than sentiment.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film's Sprout in CoS is brisk and practical in the Mandrake lesson — she demonstrates without hesitation, awards points efficiently, and her line about leaving the fainted Neville ('Very well. We'll just leave him then') captures her unsentimental tone. In DH Part 2 she delivers the single line 'That doesn't mean we can't hold him up' which is faithful in spirit. Her physical description (squat, patched hat, flyaway hair, earthy) is preserved visually.
personality_voice.difference: The film preserves the surface personality well in the scenes it includes. However, the full range of her voice — the dry humor about bubotuber pus, the pointed solidarity against Umbridge, the quiet pride in Neville, the darkly gleeful battle muttering — is absent simply because those scenes are not filmed. The film Sprout is recognizable but narrower. The CoS scene omits her scowl at Lockhart closing the greenhouse door in her face, which in the book shows her quiet assertiveness and irritation at being sidelined.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality traits that are shown are faithful. The narrowing is due to scene omission rather than mischaracterization of what is shown. Minor penalty for the loss of the Lockhart-scowl moment (her assertiveness being sidelined without reaction) and the absence of her dry humor register across later books. Score sits in the 18-22 range.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for CoS and DH2 film scenes; reasonably inferred for book patterns from repeated corpus evidence.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Sprout serves several distinct narrative functions: (1) she is the Herbology expert whose Mandrakes are the literal mechanism for curing the Petrified victims in CoS — she controls the timeline of the cure and is the only one trusted to tend them; (2) she is a quiet institutional resister against Umbridge in OotP; (3) she is Cedric Diggory's Head of House and the person who comforts his parents after his death; (4) she advocates for keeping Hogwarts open after Dumbledore's death; (5) in the Battle of Hogwarts she takes active command, leading a group with weaponized plants and contributing meaningfully to the defense. She initiates, decides, and acts across multiple books.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In CoS, her role as Mandrake cultivator is preserved — Dumbledore credits her and Madam Pomfrey at the feast. In DH2, she delivers one line of resolve before McGonagall takes over. The Cedric/Diggory function, the Umbridge resistance, and the detailed battle command (leading fighters to a tower per Kingsley's plan) are absent from the corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Mandrake cure role is acknowledged but her agency in it is reduced — in the film, Hagrid references her authority over the Mandrakes' progress (Film Scene 2: 'According to Professor Sprout, they still got a bit of growing up to do'), which preserves her as the expert but removes her direct on-screen management. Her battle role is reduced to a single reactive line rather than the active command shown in the books. Her role comforting the Diggorys is entirely absent. Her institutional resistance to Umbridge is absent.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Her core narrative function (Mandrake cure) is preserved in acknowledgment if not in direct agency. Her battle role is present but compressed to near-decoration. The Diggory function and Umbridge resistance are lost. This is a meaningful reduction in agency across the series, though what remains is not misrepresented. Score in the 14-17 range.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what appears in the film corpus; reasonably inferred for book functions from corpus.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Sprout's motivations are consistent and simple: professional dedication to Herbology and her students, loyalty to Hogwarts as an institution, and care for her Hufflepuff house. She is motivated by practical competence — she wants things done properly. Her advocacy for keeping Hogwarts open after Dumbledore's death ('I feel that if a single pupil wants to come, then the school ought to remain open for that pupil') reveals a deep institutional loyalty. There is no significant internal conflict in the books — she is a stable, grounded character.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film does not contradict her motivations. Her Mandrake work implies professional dedication. Her DH2 line implies institutional loyalty and resolve. However, her motivations are never articulated or dramatized — they are implied at best.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: Motivations are not changed, but they are almost entirely unexpressed. The film gives no scene that dramatizes her care for students, her institutional loyalty, or her professional pride. These are present in the books through repeated small moments (fitting socks on Mandrakes herself, telling Harry the Mandrakes are nearly ready, advocating for Hogwarts at the staff meeting).
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Per the scoring rules, 'poorly explained ≠ different.' Her motivations are not contradicted. The score reflects that they are present by implication but never dramatized, placing this in the 11-13 range (simplified but still correct).
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from film; directly evidenced in books.

character_arc.book_baseline: Sprout has a flat arc — she is consistent across all seven books. She begins as a competent, cheerful Herbology teacher and ends as a loyal defender of Hogwarts. There is no transformation. Her arc is intentionally flat and that is appropriate to her role.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film also presents a flat arc — she appears in CoS as a competent teacher and in DH2 as a loyal defender. The flatness is preserved.
character_arc.difference: The arc is flat in both books and films. Per the scoring rules, a flat arc faithfully preserved scores full or near-full points. The only minor issue is that the film's flat arc is thinner — fewer data points — but the trajectory is the same.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Flat arc faithfully preserved. Minor deduction only because the film's version is so sparse that the arc is more assumed than demonstrated. Score in the 11-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Sprout's most significant relationships are: (1) collegial solidarity with McGonagall and Flitwick (especially against Umbridge and in the Battle); (2) a warm, encouraging relationship with Neville Longbottom — she tells Moody he is excellent at Herbology, she is proud of him, and he leads her plant-weapon charge in the Battle; (3) a professional rivalry/irritation with Lockhart in CoS; (4) a pastoral relationship with Hufflepuff students, most notably Cedric Diggory.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Lockhart dynamic is partially present in CoS — she is shown as disgruntled when he closes the greenhouse door on her, though her scowl is not explicitly shown in the film corpus (it is in the book but the film scene does show her being sidelined). The McGonagall solidarity is present in DH2 in the exchange of resolve. The Neville relationship is entirely absent from the film corpus. The Cedric/Hufflepuff pastoral role is absent.
key_relationships.difference: The Neville relationship — one of the most textured in the books — is completely absent from the film corpus. The Lockhart dynamic is compressed. The McGonagall solidarity is present but thin.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The loss of the Neville relationship is significant — it is the most individualized relationship Sprout has in the books and it reveals her warmth and perceptiveness. Its absence weakens the emotional texture of her character. Score in the 7-8 range.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what appears; reasonably inferred for absences.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Sprout's complexity comes from accumulated small moments: her dry humor about unpleasant tasks, her quiet assertiveness when Lockhart sidelines her, her pointed solidarity with colleagues against Umbridge (awarding Harry 20 points when forbidden to comment on his interview), her pride in Neville, her darkly gleeful battle preparation, her calm institutional voice at the post-Dumbledore staff meeting. She is not a simple background figure — she has texture, humor, and moral clarity.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the surface competence and the broad strokes of her battle resolve. It loses the Umbridge resistance entirely, the Neville pride, the Lockhart irritation (partially), the bubotuber humor, the post-Dumbledore staff meeting advocacy, and the darkly gleeful battle muttering (the 'Tentacula, Devil's Snare' line is absent from the film corpus).
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film Sprout is recognizable but significantly flattened. The moments that give her texture — humor, quiet resistance, pastoral care, institutional voice — are mostly absent. What remains is competent teacher + loyal defender, which is accurate but thin.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Noticeable simplification. The character is not misrepresented but is reduced to her broadest functions. Important nuances (Umbridge resistance, Neville relationship, dark humor) are missing. Score in the 8-10 range.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for absences via corpus comparison.

lost_or_transferred_material: Sprout's quiet resistance to Umbridge (awarding Harry 20 points when forbidden to comment on his interview — OotP)
Sprout's encouraging relationship with Neville Longbottom, including telling Moody he excels at Herbology
Neville leading Sprout's plant-weapon charge in the Battle of Hogwarts
Sprout's darkly humorous battle preparation line ('Tentacula, Devil's Snare. And Snargaluff pods... yes, I'd like to see the Death Eaters fighting those')
Sprout comforting the Diggory family after Cedric's death (GoF)
Sprout's advocacy for keeping Hogwarts open at the post-Dumbledore staff meeting (HBP)
Sprout's scowl and assertiveness when Lockhart closes the greenhouse door in her face (CoS)
Sprout's dry humor about bubotuber pus and practical unsentimental classroom voice across multiple books
Sprout's significant glance with McGonagall during Umbridge's opening speech (OotP)
comparative.key_observations: Sprout's film portrayal is faithful in what it shows but significantly reduced in texture. The CoS Mandrake scene captures her brisk competence and unsentimental manner accurately, and her DH2 line of resolve is in character. However, the film loses almost all of the material that makes her more than a functional background teacher: her quiet institutional resistance, her warm relationship with Neville, her darkly humorous battle preparation, and her voice in the post-Dumbledore staff meeting. The result is a recognizable but flattened version — correct in broad strokes, thin in the details that gave her book characterization its texture. No score caps are triggered because the remaining material is genuinely faithful and her role, while reduced, is not transferred to another character.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Ernie is pompous, self-important, and prone to dramatic pronouncements delivered with great solemnity. He speaks in long, structured arguments, drops his voice for theatrical effect, puffs out his chest, and positions himself as an authority. He is also genuinely loyal, publicly courageous when it matters (his OotP declaration to Harry), and capable of formal, dignified apology. His speech is verbose, deliberate, and self-conscious. He has a recurring pattern of moral posturing combined with real anxiety when his own status or safety is threatened (hesitating to sign the DA list because he is a prefect). He is not cruel, but he is stubborn and slow to revise his views.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of Ernie's pompous, conspiratorial voice almost verbatim in the CoS library scene. His whispering darkly, his logical chain of accusations, his solemnity with Hannah, and his theatrical delivery are all present. The film corpus does not include his OotP scenes (the DA signing hesitation, the 'I personally believe you one hundred per cent' speech, the OWL revision boasting, the Dumbledore's Army fight on the train), so those dimensions of his personality are absent from the film corpus provided.
personality_voice.difference: The film captures the pompous, conspiratorial, self-important register faithfully in the one substantial scene it gives him. What is absent from the film corpus is the full range: the formal apology, the prefect-status anxiety, the chest-puffing loyalty declaration, the OWL obsession, the satisfaction at Malfoy's comeuppance. The film Ernie is recognizable but only one facet of the book Ernie is shown.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The scene that is present is highly faithful in voice and personality. The penalty is modest because the absent material is not contradicted - the film does not make Ernie different, it simply shows less of him. However, the reduction does remove secondary traits (loyalty, formal courage, prefect anxiety, pompous pride) that are part of his book texture.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for the CoS scene; reasonably inferred for the absence of later personality dimensions based on what the film corpus does not contain.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Ernie serves several distinct narrative functions across the books: (1) in CoS, he is the voice of the school's suspicion of Harry, driving the social pressure subplot and then formally resolving it with his apology; (2) in GoF, he is a minor social marker of Hufflepuff loyalty to Cedric; (3) in OotP, he is an active DA member, a prefect who publicly endorses Harry at a moment of social isolation, and a participant in the train ambush; (4) in HBP, he is a recurring Potions classmate whose actions (asking about Felix Felicis, using Specialis Revelio, competing with Harry) serve as narrative texture; (5) in DH, he stands up in the Great Hall to demand the right to fight, and casts a Patronus to save Harry from dementors.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows Ernie only in the CoS library scene and briefly as a background figure departing for the holidays. His CoS function as the voice of suspicion is preserved. His later functions - DA member, public endorser of Harry, Patronus caster, Great Hall defiance - are absent from the film corpus entirely.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film preserves his CoS narrative role. His OotP and DH agency - casting a Patronus to save Harry, standing up to demand the right to fight, publicly endorsing Harry against Umbridge's regime - are not present in the film corpus. These are active, plot-relevant moments of agency.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The CoS role is intact. The later agency losses are real: the Patronus scene and the Great Hall stand are meaningful acts of courage and loyalty that define Ernie beyond his early pomposity. Their absence reduces his narrative function from a recurring minor agent to a one-scene suspicious Hufflepuff. However, the film corpus provided may simply not include those later films' scenes rather than the films having removed them entirely - confidence is medium.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for CoS. Not enough evidence in the film corpus to confirm whether OotP/DH scenes were filmed or cut.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Ernie's motivations are consistent: he is driven by a desire to be seen as authoritative, correct, and morally serious. In CoS, his suspicion of Harry is motivated by genuine fear for his Hufflepuff housemates and a need to be the one who figured it out. His internal conflict is visible when Harry confronts him - he is trembling but stubborn, unwilling to back down even when frightened. In OotP, his hesitation to sign the DA list reveals a real conflict between his belief in the cause and his investment in his prefect status. His apology in CoS is formal and dignified, suggesting he takes his own moral standing seriously enough to correct himself publicly.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film shows his CoS motivation (fear for Justin, suspicion of Harry as Dark wizard) faithfully.
