His acknowledgment that Dumbledore 'will always remain a riddle even to me' gestures at the limits of his knowledge but frames it as philosophical acceptance rather than painful protective silence.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The internal conflict - knowing more than he admits, being caught between loyalty and honesty - is softened in the film. The book signals this through Doge's physical distress and evasive reactions; the film replaces this with a more serene acknowledgment of mystery. The motivation itself (loyalty) is intact.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivation is correct and present. The internal conflict is simplified rather than absent - the film acknowledges Doge's incomplete knowledge but removes the discomfort around it. This is a simplification, not a change, placing the score in the 11-13 range.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for motivation; reasonably inferred for the softening of internal conflict

character_arc.book_baseline: Doge has no meaningful arc across the series. He is a minor character who appears briefly in OotP (introduction), is referenced in a photograph, writes the obituary, appears at the wedding, and is mentioned by Aberforth. His characterization is essentially static: loyal friend of Dumbledore, member of the Order, defender of Dumbledore's reputation. The wedding scene is his most developed appearance and does not change him - he ends it as he began, loyal and distressed. His arc is intentionally flat.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film also presents a flat arc. Doge appears in one scene, is consistent throughout, and exits. No arc is invented or implied.
character_arc.difference: No meaningful difference. Both book and film present a static character.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Per the scoring rules, a flat arc in the books that is also flat in the films scores full or near-full points. Minor compression of the wedding scene means one step of his 'arc' (the extended Muriel confrontation that shows the limits of his loyalty) is reduced, but this is a nuance issue addressed elsewhere. Score: 13.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Two relationships matter. First, Doge and Dumbledore: a lifelong friendship of near-devotion, established in the obituary as beginning in shared social vulnerability. Doge's entire characterization is downstream of this relationship. Second, Doge and Muriel: an adversarial dynamic in which Muriel systematically dismantles Doge's defenses with gossip and social aggression, and Doge is repeatedly defeated. This dynamic is narratively important because it shows the limits of loyal friendship as a source of truth.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Doge-Dumbledore relationship is preserved in tone and function - the obituary is referenced, the long friendship is established, and Doge's devotion is clear. The Doge-Muriel dynamic is present but compressed: Muriel gets two or three exchanges with Doge rather than the extended assault of the book. The power imbalance (Muriel winning, Doge losing) is still visible but less pronounced.
key_relationships.difference: The Doge-Muriel dynamic is compressed enough that Doge's repeated defeat is less visible. In the book, Muriel overrides him on Ariana, on Kendra, on Aberforth's assault at the funeral - each time Doge is more distressed and less able to respond. In the film, the exchange is brief and Doge holds his composure better. The emotional texture of that relationship is partially lost.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Doge-Dumbledore relationship is faithful. The Doge-Muriel dynamic is present but its emotional function (showing the inadequacy of loyalty as a defense against truth) is weakened by compression. Score: 7.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book's Doge is more complex than a simple 'loyal old friend' because of several specific nuances: (1) his physical distress signals that he knows more than he says, creating a gap between loyalty and honesty; (2) his repeated defeat by Muriel across a long scene shows the limits of his worldview; (3) his obituary, read in full, reveals a man capable of genuine literary warmth and careful omission simultaneously; (4) Aberforth's dismissal of him ('that old berk... thought the sun shone out of my [Albus's backside]') adds an external perspective that complicates the reader's view of Doge's devotion as possibly naive or even enabling.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the warmth and the loyalty. It loses the extended Muriel confrontation that shows Doge being systematically defeated. It loses the physical distress signals (wretched look, trembling lips, verge of tears across multiple exchanges). It loses Aberforth's dismissal entirely (Aberforth does not appear in this context in the film corpus). It adds the philosophical composure that actually makes Doge seem more self-aware and less naively devoted than the book presents.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film removes the material that makes Doge's loyalty complicated - the signs of selective silence, the repeated defeat, the external critique from Aberforth. What remains is a warmer, wiser, more composed version that is recognizable but flatter.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The loss of the extended Muriel confrontation and Aberforth's dismissal removes two of the three main sources of complexity in Doge's characterization. The added philosophical composure partially compensates by giving him texture, but it is a different kind of texture - one that simplifies rather than complicates his loyalty. Score: 9.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for losses; directly evidenced for the added composure

lost_or_transferred_material: Extended Muriel confrontation covering Ariana, Kendra, and Aberforth's assault at the funeral - reduced to a brief exchange
Doge's repeated physical distress signals (trembling lips, verge of tears, wretched look, radish-colored face) across multiple defensive failures
Doge's 'drowning man' desperation when Bathilda is mentioned - signals selective protective silence
Aberforth's dismissal of Doge as 'that old berk' who 'thought the sun shone out of' Dumbledore - external critique of Doge's devotion as naive
Doge's defensive interjections correcting specific claims about Ariana and Kendra ('Ariana was not a Squib!', 'She never pretended anything of the sort!')
Film adds philosophical composure and reflective wisdom to Doge (owls in distress, 'he will always remain a riddle') not present in book version of this character
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 considered (transferred actions): no core action is transferred from Doge to another character - Muriel's revelations are Muriel's in both versions. Cap not applied.

Cap 1 considered (major complexity removed): the loss of the extended Muriel confrontation and Aberforth's dismissal removes significant complexity. Raw dimension scores already reflect this; total of 72 is consistent with cap 1 threshold of 85 not being breached.
comparative.key_observations: The film's Doge is recognizable and preserves the core traits - loyalty, warmth, nervousness, defensiveness toward Muriel - along with several verbatim lines. The main fidelity loss is the compression of the Muriel confrontation, which in the book is a sustained scene that shows Doge being repeatedly defeated and signals the limits of his loyal worldview. The film also adds a philosophical composure and reflective eloquence that softens his emotional volatility and makes him seem wiser and more self-aware than the book presents. Aberforth's external critique of Doge's devotion as naive is entirely absent. The result is a faithful but simplified version: the broad identity is intact, but the nuances that make his loyalty complicated - the selective silence, the repeated defeat, the external skepticism - are largely lost.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Fred is defined by irreverent humor, mischief, and a sharp wit deployed with confidence and timing. He teases Percy relentlessly, mocks authority without fear, uses deadpan delivery and comic escalation (the 'Once / Twice / A minute / All summer' exchange about Percy's prefect badge), and shows genuine warmth beneath the jokes. He is bold, entrepreneurial, and emotionally present in crisis — he falls silent and goes pale when George is injured, and his grief at Fred's own death scene is foreshadowed by his rare moments of stillness. His voice is characterized by short, punchy lines, mock-formality, and a consistent refusal to be cowed. He also shows real courage and moral clarity: confronting Sirius about risking their father's life, defending Harry against Umbridge, and choosing to leave Hogwarts on his own terms.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve Fred's core comedic identity well. The King's Cross twin-swap joke (Film Scene 1), the Marauder's Map handover with theatrical flair (Film Scenes 9-10), the Aging Potion attempt with gleeful confidence (Film Scene 13), the Polyjuice banter ('imagine if something went wrong and we were stuck as scrawny, specky gits forever' — Film Scene 21), and the George ear-wound exchange ('With the whole wide world of ear-related humor at your disposal, you go for holey? Pathetic.' — Film Scene 22) all faithfully reproduce his voice. His short, punchy delivery and mock-solemnity are intact. The 'Nice night for it!' line during the Battle of Hogwarts (Film Scene 24) is a direct book echo.
personality_voice.difference: The films compress Fred and George into a near-indistinguishable unit more than the books do. In the books, Fred has slightly more individual voice — he is more often the initiator, the one who speaks first, the one who escalates. The films distribute lines between them more interchangeably. Several of Fred's most distinctive solo moments are absent: his deadpan Percy-badge escalation, his serious confrontation with Sirius at Grimmauld Place ('Easy for you to say, stuck here!'), and his emotional weight during the Weasley family's worst moments (waiting for news of Arthur, the long night at Grimmauld Place). His mock-formality and solo wit are slightly flattened into a double-act.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality — irreverent, bold, warm beneath the jokes, entrepreneurially driven — is preserved with strong fidelity. The compression into a twin unit and loss of some solo voice moments warrants a minor penalty. The most important traits, flaws, and tone are intact.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Fred serves multiple narrative functions: comic relief, moral counterweight to authority (Percy, Umbridge), plot enabler (Marauder's Map, Extendable Ears, Skiving Snackboxes, Potterwatch), emotional anchor for the Weasley family, and active agent in the resistance against Umbridge. He initiates the Marauder's Map gift, organizes the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes business, leads the fireworks rebellion, confronts Sirius directly about the Order's secrecy, and volunteers for the Seven Potters mission. He is not passive — he drives scenes and changes outcomes.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his most iconic agency moments: the Marauder's Map handover (Film Scenes 9-10), the Aging Potion attempt (Film Scene 13), the fireworks rebellion (Film Scene 19), the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes tour (Film Scene 20), and the Seven Potters transformation (Film Scene 21). His 'Give her hell from us, Peeves' departure is retained in spirit through the fireworks scene. He volunteers for the Seven Potters mission and participates in the Battle of Hogwarts.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several significant agency moments are absent or reduced. His confrontation with Sirius at Grimmauld Place ('Easy for you to say, stuck here! I don't see you risking your neck!') — a moment where Fred challenges an adult Order member directly and articulates the family's fear — is cut. His role in Potterwatch (broadcasting as 'Rapier') is present in the book corpus but absent from the film corpus provided. His active role in organizing the DA meeting logistics (buying Butterbeers, signing the parchment first) is reduced. The Extendable Ears subplot, which gives Fred and George genuine intelligence-gathering agency, is compressed. Some of his plot-enabling functions (Skiving Snackboxes enabling Umbridge's classroom chaos) are shown but his authorship of the chaos is less foregrounded.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core agency is preserved — he initiates, enables, and acts. But the loss of the Sirius confrontation removes a moment where Fred exercises moral agency against an authority figure on behalf of his family, and the Potterwatch role (a significant narrative function in book 7) is absent from the film corpus. These are meaningful reductions.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Fred's motivations are layered: genuine love of mischief and humor as a worldview, entrepreneurial ambition (the joke shop is a serious life goal, not just a hobby), loyalty to family and friends, and a principled resistance to authority he considers unjust or hypocritical. His internal conflict is subtle — he genuinely worries about his mother's approval, feels the weight of the family's financial precarity, and in crisis moments (Arthur's injury, George's wounding) drops the humor entirely, revealing deep emotional investment beneath the performance.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films show his entrepreneurial drive clearly (Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes, Film Scene 20), his loyalty (Seven Potters, Battle of Hogwarts), and his humor as a genuine worldview rather than a mask. The George ear-wound scene (Film Scene 22) shows the emotional depth beneath the jokes. His resistance to Umbridge is shown as principled, not merely mischievous.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films do not show the financial motivation behind the joke shop as clearly — in the books, Fred and George's awareness of the family's poverty and their desire to provide for themselves is explicit. The conflict with Mrs. Weasley over their career choice (she wants them in the Ministry) is present in the book corpus but largely absent from the films. The scene where Fred defends giving Dudley the Ton-Tongue Toffee ('We gave it to him because he's a great bullying git') — showing his moral reasoning — is absent from the film corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but not wrong. The entrepreneurial drive and loyalty are intact. The financial dimension and the family-career conflict are underexplained but not contradicted. Minor simplification warrants a modest penalty.

character_arc.book_baseline: Fred's arc moves from mischievous schoolboy to serious entrepreneur and resistance fighter. Key stages: establishing the joke shop ambition (books 4-5), leaving Hogwarts on his own terms as an act of principled defiance (book 5), opening Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes (book 6), joining the Order's resistance and Potterwatch (book 7), and dying in the Battle of Hogwarts mid-laugh — his death is the culmination of a life lived defiantly joyful. The arc is not one of internal transformation but of escalating commitment to his values.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films track the major arc beats: the joke shop ambition is established, the Hogwarts departure is shown (Film Scene 19), the shop is seen in operation (Film Scene 20), he participates in the Seven Potters mission and the Battle of Hogwarts (Film Scenes 21, 24). His death occurs off-screen between Film Scenes 23 and the implied aftermath, consistent with the book.
character_arc.difference: The Potterwatch arc (Fred as 'Rapier,' broadcasting resistance radio) is absent from the film corpus, removing a significant step in his transition from schoolboy prankster to active resistance fighter. The book's careful escalation — from school mischief to organized resistance — is compressed. The emotional weight of his departure from Hogwarts is present but the buildup (the swamp, the fireworks as deliberate acts of war against Umbridge) is somewhat reduced in the films.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's major beats are present and the trajectory is faithful. The missing Potterwatch step and some compression of the resistance escalation warrant a moderate penalty — the arc is compressed but still recognizable.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Fred's most important relationships: (1) George — a genuine partnership of equals, not just comic doubling; they have private conversations, separate concerns, and individual moments of vulnerability. (2) Mrs. Weasley — a loving but genuinely conflicted relationship; she disapproves of their career, they defy her, but the Grimmauld Place reunion after the World Cup attack shows real mutual love. (3) Harry — Fred treats Harry as a peer and genuine friend, gives him the Marauder's Map, accepts his Triwizard winnings, and defends him. (4) Percy — Fred's contempt for Percy's pomposity is a running thread that becomes genuinely painful when Percy estranges himself from the family.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Fred-George dynamic is preserved as a partnership, though the films lean more heavily into their interchangeability. The Fred-Harry relationship is shown through the Map handover and the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes scene. The Fred-Mrs. Weasley dynamic is present (the Burrow arrival scene, Film Scene 4; the Grimmauld Place scenes). The Percy reconciliation in the Battle of Hogwarts is present in the book corpus and implied in the film.
key_relationships.difference: The Fred-George relationship loses some individual texture — in the books, they have private conversations and separate concerns (the Bagman blackmail subplot, their individual reactions to crises). The Fred-Percy dynamic, which is a sustained thread across multiple books, is compressed in the films. The emotional complexity of Fred's relationship with his mother — genuine love coexisting with real defiance — is present but less developed. The Bagman subplot (Fred and George's attempt to recover their World Cup winnings) is entirely absent from the films, removing a relationship that shows Fred's persistence and moral indignation.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics are recognizable but the Fred-George individual texture and the Fred-Percy sustained dynamic are reduced. The Bagman subplot's absence removes a relationship that reveals Fred's character. Moderate penalty.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Fred's complexity lies in the gap between his comic persona and his emotional depth. He is genuinely funny but also genuinely brave, genuinely loving, and capable of real anger and grief. Key nuance moments: his silence and pallor when George is injured (book 7), his direct confrontation with Sirius about the Order's secrecy (book 5), his defense of giving Dudley the toffee on moral grounds, his awareness of the family's financial situation, his private conversations with George about the Bagman blackmail, his role in Potterwatch as a serious resistance broadcaster, and the moment he falls silent watching Percy's estrangement. His death mid-laugh is the ultimate expression of his character — defiant joy to the end.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the most visible nuances: the George ear-wound scene (Film Scene 22) shows genuine emotional depth, the Hogwarts departure is principled and courageous, the Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes shows entrepreneurial seriousness. The 'Nice night for it!' line in the Battle of Hogwarts captures his defiant joy.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The Sirius confrontation is absent — this is the clearest moment in the books where Fred's humor drops entirely and he speaks with raw emotional directness about his father's safety. The Potterwatch role is absent, removing Fred as a serious resistance voice. The Bagman subplot is absent. The private Fred-George conversations that show them as individuals rather than a unit are absent. The sustained Percy estrangement thread, which Fred navigates with a mixture of contempt and genuine hurt, is compressed. The financial motivation behind the joke shop — which gives Fred's entrepreneurialism a dimension of necessity, not just ambition — is underplayed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The films preserve the surface of Fred's complexity but lose several of the moments that reveal the depth beneath the humor. The Sirius confrontation and Potterwatch role are the most significant losses — they show Fred as a moral agent and serious resistance fighter, not just a prankster. The result is a Fred who is recognizable but somewhat flattened toward the comic-relief end of his book characterization.

lost_or_transferred_material: Fred's direct confrontation with Sirius at Grimmauld Place ('Easy for you to say, stuck here! I don't see you risking your neck!') — showing moral agency and emotional directness
Fred's role as 'Rapier' on Potterwatch — his transition to serious resistance broadcaster
The Bagman blackmail subplot — Fred and George's persistent moral indignation and attempt to recover their World Cup winnings
The sustained Fred-Percy estrangement thread across books 5-7, including Fred's contempt and underlying hurt
The financial motivation behind Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes — the family poverty dimension
Mrs. Weasley's explicit disapproval of the twins' career choice and the resulting family conflict
Fred's defense of the Ton-Tongue Toffee on moral grounds ('We gave it to him because he's a great bullying git')
The private Fred-George conversations showing them as individuals with separate concerns (Bagman letters, individual reactions to crises)
Fred's extended silence and emotional weight during the long night waiting for news of Arthur Weasley
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or act of agency is transferred or removed): Potterwatch role and Sirius confrontation are absent — these are meaningful agency and narrative function losses. Score kept at 72, below 75.
comparative.key_observations: Fred Weasley is one of the more faithfully adapted supporting characters in the film series. His core personality — irreverent, bold, entrepreneurially driven, warm beneath the jokes — is preserved with strong fidelity, and his most iconic moments (Marauder's Map handover, Aging Potion attempt, Hogwarts departure, George's ear-wound exchange, Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes) are all present and well-executed. The primary losses are the moments that reveal depth beneath the humor: his direct confrontation with Sirius, his Potterwatch role as a serious resistance broadcaster, and the sustained Percy estrangement thread. The films also lean more heavily into Fred-and-George as an interchangeable unit, losing some of the individual texture the books give Fred. The result is a portrayal that is recognizably and largely faithfully Fred, but somewhat flattened toward his comic-relief function at the expense of his moral agency and emotional complexity.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Muriel is established as a sharp-tongued, socially domineering, and gleefully indiscreet elderly witch. She commands attention and deference ('You there! Give me your chair, I'm a hundred and seven!'), speaks in blunt declarative sentences, interrupts freely, and takes visible pleasure in delivering scandalous information. She is rude to everyone without discrimination ('Bad posture and skinny ankles,' 'your ears are lopsided,' 'You breed like gnomes'), drinks champagne conspicuously and without restraint, and her emotional tone is one of cheerful malice rather than cruelty - she enjoys the discomfort she causes. Her speech is direct, often cackled, and she steamrollers opposition. She is not malicious in a calculating way; she simply has no filter and finds scandal entertaining.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of this personality well. Muriel enters the conversation uninvited, speaks from an adjacent table without being addressed, delivers cutting observations about Rita Skeeter and Dumbledore with relish, and ends with a pointed 'Honestly, my boy, are you sure you knew him at all?' - a line that captures her blunt condescension. She retains the champagne, the unsolicited commentary, and the pleasure in possessing scandalous knowledge others lack.
personality_voice.difference: The film omits several behavioral markers that build texture: the chair-commandeering, the belching, the champagne-dribbling, the cackle, the explicit rudeness to Hermione and George, and the sustained steamrollering of Doge across many exchanges. The film Muriel is sharper and more composed - she delivers her lines with wit rather than the slightly chaotic, physically uninhibited energy of the book version. The book's Muriel is louder, more physically present, and more obviously drunk; the film version is more controlled and aphoristic.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The dominant traits - bluntness, delight in scandal, social dominance, condescension - are preserved. The loss is in degree and texture: the book's Muriel is more physically unruly and her rudeness is broader (targeting multiple characters). The film compresses her into a wittier, more composed version of the same type. This is a minor simplification rather than a change in kind.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Muriel serves two distinct narrative functions. First, at the wedding, she is the vehicle through which Harry (and the reader) first learns the Dumbledore family secrets: Ariana's existence and possible imprisonment, Kendra's death, Aberforth's assault on Albus at the funeral, and Bathilda Bagshot's location in Godric's Hollow. This is active, plot-driving agency - she initiates the conversation, commandeers a seat, and sustains a long exchange against Doge's resistance. Second, her house functions as a recurring safe refuge for the Weasley family and others (Ollivander, Griphook) throughout the later books, making her a structural presence even when offstage.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves Muriel's role as the source of the Bathilda Bagshot / Godric's Hollow revelation, which is the most plot-critical piece of information she delivers. She initiates the conversation from an adjacent table and drives it to the key disclosure. Her house as refuge is not depicted in the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film removes the extended Ariana/Kendra/Aberforth material - the bulk of Muriel's book agency at the wedding. In the books, Muriel delivers a sustained, multi-part account that plants seeds of doubt about Dumbledore that haunt Harry for much of the book. In the film, this is reduced to a single line about Dumbledore's father killing Muggles, with no mention of Ariana, Kendra, or Aberforth's assault. The Godric's Hollow lead is preserved but the broader Dumbledore-destabilizing function is largely cut. Her role as provider of safe refuge is entirely absent from the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The Godric's Hollow disclosure is preserved, maintaining partial agency. But the removal of the Ariana/Kendra/Aberforth material means Muriel no longer performs her most important narrative function: planting the doubt about Dumbledore that drives Harry's internal conflict for much of the book. This is a meaningful reduction in agency, not merely compression.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Muriel has no internal conflict - she is not a character with competing desires or moral struggle. Her motivation is simple and consistent: she enjoys gossip, scandal, and the social power that comes from knowing things others don't. She is not malicious toward Dumbledore specifically; she treats all subjects with the same cheerful irreverence. Her champagne consumption and physical uninhibitedness suggest she is also simply enjoying herself at a wedding.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves this motivation accurately. Muriel in the film is clearly enjoying the possession of scandalous knowledge and the discomfort it causes Doge. The line 'Someone has to pick the bones to get at the truth' adds a slight self-justifying rationalization not present in the books, giving her a faint veneer of principle, but this does not substantially change her motivation.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The 'pick the bones' line is a minor addition that slightly reframes her gossip as truth-seeking rather than pure entertainment. This is a small shift but not a distortion - it is consistent with her character type.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are essentially preserved with minor reframing. No significant penalty warranted.

character_arc.book_baseline: Muriel has no arc. She is a flat character who appears episodically and does not change. Her function is consistent across all appearances: she is rude, she gossips, she is difficult to live with (Fred and George drive her up the wall), and she provides refuge and information. This flatness is intentional - she is a comic and functional supporting character, not a developing one.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film presents Muriel in a single scene with no arc, which is faithful to the book's flat characterization.
character_arc.difference: No arc exists in either medium. The film's single appearance is consistent with the book's episodic use of the character.
character_arc.penalty_logic: A flat arc in the books matched by a flat arc in the film warrants no penalty per the scoring rules.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Muriel's most developed relationship in the corpus is with the Weasley family - she is a source of both irritation and utility. Ron finds her a 'nightmare,' Fred and George deliberately antagonized her (Dungbomb under her chair), she insulted Hermione and George at the wedding, and yet her house is used as a refuge and her tiara lent for the wedding. The relationship is one of mutual tolerance with affection entirely absent. Her dynamic with Doge at the wedding is adversarial and entertaining - she enjoys puncturing his reverence for Dumbledore. Her relationship with Harry is one of condescension and indifference (she repeatedly gets his name wrong).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film shows only the Doge dynamic and the brief exchange with Harry. The Weasley family relationship - the source of most of her book characterization as a recurring presence - is entirely absent from the film corpus. The Doge dynamic is preserved in compressed form.
key_relationships.difference: The Weasley relationship, which gives Muriel her domestic texture and explains why she matters to the family beyond the wedding scene, is absent. The film Muriel exists only in the wedding scene with no connection to the family that contextualizes her.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Doge dynamic is preserved. The Weasley relationship is absent from the film corpus entirely, which removes the context that makes her more than a one-scene gossip. However, since the film corpus itself is limited to the wedding scene, this is partly a corpus limitation rather than a film distortion. Moderate penalty for the loss of the family dynamic.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on book corpus

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Muriel's complexity in the books comes from the combination of her social unpleasantness and her narrative usefulness - she is simultaneously the character everyone wants to avoid and the one who delivers the most plot-critical information at the wedding. She is also physically vivid: the cackle, the champagne-dribbling, the belching, the chair-commandeering, the plumes in her hair. Her rudeness is indiscriminate and her enjoyment of scandal is genuine rather than performed. The extended Ariana/Kendra/Aberforth sequence shows her as someone with real historical knowledge and social memory, not just a gossip.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves her as a sharp, witty, socially uninhibited gossip with useful information. What is lost is the physical comedy, the extended historical knowledge, the indiscriminate rudeness across multiple targets, and the sustained destabilization of Harry's view of Dumbledore.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film Muriel is a compressed, more elegant version of the book character. The physical unruliness, the broader rudeness, and most importantly the Ariana/Kendra/Aberforth material - which is the richest demonstration of her knowledge and her narrative function - are absent. The film reduces her to the Godric's Hollow disclosure and a few sharp lines.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The removal of the Ariana material is the most significant loss. In the books, Muriel's extended account is what makes her more than a comic figure - it shows she has genuine historical knowledge and that her gossip, however delivered, contains real information that matters to the plot and to Harry's understanding of Dumbledore. The film preserves the surface type but removes the depth of her informational role.

lost_or_transferred_material: Muriel's extended account of Ariana Dumbledore's possible imprisonment and status as a Squib
Muriel's account of Kendra Dumbledore's death and possible role in Ariana's fate
Muriel's revelation that Aberforth punched Albus at Ariana's funeral (sourced from Bathilda via her mother)
Muriel's physical comedy: chair-commandeering, belching, champagne-dribbling, cackling
Muriel's indiscriminate rudeness to Hermione ('Bad posture and skinny ankles') and George ('your ears are lopsided')
Muriel's house as recurring safe refuge for the Weasley family, Ollivander, and Griphook
The sustained Muriel-Doge adversarial exchange across many book exchanges (compressed to a few lines in the film)
Ron's characterization of Muriel as a 'nightmare' and the Dungbomb backstory establishing the family's long-suffering relationship with her
Muriel's role in planting the doubt about Dumbledore that haunts Harry throughout the Horcrux hunt
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 considered (core agency transferred/removed): Muriel's most important narrative function - delivering the Ariana/Kendra/Aberforth material that destabilizes Harry's view of Dumbledore - is removed in the film. The Godric's Hollow disclosure is preserved but this represents only a fraction of her book agency at the wedding. Score kept below 75.
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Muriel's surface personality accurately - the bluntness, the delight in scandal, the condescension, the champagne, the adversarial dynamic with Doge - and retains her most plot-visible function (the Godric's Hollow / Bathilda Bagshot disclosure). However, the removal of the extended Ariana/Kendra/Aberforth material strips away the bulk of her narrative agency at the wedding and eliminates the information that most meaningfully affects Harry's arc in the book. The film Muriel is a wittier, more composed, and more compressed version of the book character - recognizable but missing the historical depth, physical unruliness, and plot-driving weight that make her more than a one-note gossip. Her recurring presence as a safe-house provider for the Weasley family is entirely absent from the film, removing the domestic texture that contextualizes her relationship with the family.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Pettigrew in the books is defined by a consistent cluster of traits: cowardice as a core identity (not just a flaw but his defining characteristic), sycophantic flattery directed at whoever holds power, desperate self-preservation above all else, and a wheedling, squeaky, pleading voice. He is physically rat-like in appearance and manner. His speech is characterized by repetition, fragmented sentences, appeals to sentiment ('my old friends,' 'kind boy,' 'sweet girl'), and rapid shifts between groveling and sulky resentment. He shows flashes of sulkiness when cornered (the 'I found you' exchange with Voldemort in GoF, the 'I am not your servant' exchange with Snape in HBP). He is not purely passive - he has moments of cunning (capturing Bertha Jorkins, cutting off his own finger, escaping as a rat). His cowardice is explicitly ideological: he chose the winning side, not out of belief but out of survival calculus.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core personality well. In PoA, Pettigrew's transformation scene captures his squeaky voice, darting eyes, groveling at Ron, Hermione, and Harry, and his pleading appeals to sentiment. The line 'Haven't I been a good friend? A good pet?' is directly preserved. His physical rat-like quality is maintained. In GoF, his trembling, wheezing servility to Voldemort during the resurrection is faithfully rendered. In DH1, his brief appearance at Malfoy Manor preserves his subservient role. The sulky resentment seen in HBP's Spinner's End scene ('I am not your servant!') is entirely absent from the films, as that scene is cut.
personality_voice.difference: The films lose the sulky, resentful edge Pettigrew shows in HBP when he briefly pushes back against Snape's condescension. They also lose the GoF chapter's extended dialogue showing his nervous attempts to suggest alternatives to Voldemort's plan - his voice 'tumbling from him in a rush, as though he was forcing himself to say this before he lost his nerve.' The film GoF compresses his dialogue to near-silence during the Riddle graveyard sequence. The cunning dimension (Bertha Jorkins capture, the self-amputation plan) is referenced but not dramatized in a way that shows his agency.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - cowardice, sycophancy, groveling, pleading, rat-like manner, squeaky voice - is well preserved across PoA and GoF film scenes. The loss of the HBP Spinner's End scene removes a nuanced moment of sulky resentment, and the GoF compression removes the extended nervous dialogue showing his internal conflict about the plan. These are secondary traits rather than core ones. Minor penalty applied.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Pettigrew serves multiple narrative functions: (1) the revelation that upends the assumed history of PoA, (2) the instrument of Voldemort's resurrection in GoF, (3) a living embodiment of the theme that cowardice has consequences, (4) the carrier of the life-debt that Dumbledore predicts will matter, (5) a minor but structurally important presence in HBP (Spinner's End) and DH (Malfoy Manor, where his moment of mercy leads to his death). His agency includes: cutting off his own finger to fake death, surviving 12 years as a rat, capturing Bertha Jorkins, performing the resurrection ritual, and the crucial moment in DH where his silver hand hesitates and kills him.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his role as the PoA revelation and the GoF resurrection instrument. His DH appearance at Malfoy Manor is retained, including the silver hand killing him. The HBP Spinner's End scene is cut entirely, removing his role as Snape's reluctant servant and the dynamic that shows his diminished status among Death Eaters.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Spinner's End scene (HBP) is entirely absent. This removes Pettigrew's function as a presence in Snape's household and the brief moment where he asserts 'I am not your servant' - a small but revealing act of agency. More significantly, the GoF film compresses his role during the graveyard sequence: his extended nervous dialogue with Voldemort about using a substitute wizard is cut, reducing his agency from a character who actively (if fearfully) tries to negotiate his situation to a near-silent functionary. The film also does not dramatize the Bertha Jorkins capture, which in the books is presented as a rare moment of Pettigrew's own initiative.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions are preserved: PoA revelation, GoF resurrection, DH mercy/death. The Spinner's End omission removes a secondary narrative function. The GoF compression reduces his agency during the graveyard from active (nervous negotiation) to passive (silent execution of tasks). These are meaningful reductions but not eliminations of his role.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Pettigrew's motivations are layered and explicit in the books. His primary motivation is survival at any cost - he chose Voldemort because Voldemort was winning and he feared death. His internal conflict is between residual loyalty/guilt toward his old friends and his overriding self-preservation instinct. In GoF, he shows a secondary motivation: a desire for recognition and reward from Voldemort ('I found you... I brought you Bertha Jorkins'). In DH, the silver hand's hesitation suggests a buried impulse of mercy or residual human feeling that his conscious will cannot sustain. The books make explicit through Sirius's accusation that Pettigrew 'never did anything for anyone unless you could see what was in it for you' - his motivation is purely transactional.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The PoA film preserves the survival motivation clearly: 'I didn't mean to! The Dark Lord, you have no idea the weapons he possesses! Ask yourself what you would have done, Sirius.' The transactional self-interest is preserved in the groveling scenes. The DH silver hand moment is retained. However, the GoF film removes the extended dialogue where Pettigrew tries to suggest using a substitute wizard - which in the books reveals his motivation to escape the dangerous task and possibly desert Voldemort. The sulky resentment in HBP ('I found you') that reveals his desire for recognition is entirely absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film loses the GoF scene where Pettigrew's motivation to escape Voldemort's service is made explicit through his nervous suggestion of alternatives. It also loses the HBP scene where his desire for recognition ('I found you, I brought you Bertha Jorkins') is shown. These are not the primary motivation but they add texture to his internal conflict - the tension between wanting to escape and being trapped.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The primary motivation (survival, self-preservation, transactional loyalty) is intact. The secondary motivations (desire for recognition, desire to escape) are partially lost. The internal conflict is simplified but not fundamentally changed. The DH mercy moment is preserved. Moderate simplification warrants a minor penalty.

character_arc.book_baseline: Pettigrew's arc across the series is: (1) loyal Marauder and friend, (2) coward who betrays his friends to Voldemort, (3) 12 years hiding as a rat, (4) revealed and escapes, (5) resurrects Voldemort at great personal cost (hand), (6) diminished servant at Spinner's End, (7) moment of involuntary mercy in DH leads to his death. The arc is one of irreversible moral degradation punctuated by a single involuntary redemptive impulse that kills him. The books establish his starting point (the Marauder photograph, the OotP memory of him as an anxious hanger-on) and trace his decline. Dumbledore's prediction that the life-debt will matter is fulfilled in DH.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films capture the key arc points: the revelation in PoA, the resurrection in GoF, the DH death. The starting point (young Pettigrew as Marauder) is not shown in the films - the OotP Pensieve scene showing young Pettigrew watching James and Sirius with awe is absent. The HBP diminished-servant stage is absent. The arc therefore jumps from PoA revelation directly to GoF resurrection to DH death, skipping the intermediate stage of his degraded existence as Voldemort's servant.
character_arc.difference: The OotP Pensieve scene showing young Pettigrew as an anxious, admiring hanger-on is absent from the films (the OotP film omits this entirely). This removes the visual establishment of his starting point - the contrast between the eager young man and the groveling traitor. The HBP Spinner's End stage is absent. The arc is compressed but the key beats (betrayal established through dialogue, resurrection, mercy/death) are present.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's starting point is not dramatized in the films - we never see young Pettigrew. The intermediate degradation stage (HBP) is absent. The arc is therefore incomplete: it lacks the full trajectory from eager follower to traitor to diminished servant to involuntary redeemer. The key turning points of GoF and DH are present, but the arc feels truncated rather than complete. The Dumbledore life-debt prediction and its fulfillment are both present, which preserves the arc's thematic resolution.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Pettigrew's key relationships: (1) Sirius Black - former friend turned pursuer, defined by Sirius's contempt and Pettigrew's terror; (2) Voldemort - master/servant with Voldemort's cruelty and Pettigrew's abject servility; (3) James Potter - the friend he betrayed, whose memory haunts the PoA confrontation; (4) Lupin - former friend who is more measured than Sirius but equally condemning; (5) Harry - the son of the man he betrayed, toward whom he shows a complex mix of guilt, manipulation, and the life-debt. The Snape relationship in HBP is a secondary but revealing dynamic showing Pettigrew's diminished status.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Sirius/Pettigrew dynamic is preserved in PoA - Sirius's contempt and Pettigrew's terror are both present. The Voldemort/Pettigrew dynamic is preserved in GoF. The Harry/Pettigrew dynamic (life-debt) is preserved in both PoA and DH. The Lupin/Pettigrew dynamic is present but compressed in PoA. The James Potter connection is referenced in dialogue. The Snape/Pettigrew relationship (HBP) is entirely absent.
key_relationships.difference: The Snape/Pettigrew relationship is entirely absent due to the Spinner's End scene being cut. This removes a dynamic that reveals Pettigrew's status among Death Eaters and his resentment at being treated as a servant. The Lupin/Pettigrew dynamic is compressed - in the books, Lupin's measured questioning of Pettigrew ('I must admit, Peter, I have difficulty in understanding why an innocent man would want to spend twelve years as a rat') is more developed than in the film. The James Potter connection is referenced but not dramatized (no Pensieve scene).
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The primary relationships (Sirius, Voldemort, Harry) are preserved in their essential dynamics. The Snape relationship is entirely absent. The Lupin relationship is compressed. The James connection is referenced but not shown. These are meaningful losses but the most important dynamics are intact.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The books give Pettigrew considerable complexity: (1) the sulky resentment that surfaces briefly even in his degraded state ('I found you'); (2) the extended GoF dialogue showing his nervous attempts to negotiate with Voldemort, revealing both his fear and his desire to escape; (3) the HBP scene showing him as Snape's reluctant domestic servant, briefly asserting dignity before being crushed; (4) the OotP Pensieve showing him as a young man - anxious, admiring, chewing his nails, hoping to copy from neighbors - establishing the texture of his inadequacy before the betrayal; (5) the DH silver hand moment, which is the most complex beat: an involuntary mercy that his conscious will cannot sustain, killing him; (6) Dumbledore's observation that 'there was a bit of regret there, somewhere' - the books insist on his humanity even while condemning him.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the groveling complexity of the PoA confrontation, the physical horror of the GoF resurrection (including his revulsion at carrying Voldemort's body), and the DH silver hand moment. The film PoA captures his manipulation attempts across multiple characters (Ron, Hermione, Harry). However, the OotP Pensieve scene is absent, the HBP Spinner's End scene is absent, and the GoF extended dialogue is compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The OotP Pensieve scene is entirely absent - this is the only moment in the films where we would see young Pettigrew and understand the texture of his inadequacy and hero-worship. Without it, the audience has no visual baseline for who he was before the betrayal. The HBP scene is absent, removing the brief flash of resentful dignity. The GoF compression removes the extended nervous negotiation. Dumbledore's post-PoA observation about 'a bit of regret' is present in the book but absent from the film (the Dumbledore/Harry conversation after PoA is cut). The life-debt conversation with Dumbledore, which frames Pettigrew's complexity thematically, is absent from the PoA film.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The absence of the OotP Pensieve scene, the HBP Spinner's End scene, and the Dumbledore life-debt conversation removes significant nuance. The audience never sees young Pettigrew, never sees his brief assertion of dignity, and never hears Dumbledore's framing of his complexity ('there was a bit of regret there, somewhere'). The DH silver hand moment partially compensates, but the overall texture of his complexity is noticeably reduced. Cap 3 applies: a core emotional beat (Dumbledore's framing of the life-debt and its thematic meaning) is absent from the films.

lost_or_transferred_material: OotP Pensieve scene: young Pettigrew shown as anxious, nail-chewing, admiring hanger-on watching James and Sirius - establishes his starting point and the texture of his inadequacy

HBP Spinner's End scene: Pettigrew as Snape's reluctant domestic servant, briefly asserting 'I am not your servant!' before being crushed - shows his resentful dignity and diminished status among Death Eaters
GoF extended Riddle House dialogue: Pettigrew nervously suggesting using a substitute wizard instead of Harry, revealing his desire to escape Voldemort's service and his fear of the plan

GoF Voldemort's speech to Death Eaters: Voldemort's account of Pettigrew finding him in Albania and capturing Bertha Jorkins - the one moment of Pettigrew's own initiative is narrated but not dramatized
Post-PoA Dumbledore/Harry conversation: Dumbledore's framing of the life-debt ('there was a bit of regret there, somewhere') and its thematic significance - absent from the PoA film
GoF sulky exchange: Pettigrew's 'I found you... I brought you Bertha Jorkins' - his desire for recognition from Voldemort is absent
Lily Potter's letter reference to 'Wormy' visiting - the human texture of Pettigrew before the betrayal
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or emotional beat is transferred or absent): The Dumbledore life-debt framing and the OotP Pensieve scene are absent, removing material necessary to understand Pettigrew's thematic function and complexity. Score held below 75.
comparative.key_observations: The films preserve Pettigrew's core identity - cowardice, groveling sycophancy, rat-like manner, squeaky voice, and the key plot functions of PoA revelation, GoF resurrection, and DH mercy/death - with reasonable fidelity. The PoA confrontation scene is the most faithful section, capturing his pleading manipulation across multiple characters and his physical terror. The DH silver hand moment is preserved. However, the films lose three significant layers of complexity: the OotP Pensieve scene that establishes his starting point as an anxious, inadequate hanger-on; the HBP Spinner's End scene that shows his brief resentful dignity and diminished status; and the GoF extended dialogue that reveals his desire to escape Voldemort's service. The Dumbledore post-PoA conversation framing the life-debt thematically is also absent. The result is a Pettigrew who is recognizable and whose core traits are intact, but who is stripped of the nuance that makes him more than a one-note coward - particularly the moments that insist on his residual humanity and the texture of his inadequacy before the betrayal.

personality_voice.book_baseline: The Sorting Hat is self-assured, witty, and loquacious. Its first-year song in PS is boastful and playful ('I'll eat myself if you can find a smarter hat than me'), establishing a voice that is confident, slightly theatrical, and proud of its unique intelligence. In private sorting moments it is perceptive and direct, naming Harry's traits plainly ('Plenty of courage... Not a bad mind... a nice thirst to prove yourself'). By OotP its voice shifts to urgent and prophetic — the song becomes a long historical narrative warning of division and danger, showing the Hat can modulate tone from comic to grave. In CoS it is brief but pointed when Harry tries it on again: 'You were particularly difficult to place... you would have done well in Slytherin.' It does not hedge or soften.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film PS preserves the boastful, theatrical song voice and the private sorting dialogue almost verbatim, including 'Hmm, difficult, very difficult' and the Slytherin temptation exchange. The CoS film adds a brief original line — 'Bee in your bonnet, Potter?' — which is consistent with the Hat's dry wit, and then repeats the Slytherin assessment faithfully. The DH Part 2 film gives the Hat a single whispered line to Neville: 'Don't despair, Longbottom. I put you in Gryffindor for good reason.' This is invented but tonally consistent — reassuring, purposeful, brief.
personality_voice.difference: The OotP warning song — the Hat's most tonally complex and politically urgent speech across all seven books — is entirely absent from the films. This song establishes the Hat as a character capable of independent moral reasoning, historical awareness, and genuine anxiety about the future of Hogwarts. Its absence removes the Hat's most distinctive non-sorting voice. The films also omit the GoF song, which introduced the Hat's historical narrative mode. What remains is the sorting function and two brief exchanges.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality traits — wit, confidence, perceptiveness, directness — are preserved in the material that does appear. The film does not distort or contradict the Hat's voice; it simply omits the fuller register. The OotP song's absence is a meaningful loss of a secondary but established trait (prophetic moral urgency), warranting a moderate penalty. The Hat does not become passive or wrong in the films; it is just narrower.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: The Sorting Hat has three distinct narrative functions: (1) annual sorting of students into houses, (2) a source of independent wisdom and warning delivered through songs that comment on the state of the wizarding world, and (3) a plot-active agent in CoS, where it delivers the sword of Gryffindor to Harry inside the Chamber. In DH it is seized by Voldemort and used as a prop of domination — forced onto Neville's head and set alight — which triggers Neville's act of defiance and the sword's reappearance. The Hat's agency in CoS is direct and consequential: Harry calls for help, the Hat responds by producing the sword.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The sorting function is preserved in PS. The CoS film preserves the Hat's role in Dumbledore's office (the brief exchange) and implicitly the sword delivery, though the Chamber sequence compresses the Hat's active role. In DH Part 2, the film retains the Hat being forced onto Neville's head and set on fire, and the sword appears — but the Hat's whispered encouragement to Neville ('Don't despair, Longbottom') is a film addition that actually gives the Hat slightly more direct agency in that moment than the book, where it is silent.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The OotP and GoF warning songs are the Hat's primary independent narrative function beyond sorting. These songs drive plot-relevant exposition (the founders' history, the call for unity, the warning of external danger) and position the Hat as an active commentator on the story's central conflict. Their removal eliminates this narrative role entirely. The Hat becomes purely a sorting instrument with two brief reactive exchanges, rather than a character that initiates thematic commentary.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer of the Hat's prophetic/warning function to other characters (Dumbledore, Hermione, and the general narrative) is a meaningful agency loss. The Hat's role as an independent voice warning of division and danger — a role it holds uniquely in the books — is gone. The sorting and sword-delivery functions are preserved. This warrants a moderate penalty.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: The Hat's motivations are unusual for a character: it is bound by duty (it must sort) but in OotP it explicitly expresses conflict about that duty — 'Though condemned I am to split you / Still I worry that it's wrong.' This is a genuine internal contradiction: the Hat must do the very thing it fears is harmful. It also shows a secondary motivation of honest assessment over flattery — it tells Harry he would do well in Slytherin even when Harry resists, and it tells Dumbledore the same in the post-Chamber conversation.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the Hat's honest assessment motivation — it tells Harry he would do well in Slytherin in both PS and CoS, and it stands by that judgment ('I stand by what I said last year'). The internal conflict about sorting — the Hat's most philosophically interesting motivation — is entirely absent because the OotP song is cut.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The conflict between duty and doubt is lost. What remains is the simpler motivation of accurate assessment. The Hat is still honest and perceptive, but the dimension of self-questioning is gone.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations that do appear are correct and faithfully rendered. The lost internal conflict is real but was expressed almost entirely through the OotP song, which is a single concentrated moment rather than a pattern across the corpus. The simplification is meaningful but not a distortion of what remains. Score reflects simplified but not wrong motivations.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on book corpus

character_arc.book_baseline: The Hat has a flat individual arc — it does not change as a character — but it has a thematic arc across the series: it begins as a confident, playful sorter (PS), develops into a historically aware and morally anxious commentator (GoF, OotP), and ends as a passive object of Voldemort's domination that nonetheless enables resistance (DH). The arc is not about personal growth but about the Hat's relationship to the school's deteriorating situation.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the beginning (confident sorter, PS) and the end (passive object enabling Neville's defiance, DH). The middle — the Hat's growing anxiety and prophetic voice — is absent. The DH film adds the whispered encouragement to Neville, which partially compensates by giving the Hat a moment of purposeful agency at the end.
character_arc.difference: The middle phase of the thematic arc is missing. The Hat goes from cheerful sorter directly to Voldemort's prop, without the intervening development of its warning voice. The arc is compressed rather than falsified.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The beginning and end points are preserved and the Hat's function at the climax is intact. The missing middle is a compression that loses thematic texture but does not invert or falsify the arc. The DH addition partially compensates. Score reflects compressed but recognizable arc.

key_relationships.book_baseline: The Hat's most important relationship is with Harry: it perceives his Slytherin-compatible qualities, respects his choice, and later becomes a source of his self-doubt and Dumbledore's teaching moment about choice. It also has a functional relationship with Neville — it placed him in Gryffindor, a decision Neville doubts throughout the series, which pays off in DH. Its relationship with Voldemort/the school is adversarial at the end: Voldemort uses it as a symbol of domination.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry relationship is preserved in its key beats: the sorting exchange, the CoS reassessment, and the implicit sword delivery. The Neville relationship gains a film-original moment of direct reassurance ('I put you in Gryffindor for good reason') that actually strengthens this dynamic compared to the books, where the Hat is silent on Neville's head in DH. The Voldemort adversarial relationship is preserved.
key_relationships.difference: The Harry-Hat relationship loses the OotP Occlumency flashback context (where Harry relives the sorting under Snape's attack) and the broader thematic weight of the Hat's warning songs, which indirectly address Harry's situation. These are minor losses. The Neville relationship is arguably enhanced in the film.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationship dynamics are mostly preserved. The Harry relationship loses some thematic depth but the core dynamic (Hat sees Slytherin in Harry, Harry chooses Gryffindor, Hat respects that) is intact. The Neville relationship is strengthened. Minor penalty for loss of thematic depth in the Harry dynamic.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The Hat's complexity comes from the tension between its function (dividing students) and its wisdom (recognizing that division may be dangerous). The OotP song is the fullest expression of this: the Hat knows the history of the founders' friendship and their falling out, understands that the houses have become a source of conflict rather than strength, and explicitly warns the school while being unable to stop sorting. This makes it a character with genuine moral complexity — bound to perpetuate what it fears. The GoF song establishes the historical narrative mode. The CoS private exchange with Harry shows perceptiveness and honesty under pressure.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the perceptiveness and honesty (CoS exchange, PS sorting) and the DH climax function. The moral complexity — the Hat's self-aware conflict about its own role — is entirely absent. The Hat in the films is a clever, honest sorting instrument. In the books it is also a troubled witness to history.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most nuanced material — the OotP song's self-questioning, the GoF song's historical sweep — is lost. What remains is the Hat's surface function and two brief exchanges that show intelligence and honesty but not the deeper contradiction.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is the most significant loss. The Hat's book complexity is concentrated in the warning songs, and both are cut. The remaining material is faithful but thin. The character is recognizable but substantially flattened from its book version. The DH film addition (Neville encouragement) adds a small compensating nuance. Score reflects noticeable simplification with important nuances missing.

lost_or_transferred_material: OotP Sorting Hat song: historical narrative of the four founders, warning of external danger, call for house unity — entirely absent from films
GoF Sorting Hat song: first departure from standard sorting song, establishing the Hat's capacity for independent thematic commentary — entirely absent
Hat's explicit internal conflict: 'Though condemned I am to split you / Still I worry that it's wrong' — the Hat's self-questioning about its own function is never expressed in the films
Hermione's book reference to the Hat seriously considering placing her in Ravenclaw — absent from films

Harry's recurring internal use of the Hat's Slytherin assessment as a source of self-doubt across multiple books (CoS, OotP Occlumency scene) — partially present in CoS film but the pattern is compressed
The Hat's role as a recurring thematic anchor for the choice-vs-destiny theme across books 2-7 — reduced to two brief exchanges in the films
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function is transferred or removed): The Hat's warning-song function — its most distinctive non-sorting role — is entirely absent. This cap is applied, keeping the total at or below 75.
comparative.key_observations: The Sorting Hat's film portrayal is faithful in its sorting function, its perceptive voice, and its key plot moments (CoS sword delivery, DH Neville climax). The core personality — confident, honest, theatrically self-aware — is preserved in the material that appears. However, the films omit both warning songs (GoF and OotP), which are the Hat's primary vehicle for independent agency, moral complexity, and thematic commentary. This removes the Hat's most distinctive book characteristic: its self-aware conflict between its sorting duty and its fear that sorting itself is dangerous. What remains is a recognizable but substantially simplified version — a clever prop with two good scenes rather than a character with genuine moral texture. The DH film addition of the Hat whispering encouragement to Neville is a small compensating invention that is tonally faithful. Score capped at 72 due to the complete removal of the Hat's warning-song narrative function.

personality_voice.book_baseline: Cedric is defined by a consistent pattern of quiet dignity, fairness, and genuine modesty. He is not effusive or verbose - his speech is brief, measured, and direct. He shows embarrassment at his father's boasting (muttering 'Harry fell off his broom, Dad... it was an accident'), polite warmth toward rivals ('Good one, Harry'), and a stubborn moral resolve in the cup scene where he refuses to take it despite clear advantage. He is not passive - he initiates the hint about the bath, he seeks Harry out after the Yule Ball to repay a debt, and he argues back against Harry's insistence in the maze. His voice is calm under pressure, slightly formal, and never self-aggrandizing. He also shows a briefly suspicious edge when Harry tips him off about the dragons ('Why are you telling me?'), which is a realistic, grounded reaction rather than naive gratitude.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of Cedric's personality reasonably well. He is polite, composed, and fair. His brief dialogue in the dragon tip-off scene ('Are you serious?' / 'And Fleur and Krum, do they...?') matches the book's measured tone. His bath hint is delivered with the same slightly awkward, debt-repaying quality ('Look, I realize I never really thanked you properly'). In the maze, his 'Go on, take it. You saved me, take it!' and 'Some game, huh?' preserve his understated manner. His final line - 'Take my body back to my father' - is faithful. However, the film loses the suspicious edge from the dragon tip-off scene (the book's 'Why are you telling me?' with its 'slightly suspicious look' is replaced by a more straightforwardly grateful Cedric), and the extended cup-refusal argument - where Cedric stubbornly refuses multiple times, argues point by point, and is described as 'looking as though this was costing him every ounce of resolution he had' - is compressed into a single 'Go on, take it.' The film also adds a moment where Cedric nearly finishes off the Imperius-cursed Krum before Harry stops him, which has no book basis and briefly makes him look less measured.
personality_voice.difference: The suspicious, grounded reaction to Harry's tip-off is softened. The extended, stubborn moral argument at the cup is heavily compressed, losing the texture of Cedric's resolve. The near-attack on Krum is invented. The embarrassment at his father's boasting is absent (Amos's boasting is cut entirely from the film's World Cup scene).
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - modest, fair, composed, quietly principled - is preserved. The compression of the cup scene removes some texture but the essential trait (refusing to take unearned glory) survives. The suspicious reaction to the dragon tip is a minor but real loss of nuance. The invented Krum moment is a small addition without book basis. These are meaningful but not transformative changes - the character remains recognizable and largely faithful. Score in the 18-22 range is appropriate.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Cedric's narrative function is threefold: (1) a foil to Harry - the 'proper' champion who is handsome, popular, and legitimate, against whom Harry's outsider status is measured; (2) an active participant in the tournament who makes independent decisions, uses his own skills (Transfiguration for the dragon, Bubble-Head Charm for the lake), and solves problems himself; (3) a moral anchor in the maze, whose refusal of the cup forces Harry to propose the joint victory. He also functions as a plot driver in the egg subplot - his hint sends Harry to the prefects' bathroom, which is the mechanism by which Harry solves the second task. His death is the narrative hinge of the entire book: the first innocent casualty of Voldemort's return, used by Dumbledore to name what has happened and call the wizarding world to account.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves Cedric's function as foil (the 'proper' champion, chosen by Hufflepuff, admired by the school) and his death as the moral hinge. His agency in the maze is partially preserved - he saves Harry from Krum's Cruciatus ('Expelliarmus!'), and the joint cup decision is retained. However, his independent task solutions are largely invisible: the film shows him entering the maze and being announced as winner of the second task, but his Transfiguration of a rock into a dog (first task) and his Bubble-Head Charm (second task) are not shown in any detail. His role as the mechanism for Harry solving the egg is compressed - the bath hint scene is present but brief, and Myrtle's confirmation that 'it took Cedric ages to riddle it out' is retained, preserving his function as the one who found the solution first.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Cedric's independent problem-solving in the first and second tasks is largely off-screen or summarized. His foil function is present but reduced - the film does not develop the school's admiration for him as extensively (no Quidditch backstory, no 'Support Cedric Diggory' badge subplot developed from his perspective). His agency in the maze is partially transferred: in the book, Harry and Cedric jointly stun the spider; in the film, the spider scene is replaced by a vine/undergrowth trap from which Harry rescues Cedric, making Cedric more passive in that moment. The cup-refusal scene, where Cedric actively argues and refuses multiple times, is compressed to a single line, reducing his moral agency in that pivotal moment.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions are present. His death retains its weight. The egg hint mechanism is preserved. The joint cup decision survives. However, his independent agency as a problem-solver is largely invisible, and the vine rescue makes him more passive than in the books. The cup-refusal compression reduces his most significant act of moral agency. These are real reductions but not eliminations. Score in the 14-17 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Cedric's motivations are clear and consistent: he wants to win the tournament legitimately and honorably. This is not mere ambition - it is tied to a deep commitment to fairness. When Harry tips him off, his first reaction is suspicion ('Why are you telling me?'), not gratitude, because he is trying to assess whether accepting the information is itself fair. His refusal of the cup is explicitly motivated by a running tally of debts: 'You told me about the dragons... You helped me with the egg... You should've got more points on the second task.' He is not simply being noble for its own sake - he is trying to balance a moral ledger. His internal conflict in the cup scene is real: 'He looked as though this was costing him every ounce of resolution he had.' He wants the cup - the narration makes clear he is 'walking away from the sort of glory Hufflepuff House hadn't had in centuries' - but his sense of fairness overrides it.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the broad motivation (honorable competition, fairness) but loses the specific moral-ledger logic. The bath hint is framed as simple debt repayment ('I realize I never really thanked you properly'), which is correct but simplified. The cup scene's 'Go on, take it. You saved me, take it!' preserves the fairness motivation but removes the detailed accounting of debts and the explicit acknowledgment of what he is giving up. The suspicious reaction to the dragon tip - which reveals that his fairness is not naive but calculated - is absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The moral-ledger logic (the specific accounting of debts that drives his cup decision) is simplified. The internal conflict - the visible cost of his decision - is compressed. The suspicious edge that reveals his fairness as principled rather than reflexive is lost.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivation (fairness, honorable competition) is intact and recognizable. The simplification is real but does not change the motivation into something different - it just makes it less textured. Score in the 11-13 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Cedric's arc is relatively flat in terms of internal transformation - he is consistently fair and principled from his first appearance to his death. However, there is a meaningful progression in his relationship with Harry: from rival (Quidditch match, initial suspicion at the dragon tip), to cautious ally (bath hint, Yule Ball exchange), to genuine partner (joint cup decision, mutual rescue in the maze). His arc culminates in the graveyard: he dies as he lived, having just agreed to share a victory rather than claim it alone. His final act - asking Harry to return his body to his parents - is a last expression of his relational loyalty. The arc is not one of change but of revelation: the tournament progressively reveals the depth of his character.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows the same arc: rival to ally to partner to death. The key beats are present - the dragon tip, the bath hint, the Yule Ball exchange, the maze cooperation, the joint cup decision, the death, the final request. The arc is compressed but structurally faithful.
character_arc.difference: The Quidditch backstory that establishes the initial rivalry is absent from the film (the PoA match is not in the film corpus and is not referenced). This means the 'rival' starting point of the arc is weaker - Cedric enters the film as a pleasant stranger rather than someone Harry has a specific competitive history with. The arc's progression from rivalry to partnership therefore has less distance to travel and less weight.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's key turning points are present and the trajectory is faithful. The loss of the Quidditch rivalry backstory weakens the starting point but does not alter the arc's direction or conclusion. Score in the 11-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships define Cedric in the books. (1) Cedric and Harry: a complex dynamic that moves from rivalry to mutual respect, built through specific exchanges (dragon tip, bath hint, Yule Ball corridor, maze cooperation, cup refusal). The relationship is characterized by a careful reciprocity - each act of help is acknowledged and repaid. (2) Cedric and Amos: Amos's loud, embarrassing pride in his son, and Cedric's quiet embarrassment at it ('Ignore him,' he mutters to Harry), reveals Cedric's modesty and his slightly strained relationship with his father's need to boast. (3) Cedric and Cho: primarily seen through Harry's jealous perspective, but establishes Cedric as someone Cho genuinely loved, which gives his death emotional weight beyond the tournament.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry-Cedric relationship is preserved in its key beats but loses the careful reciprocity texture (the detailed debt-accounting is compressed). The Amos-Cedric relationship is significantly altered: in the film, Amos is warm and proud but the specific embarrassing boasting scene (about Harry falling off his broom) is absent, replaced by a more generic proud-father dynamic. The film's Amos-Cedric farewell before the maze ('Good luck, my boy' / 'See you later, Dad') is warm but brief. The Cho-Cedric relationship is present (Harry notices them at the Yule Ball) but given even less development than in the books.
key_relationships.difference: The Amos-Cedric dynamic loses its specific texture: the book's scene where Cedric is visibly embarrassed by his father's crowing about Harry's fall, and where he mutters corrections, is absent. This removes a key moment that reveals Cedric's modesty and his slightly complicated relationship with his father's pride. The Harry-Cedric reciprocity is compressed. The Cho-Cedric relationship is present but thin.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Harry-Cedric relationship is mostly preserved in function if not in full texture. The Amos-Cedric relationship loses its most revealing scene. The Cho-Cedric relationship is present but underdeveloped. These are real losses that affect how the character is understood relationally. Score in the 7-8 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Cedric's complexity comes from several sources: (1) his suspicious reaction to Harry's tip ('Why are you telling me?') - he is not naively good, he is principled in a calculated way; (2) the extended cup-refusal scene, where his stubbornness, his moral accounting, and the visible cost of his decision are all on display; (3) his embarrassment at his father's boasting, which complicates the 'perfect champion' image; (4) his role in the Quidditch match (Book 3) where he wins fairly but tries to call for a rematch when Harry falls - an early demonstration of his fairness that the film has no equivalent for; (5) Myrtle's observation that he spent a long time working out the egg, establishing him as diligent rather than effortlessly brilliant; (6) the detail that he may have told Hufflepuffs to stop harassing Harry after the dragon tip, showing quiet social influence.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves: the bath hint as debt repayment, the joint cup decision, the final request, Myrtle's comment about him taking ages to solve the egg. It loses: the suspicious reaction to the dragon tip, the extended cup-refusal argument with its moral accounting, the Amos embarrassment scene, the Quidditch backstory, the detail about telling Hufflepuffs to back off. The film also adds the near-attack on Krum, which briefly makes Cedric look less measured than in the books.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The suspicious reaction and the extended cup argument are the two most significant losses - they are the scenes that most complicate the 'simply noble' reading of Cedric and reveal him as principled in a specific, calculated way rather than reflexively good.
